questions.text: Which one of the following organisms is scientifically correctly named, correctly printed according to the International Rules of Nomenclature, and correctly described?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Musca domestica - a common house reptile
options.explanation: Musca domestica is the scientific name of the common housefly so it should be written in italics.

options.text: Plasmodium falciparum - a protozoan pathogen causing the most serious type of malaria
options.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum is a protozoan parasite, one of the species of Plasmodium that causes malaria in humans. Being digenetic, its life cycle is complete in two host—man and mosquito. Its sexual cycle is completed in the female Anopheles mosquito and infective individuals called sporozoites are formed. Which are transmitted to humans with the bite of infected female Anopheles. The asexual cycle is passed in man in two phases. Malaria caused by P. falciparum (also known as aestivoautumnal, malignant tertian, or pernicious malaria) is the most dangerous form of malaria, with the highest rate of complication and mortality. In this case, the fever cycle is of 48 hours and is often fatal to the patient as it affects the brain.

options.text: felis tigris - the Indian tiger, well protected in Gir forests
options.explanation: The scientific name of the Indian tiger is Felis tigris, so 1st letter should be capitalized.

options.explanation: The full name of E.coli is Escherichia coli.

questions.text: Species are considered as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A species is a natural population or group of natural populations of individuals that are genetically distinct and reproductively isolated with similar essential morphological traits. Species is also a genetically closed system because its members do not interbreed with members of other species. Species are the lowest or basic taxonomic category, e.g., mango (Mangifera indica), potato (Solanum tuberosum), and lion (Panthera leo). Hereindica, tuberosum, and Leo are species of genera Mangifera, Solanum, and Pantera respectively. All other taxonomic categories are defined and described as species. For example, a genus is a group of species, and a subspecies or a variety is a part of a species. New species originate from already existing species. Species is considered a basic unit of taxonomy since in the greater majority of cases we do not have intraspecific names.
Specie is the lowest unit of classification.

options.text: Artificial concept of human mind which cannot be defined in absolute terms
options.explanation: This statement is totally incorrect. As species is not the artificial concept.

options.text: Real units of classification devised by taxonomists

In other words, it is the study of the diversity of organisms, their comparative and evolutionary relationships based on comparative anatomy, ecology, physiology, biochemistry, and other fields. Biosystematics plays a crucial role in various scientific fields, including ecology, conservation biology, and evolutionary biology. It helps scientists understand the biodiversity of the planet, make informed conservation decisions, and contribute to our knowledge of the tree of life.

questions.text: Which of the following is less general in characters as compared to genus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A taxonomic hierarchy is the sequence of arrangement of taxonomic categories in descending order during the classification of an organism. There are seven obligate categories kingdom, division, class, order, family, genus, and species. Species is the lowest category while kingdom is the highest category. The number of common characters is maximum in the case of organisms placed in the lowest category. The number of common characters decreases with the rise in category. Species are the smallest group of individuals that can be recognized by ordinary methods as groups and which are consistently and persistently different from other groups because their characters are less general.

questions.explanation: The term phylum was coined in 1866 by Ernst Haeckel from the Greek phylon (φῦλον, "race, stock"), related to phyle (φυλή, "tribe, clan"). The concept of "phylum" was actually developed by the German biologist Ernst Haeckel (1834-1919) in the 19th century. Haeckel is credited with proposing the term "phylum" as a rank in the hierarchy of biological classification to group organisms with similar body plans and organizational features.

The gamma fraction usually makes up the largest portion of the globulins, therefore antibody deficiency is mainly related to the low level of serum globulins.

questions.explanation: Serum globulins are proteins that include gamma globulins(antibodies) and a variety of enzymes and carrier/transport proteins. The specific profile of the globulins is determined by protein electrophoresis(SPEP), which separates the proteins according to size and charge. There are four major groups that can be identified: α-1 globulins, α-2 globulins, beta globulins, and gamma globulins. Once the abnormal group has been identified, further studies can determine the specific protein excess or deficit. Since the gamma fraction usually makes up the largest portion of the globulins, therefore antibody deficiency is mainly related to the low level of serum globulins.

options.explanation: This option is correct becauase Entamoeba histolytica(Gr., entos: within+amoeba: change+histones: tissue + lysis: dissolve) is the causative organism of amoebic dysentery or amoebiasis in man. It is a microscopic endoparasite of man. It is commonly found in the upper part of the large intestine(colon) and is very often lodged in the liver, lungs, brain, and testes. In its life cycle, it occurs in three distinct forms (i) trophozoite or magna form, (ii) precystic or minuta form, and (iii) cystic form. Trophozoite is the most active, motile, and feeding form which is pathogenic to man. It lives in the mucous and submucous layers of the colon and feeds on these layers and erythrocytes.

In its life cycle, it occurs in three distinct forms (i) trophozoite or magna form, (ii) precystic or minuta form, and (iii) cystic form. Trophozoite is the most active, motile, and feeding form which is pathogenic to man. It lives in the mucous and submucous layers of the colon and feeds on these layers and erythrocytes.

questions.text: HIV that causes AIDS first starts destroying:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the AIDS retrovirus, called human immunodeficiency virus(HIV), mounts a direct attack on CD4 + T-helper cells because it recognizes the CD4 coreceptors associated with these cells. HIV’s attack on CD4 + T cells cripples the immune system in at least three ways. First, HIV-infected cells die only after releasing replicated viruses that infect other CD4 + T cells, until the entire population of CD4 + T cells is destroyed. Second, HIV causes infected CD4 + T cells, to secrete a soluble suppressing factor that blocks other T cells from responding to the HIV antigen. Finally, HIV may block transcription of MHC genes, hindering the recognition and destruction of infected CD4 + T cells, and thus protecting those cells from any remaining vestiges of the immune system. The combined effect of these responses to HIV infection is to wipe out the human immune defense.

This option is incorrect because leucocytes are white blood cells and have no relation with HIV.

questions.explanation: Wuchereria bancrofti is a dreaded human parasite. It is a digenetic parasite completing its life cycle in two hosts the final host is man harboring the adult worm. The disease passes through four stages in human beings: In the first stage, the patient has increased eosinophils, and enlarged lymph nodes. The second or carrier stage is symptomless. The third stage is characterized by filarial fever, inflammation of lymph nodes (lymphadenitis) and lymph vessels (lymphangiectasis), and reversible lymphoedema(excess fluid in tissues due to 387 obstruction of lymph vessels) in various body parts. The fourth or final stage is manifested by lymphoedema accompanied by thickening of subcutaneous tissues and skin so that there is permanent swelling mostly of feet, legs, thighs, scrotal sacs, breast, etc. It is called elephantiasis.

questions.explanation: Plasmodium, a tiny protozoan parasite causes malaria in humans and is transmitted through the bite of an infected female Anopheles mosquito. When female Anopheles sucks the blood of an infected human it takes up gametocytes (sexual stages of parasite) with blood meal. The gametocytes come out of the RBCs into the lumen(cavity) of the stomach of the mosquito. In the stomach, the male gametocyte divides and forms 6 to 8 long, motile, whiplike microgametes(male gametes). The female gametocyte does not divide but undergoes a process of maturation to become the macrogamete(female gamete). A microgamete penetrates a macrogamete and fertilization(syngamy) takes place, resulting in the formation of a zygote. The zygote elongates and becomes a worm-like motile organism called an ookinete. Ookinete further changes into sporozoites(mature infective stage of Plasmodium).

questions.explanation: Contact inhibition is a property of normal cells by virtue of which contact withother cells inhibits their uncontrolled growth. Cancerous cells lack this property. questions.text: Lysozyme that is present in perspiration, saliva and tears, destroys :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysozyme is an antibacterial enzyme with natural antibiotic properties. Normally excreted in the tears, nasal mucus, milk, and saliva in most animals, lysozyme is part of the body’s first natural defense against bacteria and viruses. Lysozyme is an enzyme that degrades the polysaccharide protective coating on the surface of many bacteria and viruses (glycoprotein covering) to allow other enzymes and antibodies to find their appropriate attachment site. Most of the bacteria affected by lysozyme are not pathogenic. Lysozyme serves as a nonspecific innate opsonize by binding to the bacterial surface, reducing the negative charge, and facilitating phagocytosis of the bacterium before opsonins from the acquired immune system arrive at the scene. In other words, lysozyme makes it easier for phagocytic white blood cells to engulf bacteria.

Lysozymes are specific to the structure of bacterial cell walls, primarily targeting peptidoglycan, a component unique to bacteria. Viruses and fungi have different structures and are not susceptible to this mode of action.
Viruses are not considered as complete cells , as they lack cell wall. Since they lack cell wall , lysozymes have no target structure to act upon.
Fungi have complex cell wall. While lysozymes may affect some fungi, fungal cell walls are more complex than bacterial cell wall and may not be suspectable for lysozyme action.

questions.text: The bacterium (Clostridium botulinum) that causes botulism is :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An obligate aerobe is an organism, typically a microorganism such as a bacterium, that requires oxygen for its growth and survival. These organisms depend on oxygen to carry out their metabolic processes, specifically aerobic respiration, which involves the use of oxygen to produce energy (ATP) from organic compounds like glucose.Obligate aerobes cannot grow in the absence of oxygen because their metabolic pathways are specifically adapted to function in an oxygen-rich environment. When placed in an environment with low or no oxygen, obligate aerobes may become dormant, die, or suffer from a lack of energy production, depending on the severity of the oxygen deprivation. In contrast to obligate aerobes, there are obligate anaerobes that cannot survive in the presence of oxygen and facultative anaerobes, which can adapt to both aerobic and anaerobic conditions for growth and energy production. These classifications are important in microbiology and have practical implications in various fields, including clinical microbiology, biotechnology, and environmental science.

A facultative anaerobe is an organism, typically a microorganism like bacteria or yeast, that can thrive in environments with or without oxygen. These organisms have the metabolic flexibility to switch between aerobic respiration (using oxygen) and anaerobic respiration (in the absence of oxygen) depending on the availability of oxygen in their surroundings.In the presence of oxygen, facultative anaerobes will utilize aerobic respiration to produce energy (ATP) as efficiently as possible. However, when oxygen is limited or absent, they can switch to anaerobic respiration or fermentation processes to continue generating energy, even though these alternative methods are typically less efficient than aerobic respiration.This adaptability to changing environmental conditions allows facultative anaerobes to survive and grow in a wide range of habitats. They are often found in environments with fluctuating oxygen levels, such as the human digestive tract, where oxygen availability varies in different sections. Some common examples of facultative anaerobes include Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Saccharomyces cerevisiae, which is used in baking and brewing processes.

Clostridium is a genus of gram-positive bacteria. They are obligate anaerobes capable of producing endospores. Individual cells are rod-shaped. Foodborne disease caused by C. botulinum is referred to as botulism (a muscle-paralyzing disease). It is caused by the ingestion of a neurotoxin (botulin) produced by the microorganism in the food. Botulin blocks nerve function leading to respiratory and musculoskeletal paralysis. Symptoms of botulism include weakness, fatigue, and dizziness, followed by blurred vision and progressive difficulty in speaking and swallowing. Weakening of the respiratory muscles is also observed and death may occur due to respiratory failure.

questions.text: A person showing unpredictable moods, outbursts of emotion, quarrelsome behaviour and conflicts with others is suffering from :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Addictive disorders, also known as substance use disorders or addiction, are a group of conditions characterized by a person's compulsive and problematic use of substances or engagement in activities, despite experiencing negative consequences. These disorders can have a significant impact on a person's physical and mental health, as well as their social and economic well-being. These are characterized by the excessive and harmful use of drugs or alcohol. Common substances that can lead to substance use disorders include alcohol, tobacco, opioids, stimulants, cannabis, and more.This is a behavioral addiction characterized by a persistent and problematic pattern of gambling, even when it leads to negative consequences.Common signs and symptoms of addictive disorders may include cravings, loss of control over substance use or behavior, withdrawal symptoms when not engaging in the addictive behavior, and the neglect of other important life activities and responsibilities. Treatment for addictive disorders often includes therapy, counseling, support groups, and, in some cases, medication. It's essential for individuals struggling with these disorders to seek help, as addiction can have severe physical, psychological, and social consequences.

Schizophrenia is a complex and chronic mental disorder that affects how a person thinks, feels, and behaves. It is characterized by a range of symptoms that can impact an individual's perception of reality, cognition, emotions, and social functioning. Schizophrenia is typically diagnosed in late adolescence or early adulthood and can be a lifelong condition.People with schizophrenia may experience hallucinations, which involve perceiving things that are not present, such as hearing voices or seeing things that others cannot. Delusions are false beliefs that are firmly held even when there is evidence to the contrary. These beliefs can be paranoid (believing others are plotting against them), grandiose (having an inflated sense of self-importance), or bizarre.Schizophrenia often leads to difficulty in maintaining relationships, holding a job, and managing daily life.The exact cause of schizophrenia is not fully understood, but it is believed to result from a combination of genetic, environmental, and neurobiological factors. Treatment for schizophrenia typically involves a combination of antipsychotic medications, psychotherapy, and support services to help individuals manage their symptoms and improve their quality of life. Early diagnosis and intervention are important for better outcomes, and individuals with schizophrenia often require ongoing care and support from mental health professionals and their families.

Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD) is a complex and serious mental health condition characterized by pervasive and enduring patterns of instability in relationships, self-image, emotions, and behavior. Individuals with BPD often struggle with intense mood swings, impulsivity, a strong fear of abandonment, and difficulties in forming and maintaining relationships. People with BPD often have tumultuous and unstable interpersonal relationships. They may experience rapid shifts between idealizing and devaluing others, have a fear of abandonment, and engage in intense, but unstable, attachments.Individuals with BPD frequently experience intense and rapidly changing emotions. They may have difficulty managing anger, sadness, and anxiety. Mood swings can be severe and lead to self-harm or suicidal thoughts.The exact cause of BPD is not fully understood, but it is thought to result from a combination of genetic, environmental, and neurobiological factors. BPD is typically diagnosed in early adulthood, but symptoms can appear earlier. It is often associated with a history of traumatic experiences or adverse childhood events.Treatment for BPD usually involves a combination of psychotherapy, medication (to manage specific symptoms like depression or anxiety), and support from a mental health professional. Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) is a specific form of psychotherapy that has been shown to be effective in treating BPD. With appropriate treatment and support, individuals with BPD can improve their quality of life and develop more stable and fulfilling relationships. Early intervention is key to better outcomes for those with BPD.

Mood disorders are a group of mental health conditions characterized by disturbances in a person's mood, leading to significant emotional dysregulation. These disorders can affect a person's daily functioning, relationships, and overall quality of life. Also known as clinical depression, MDD is characterized by persistent and severe feelings of sadness, hopelessness, and a loss of interest or pleasure in activities. Other symptoms can include changes in appetite or weight, sleep disturbances, fatigue, and feelings of worthlessness or guilt. It is a debilitating condition that can significantly impair one's ability to function.This disorder involves recurrent episodes of both depression and mania (or hypomania, which is a less severe form of mania). During manic episodes, individuals may experience heightened energy, increased activity, racing thoughts, and impulsivity. Bipolar disorder can cause significant mood swings and impact one's ability to maintain stable relationships and employment.The exact causes of mood disorders are not fully understood but involve a complex interplay of genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors. Treatment for mood disorders often includes a combination of psychotherapy (such as cognitive-behavioral therapy or interpersonal therapy), medication (such as antidepressants or mood stabilizers), lifestyle modifications, and support from mental health professionals. Early diagnosis and appropriate treatment are essential for managing mood disorders and improving an individual's quality of life.

questions.explanation: Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a serious mental illness characterized by pervasive instability in moods, interpersonal relationships, self-image, and behavior. This instability often disrupts family and work life, long-term planning, and the individual’s sense of self-identity. Originally thought to be at the “borderline” of psychosis, people with BPD suffer from a disorder of emotion regulation. Schizophrenia is a group of severe mental disorders characterized by disturbances of language and communication; thought disturbances that may involve distortion of reality, misperceptions, delusions, and hallucinations; mood changes, and withdrawn, regressive, or bizarre behavior, lasting longer than six months. A mood disorder is a condition where the prevailing emotional mood is distorted or inappropriate to the circumstances. Addiction is a state of dependence produced by the habitual taking of drugs, alcohol, etc.

questions.text: Antibodies in our body are complex :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glycoproteins are a type of biomolecule that consists of a protein linked to one or more carbohydrate chains. They play important roles in various biological processes and are found in all living organisms, including humans. Glycoproteins are crucial for cell-to-cell communication, immune system functioning, and various other biological functions.Glycoproteins have a protein (polypeptide) component to which one or more carbohydrate chains (glycans) are covalently attached. The carbohydrates can be either simple sugars (monosaccharides) or complex sugar structures. Many glycoproteins on the cell surface are involved in cell recognition, adhesion, and signaling. They help cells interact with other cells, pathogens, and molecules in the surrounding environment.The ABO and Rh blood group systems are determined by the presence or absence of specific glycoproteins (antigens) on the surface of red blood cells. These glycoproteins are used for blood typing and are important in blood transfusions.Glycoproteins are incredibly diverse and have diverse functions, making them an important area of study in biochemistry and biology. They are involved in many physiological and pathological processes, and research on glycoproteins is essential for understanding and treating various diseases.

Lipoproteins are complex molecules composed of proteins and lipids (fats). They play a crucial role in the transportation of lipids in the bloodstream, particularly cholesterol and triglycerides. Lipoproteins are essential for maintaining the body's lipid balance, as they transport these hydrophobic molecules, which are not water-soluble, through the watery environment of the blood. Chylomicrons are the largest and least dense lipoproteins. They are produced in the intestines and primarily transport dietary triglycerides (fats) from the small intestine to the body's tissues, including adipose (fat) tissue. Chylomicrons release their triglycerides to be used for energy or stored in fat cells.VLDLs are produced in the liver and are responsible for carrying endogenous triglycerides. They transport triglycerides to adipose tissue, where they are stored, and to muscles, where they can be used for energy. VLDLs are less dense than low-density lipoproteins (LDL). IDLs are formed when VLDLs lose some of their triglycerides. They can either be further processed into LDLs or taken up by the liver.The balance of these lipoproteins in the bloodstream is essential for maintaining overall health. An imbalance can lead to conditions like high cholesterol, atherosclerosis, and an increased risk of heart disease. Lipoprotein levels can be influenced by dietary choices, genetics, and lifestyle factors. Medical professionals often measure the levels of LDL and HDL cholesterol in a person's blood to assess their cardiovascular health and determine their risk of heart disease.

Steroids, in the context of biology and medicine, refer to a class of organic compounds that share a common structure characterized by four carbon rings. Steroids have diverse functions in the body, and they can be broadly categorized into two main groups: corticosteroids and anabolic steroids.These are a group of steroid hormones produced by the adrenal glands, which are situated on top of each kidney. Corticosteroids have important roles in regulating various physiological processes, including metabolism, immune response, and the body's response to stress. Anabolic steroids are synthetic compounds that mimic the effects of the male sex hormone testosterone. They are sometimes used illegally by athletes and bodybuilders to enhance muscle growth, strength, and athletic performance. The use of anabolic steroids for non-medical purposes is associated with numerous health risks and is often banned in sports.It's essential to distinguish between the legitimate medical use of corticosteroids and the illicit use of anabolic steroids. Corticosteroids, when prescribed by a healthcare professional, can be effective and safe for managing various medical conditions. However, the misuse of anabolic steroids for non-medical purposes is illegal and poses significant health risks. Always consult with a healthcare provider before using any medication, including corticosteroids, and never use anabolic steroids without a legitimate medical need and prescription.

Prostaglandins are a group of lipid compounds that play a vital role in various physiological processes in the human body. They are part of a larger group of compounds known as eicosanoids. Prostaglandins are produced and act locally in different tissues and have a wide range of effects on the body. Prostaglandins are short-lived, locally acting lipids that are derived from fatty acids, particularly arachidonic acid. They have a 20-carbon chain and are part of the eicosanoid family, which also includes leukotrienes and thromboxanes.Prostaglandins are synthesized in most tissues and are produced on demand. Enzymes known as cyclooxygenases (COX) convert arachidonic acid into prostaglandin H2 (PGH2), which is then further modified to produce various types of prostaglandins.It's important to note that the role of prostaglandins in the body is highly complex and context-dependent. While they are involved in various physiological processes, an imbalance or excessive production of prostaglandins can lead to health issues. Researchers and healthcare providers continue to study and better understand the functions of prostaglandins in health and disease.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are members of a class of proteins known as immunoglobulins. Immunoglobulins are glycoproteins in the immunoglobulin superfamily. The terms antibody and immunoglobulin are often used interchangeably. They are found in the blood and tissue fluids, as well as many secretions. In structure, they are globulins (in the region of protein electrophoresis). They are synthesized and secreted by plasma cells that are derived from the B cells of the immune system.

questions.text: Which one of the following depresses brain activity and produces feelings of calmness, relaxation, and drowsiness?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Morphine is a potent opioid analgesic (pain-relieving) medication that is derived from the opium poppy plant, Papaver somniferum. It is one of the most effective pain management drugs available and has been used for centuries to relieve severe and chronic pain. Morphine is primarily used to relieve severe and chronic pain, such as that resulting from surgery, injury, cancer, or other medical conditions. It is especially useful in managing pain that does not respond well to other medications.Morphine acts on the central nervous system by binding to specific receptors called opioid receptors. It alters the perception of pain and how pain signals are processed in the brain, leading to pain relief.Like other opioids, morphine has a potential for abuse and can lead to physical and psychological dependence if used improperly or for an extended period. Tolerance can develop, requiring higher doses to achieve the same pain relief. Morphine is typically prescribed and administered by a healthcare professional in a clinical setting, such as a hospital, for severe pain management. However, it can also be prescribed for chronic pain in outpatient settings.The legal status of morphine varies by country and jurisdiction. It is generally classified as a controlled substance due to its potential for abuse. In many places, it is available by prescription only.An overdose of morphine can be life-threatening and may lead to respiratory failure. Signs of overdose include extreme drowsiness, slow or shallow breathing, and pinpoint pupils. Morphine is an essential medication in palliative care, where its primary goal is to provide comfort and pain relief to patients with terminal illnesses, such as cancer.Morphine is a powerful medication that is highly effective for managing severe pain when used as prescribed and under the guidance of a healthcare professional. However, it should be used with caution and awareness of its potential for addiction and side effects. Patients receiving morphine should be closely monitored for adverse effects, and its use should be discussed thoroughly with a healthcare provider.

Valium is a brand name for the generic drug diazepam, which belongs to the class of medications known as benzodiazepines. Diazepam is a central nervous system depressant that is primarily prescribed to treat various conditions, including anxiety disorders, muscle spasms, alcohol withdrawal symptoms, and certain seizure disorders. Valium is often prescribed to relieve symptoms of anxiety, including generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) and panic disorder. It can help reduce nervousness, tension, and restlessness.Diazepam is used to relieve muscle spasms and is sometimes prescribed to individuals with conditions such as muscle strains, spasticity associated with neurological disorders, and muscle tightness.Valium can be used to manage the symptoms of alcohol withdrawal, as it helps to relieve anxiety and prevent severe withdrawal symptoms like seizures and delirium tremens.Common side effects of Valium may include drowsiness, dizziness, fatigue, and impaired coordination. More serious side effects, including respiratory depression, are possible, especially when taken in high doses or combined with other central nervous system depressants.When someone has been taking Valium regularly and discontinues its use, they may experience withdrawal symptoms, such as anxiety, insomnia, restlessness, and potentially more severe symptoms. Tapering under medical supervision is usually recommended when discontinuing the medication.It's important to use Valium only under the guidance of a healthcare provider who can prescribe it in the appropriate dosage and monitor its use to minimize the risks associated with its use.

Amphetamines are a class of powerful central nervous system stimulants. They are psychoactive drugs that affect the brain and body by increasing the release and inhibiting the reuptake of certain neurotransmitters, particularly dopamine and norepinephrine. This action leads to increased alertness, attention, and a sense of energy. Amphetamines can have therapeutic uses, but they are also commonly abused as recreational drugs.Amphetamines and related compounds have medical uses in treating conditions such as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), narcolepsy (a sleep disorder), and, less commonly, obesity. They are typically prescribed in controlled and carefully monitored doses to improve focus, attention, and wakefulness.The use of amphetamines can lead to various physical and psychological effects, including increased heart rate, elevated blood pressure, reduced appetite, enhanced wakefulness, improved focus, and a feeling of being "high." However, they can also cause restlessness, anxiety, and paranoia, particularly at high doses.Chronic use and high doses of amphetamines can lead to a range of health problems, including cardiovascular issues, sleep disturbances, appetite and weight loss, mood disorders, and cognitive deficits. Long-term use can also lead to addiction and withdrawal symptoms when attempting to stop. Individuals struggling with amphetamine addiction may require medical and psychological treatment to overcome the addiction and manage withdrawal symptoms. Behavioral therapy, counseling, and support groups can be part of the treatment plan.It's important to use amphetamines only under the supervision of a qualified healthcare professional, following prescribed dosages and guidelines. Recreational use and abuse of amphetamines can lead to serious physical and psychological harm, as well as legal consequences. If you or someone you know is struggling with amphetamine abuse or addiction, seeking help from a healthcare provider or addiction specialist is strongly advised.

Hashish, often referred to as hash, is a concentrated form of cannabis, which is derived from the Cannabis sativa or Cannabis indica plant. It is made by extracting the resin glands, or trichomes, from the flowers and leaves of the cannabis plant. The resin contains the psychoactive compounds, primarily delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), which is responsible for the psychoactive effects associated with marijuana use. Hashish typically appears as a soft, brownish, or black substance, which can be crumbly, sticky, or pliable. The texture and appearance can vary depending on the method of production.The effects of hashish are similar to those of marijuana but are often more pronounced due to its higher THC content. Users may experience relaxation, euphoria, altered perception of time, increased appetite, and, in some cases, anxiety or paranoia. The specific effects can vary depending on the individual, the strain, and the amount consumed.It's important to note that the use of hashish, like marijuana, carries potential health risks, including impaired judgment, cognitive effects, and the risk of addiction. Furthermore, its legal status varies, and individuals should be aware of and abide by local laws and regulations related to its use and possession. If you have questions or concerns about hashish, its effects, or its legality in your area, it's advisable to consult with a healthcare professional or legal expert.

questions.explanation: Valium is a benzodiazepine (sedative) that gives a feeling of relaxation, calmness, or drowsiness in the body. Morphine is the main opium alkaloid that depresses the respiratory center and contributes to the fall in blood pressure. Amphetamines are synthetic drugs and are stimulant in nature. Hashish is a hallucinogen.

questions.text: Damage to thymus in a child may lead to :
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A reduction in haemoglobin content of blood
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as damage to thymus in a child may lead to loss of cell mediated immunity.

options.text: A reduction in stem cell production
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as damage to thymus in a child may lead to loss of cell mediated immunity.
This option is incorrect as damage to thymus in a child may lead to loss of cell mediated immunity.
This option is correct.

questions.explanation: The thymus is the major gland of our immune system. It is responsible for numerous immune system functions, including the production of T-lymphocytes, a type of white blood cell responsible for cell-mediated immunity. Cell-mediated immunity is a type of defense in which specialized cells carry out protective activities, safeguarding the body against pathogens, including protists and fungi that have entered the host's cells. T-cells and B-cells are the types of lymphocytes that develop from bone marrow cells. Those lymphocytes that migrate to the thymus and differentiate are called T-cells, while those cells that continue to differentiate in the bone marrow are known as B-cells. T-cells are responsible for cell-mediated immunity, whereas B-cells produce antibodies and participate in antibody-mediated immunity.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not correctly matched?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glossina palpalis, also known as the tsetse fly, is a vector for the transmission of sleeping sickness (African trypanosomiasis). This parasitic disease is caused by the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma brucei and is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected tsetse flies.
Culex mosquitoes are indeed vectors for the transmission of filariasis. Filariasis is caused by parasitic worms called filariae, and Culex mosquitoes can carry and transmit these worms to humans.

Aedes aegypti mosquitoes are indeed responsible for transmitting dengue fever. Dengue is caused by the dengue virus and is transmitted to humans when they are bitten by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes that are infected with the virus.
Anopheles mosquitoes are primarily known for transmitting malaria, not leishmaniasis. Leishmaniasis is usually transmitted by sandflies (Phlebotomus and Lutzomyia species), not Anopheles mosquitoes.

questions.explanation: All the options given are diseases with their associated vectors that transmit the respective diseases. Leishmaniasis, also called kala azar, is caused by Leishmania donovani. It is spread by the sand fly (Phlebotomus) and is characterized by an enlarged spleen and liver, along with high fever. Sleeping sickness is caused by the protozoan Trypanosoma gambiense. Filariasis is caused by the worm Wuchereria bancrofti. Dengue fever is caused by an arbovirus.

questions.text: Carcinoma refers to :
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Malignant tumours of the connective tissue
options.explanation: Malignant tumors of the connective tissue are known as sarcomas, not carcinomas. Sarcomas originate from connective tissue, such as bones, muscles, or cartilage.

options.text: Malignant tumours of the skin or mucous membrane
options.explanation: Carcinoma specifically refers to malignant tumors that originate from epithelial tissues, which can include the skin and the mucous membranes lining various organs. However, it is not limited to just the skin or mucous membranes. Carcinomas can occur in various organs and tissues throughout the body, such as the lungs, breast, colon, prostate, and more. So, while it is correct that carcinomas can involve the skin and mucous membranes, they are not limited to these locations.

Malignant tumors of the colon are generally referred to as colorectal cancer, and they fall under the category of adenocarcinomas. While colorectal cancer is a type of carcinoma, the term "carcinoma" alone does not specifically indicate colon cancer.

options.text: Benign tumours of the connective tissue.
options.explanation: Benign tumors of the connective tissue are not referred to as carcinomas. They are typically called benign tumors or growths, and they do not have malignant characteristics.

questions.explanation: Carcinoma is a type of cancer that arises in the epithelium, which is the tissue that lines the skin and internal organs of the body. It may occur in any tissue containing epithelial cells. This category includes cervical cancer, breast cancer, skin cancer, stomach cancer, and others.

questions.text: Christmas disease is another name for :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: "Christmas disease" is another name for Haemophilia B. Therefore, the correct option is Haemophilia-B.
Hepatitis-B is a viral infection that affects the liver and is not related to Christmas disease.
Down's syndrome is a chromosomal disorder caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21, and it has no connection to Christmas disease.
Sleeping sickness is a parasitic infection transmitted by tsetse flies and is entirely unrelated to Christmas disease.

questions.explanation: Haemophilia B, a type of haemophilia, is also known as Christmas disease. It is due to a deficiency of a blood coagulation factor known as the Christmas factor (Factor IX). Christmas was the individual (20th century) in whom the factor was first identified. Haemophilia B is a blood disorder that prevents it from clotting.

questions.text: Salmonella is related with :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Salmonella is related to Typhoid. Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi is the bacterium responsible for causing Typhoid fever, a bacterial infection characterized by high fever, abdominal pain, and gastrointestinal symptoms.
Polio is caused by the poliovirus and has no direct relation to Salmonella.
Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, which is different from Salmonella.
Tetanus is caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani and is not related to Salmonella.

questions.explanation: Typhoid is caused by Salmonella typhi. The organisms responsible for the disease are present in the stool and may also be found in urine. Consequently, they can be carried by water and contaminated food, potentially leading to severe epidemics. Polio is caused by Enterovirus. TB is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Tetanus is caused by Clostridium tetani.

questions.text: Which is the most infectious disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatitis-B is highly infectious because it can be transmitted through various bodily fluids, including blood, semen, vaginal fluids, and saliva.

AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) caused by HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) is one of the most infectious diseases. It is transmitted through various means, including unprotected sexual contact, sharing needles, and from mother to child during childbirth or breastfeeding
The common cold and cough are infectious, but they are generally less severe and easier to contain compared to AIDS. They spread through respiratory droplets and direct contact.

Malaria is caused by the Plasmodium parasite and is transmitted through the bite of infected mosquitoes. While it is a significant infectious disease, AIDS is typically considered more infectious due to its modes of transmission and impact on the immune system.

questions.explanation: Hepatitis-B (serum hepatitis) can occur at any age, and its mode of transmission is through contact with infected blood. Infection is severe, often fatal, and is accompanied by symptoms such as loss of appetite, nausea, pale or whitish stool (due to a lack of bile), and jaundice. Even a very small amount, as little as 0.0002% of hepatitis B infected blood contact, is sufficient to transmit hepatitis B.

questions.text: Interferons are synthesized in response to :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While the immune system responds to Mycoplasma infections, interferons are not specifically synthesized in response to Mycoplasma.
Interferons primarily respond to viral infections and are not produced as a direct response to bacterial infections

Interferons are synthesized in response to viral infections.Interferons are signaling proteins produced by host cells in response to the presence of viruses. They play a crucial role in the body's antiviral defense mechanisms.
Interferons are not typically synthesized in response to fungal infections but rather in response to viruses.

questions.explanation: Interferons are proteins that increase a cell's resistance to viral attacks by unmasking genes that synthesize antiviral proteins. In humans, three groups of interferons have been discovered: a) interferons from white blood cells; b) interferons from connective tissue fibroblasts; and c) interferons from lymphocytes.

questions.text: Reason of lung cancer is :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: One of the causes of lung cancer is exposure to coal dust. Exposure to coal dust can cause some coal mine workers to develop pneumoconiosis, or "black lung." This occurs when inhaled coal dust becomes embedded in the lungs, causing them to harden and making breathing difficult.
Calcium fluoride is a compound and not a known direct cause of lung cancer.
Working in a cement factory can expose individuals to dust and pollutants, which may be harmful to respiratory health, but it is not a direct cause of lung cancer
While exposure to certain substances during bauxite mining can pose health risks, lung cancer is primarily linked to factors such as smoking, exposure to asbestos, radon, and air pollution

questions.explanation: Lung cancer is a disease where lung tissue grows out of control. This may lead to metastasis, invasion of adjacent tissue, and infiltration beyond the lungs. The vast majority of primary lung cancers are carcinomas of the lung, resulting from epithelial cells. One of the causes of lung cancer is exposure to coal dust. Exposure to coal dust can cause some coal mine workers to develop pneumoconiosis, or "black lung." This occurs when inhaled coal dust becomes embedded in the lungs, causing them to harden and making breathing difficult.

questions.text: Which is showing accurate pairing?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Syphilis is accurately paired with its causative agent, Treponema pallidum. Treponema pallidum is the bacterium responsible for causing syphilis, a sexually transmitted infection.
This pairing is incorrect. AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is caused by HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus), not Bacillus conjugalis.
This pairing is incorrect. Gonorrhoea is caused by the bacterium Neisseria gonorrhoeae, not Leishmania donovani.
This pairing is incorrect. Typhoid fever is caused by Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi, not Mycobacterium leprae. Mycobacterium leprae is associated with leprosy, not typhoid fever.

questions.explanation: Syphilis is caused by a spirochete (spiral bacterium) called Treponema pallidum. The symptoms of syphilis occur in three stages. The first stage usually consists of a painless lesion called a chancre at the organism's site of entry. The second stage begins when the organism enters the bloodstream. Symptoms such as fever, flu-like illness, a skin rash, hair loss, and swollen joints may come and go over a period of several years. In the third stage, permanent brain damage, heart disease, and blindness often occur. AIDS is a viral disease caused by the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV). Gonorrhea is a sexually transmitted disease, and its causative organism is Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Typhoid is caused by the bacillus bacterium Salmonella typhi.

questions.text: Which disease of man is similar with cattle’s, bovine spongiform encephalopathy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Encephalitis is a general term for inflammation of the brain and can have various causes, including infections. It is not directly related to Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy.

Jakob Creutzfeldt Disease (often referred to as Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease) is a human disease similar to Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy (BSE), also known as "mad cow disease." Both diseases are characterized by spongiform changes in brain tissue and are caused by prions, which are misfolded proteins.
"Spongiocitis of cerebrum" is not a recognized medical term or condition, and it is not related to Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy.
Spondylitis refers to inflammation of the vertebrae and is unrelated to diseases like Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy or Jakob Creutzfeldt Disease.

questions.explanation: The common term for bovine spongiform encephalopathy is mad cow disease, which is a progressive neurological disorder in cattle. In humans, it is called Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, named after the two doctors who first described its symptoms. It is caused by prions (proteinaceous infectious particles) and is characterized by rapidly progressive dementia associated with myoclonic jerks. The brains of affected individuals show characteristic cystic degeneration.

questions.text: Saline solution is given to patients of cholera because :
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Na+ prevents water loss from body
options.explanation: Saline solution is given to patients with cholera primarily because it contains sodium ions (Na+), which help prevent water loss from the body.

While sodium (Na+) is important for regulating various physiological processes in the body, the primary reason for administering saline solution in cholera is to address the severe dehydration caused by the loss of fluids through diarrhea.
Sodium chloride (NaCl) does not directly produce energy in the body. Energy production involves metabolic processes that use nutrients, not salt.

Sodium chloride (NaCl) itself is not an antibacterial agent. It is used in saline solution to help restore electrolyte balance and hydration in patients but does not have direct antibacterial properties. Cholera is primarily treated with antibiotics to target the bacterial infection.

questions.explanation: Cholera is an acute infection of the small intestine caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae, resulting in severe vomiting and diarrhea (known as rice-water stools) that leads to dehydration. The disease is contracted through the consumption of contaminated food or drinking water containing feces from an infected individual. The resulting dehydration and imbalance in the body's fluid concentration can lead to death within 24 hours. Since a significant amount of fluids and salts are rapidly lost through stools and vomiting, the most critical treatment involves rapidly replacing the lost fluids and salts. Rapid fluid and electrolyte replacement is achieved through oral rehydration therapy, with electrolytes comprising Na+ ions that help prevent water loss from the body.

questions.text: The antibodies are :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antibodies are proteins, specifically immunoglobulins, that are produced by the immune system to recognize and neutralize foreign substances, such as pathogens.
Carbohydrates are another type of biomolecule but are not antibodies. They play different roles in the body, such as providing energy and structural support.
Lipids are another category of biomolecules that include fats, oils, and phospholipids. They are not antibodies and do not have the same function in the immune system.

"Germs" refers to microorganisms like bacteria, viruses, and fungi, which antibodies target. However, antibodies themselves are not germs; they are molecules produced by the immune system to combat germs and other foreign invaders.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are members of a class of proteins known as immunoglobulins. Immunoglobulins are glycoproteins in the immunoglobulin superfamily. The terms 'antibody' and 'immunoglobulin' are often used interchangeably. They are found in blood and tissue fluids, as well as in many secretions. In terms of structure, they are globulins (in the region of protein electrophoresis). They are synthesized and secreted by plasma cells derived from the B cells of the immune system.

questions.text: The term ‘active immunity’ means :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Increasing the rate of heart beat is unrelated to the term "active immunity." Heart rate is a physiological response that can occur for various reasons but is not the definition of active immunity
Increasing the quantity of blood is not the definition of active immunity. Active immunity is focused on the body's immune response to pathogens, not changes in blood quantity.

Active immunity refers to the resistance or immunity that an individual develops after being exposed to a disease-causing agent (pathogen) and subsequently recovering from the disease. The immune system "actively" produces antibodies and immune memory in response to the infection.
Active immunity is the development of resistance after exposure to a disease, not before. It involves the immune system's response to a specific pathogen encountered during an infection.

questions.explanation: Active immunity is the immune response generated in an individual due to contact with an infectious pathogen or vaccination. In many cases, it can last a lifetime.

questions.text: Typhoid fever is caused by :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Shigella is a different bacterium that causes Shigellosis, a gastrointestinal infection with symptoms different from those of typhoid fever.
Escherichia coli (E. coli) is a different bacterium and can cause various gastrointestinal illnesses but is not the primary cause of typhoid fever.
Giardia is a parasite that causes Giardiasis, which is a gastrointestinal infection distinct from typhoid fever.
Typhoid fever is caused by Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi.

questions.explanation: Typhoid is caused by Salmonella typhi. The organisms of the disease are present in the stool, and they may also be found in urine. Therefore, they can be carried by water and contaminated food, which can lead to severe epidemics. Polio is caused by Enterovirus. TB is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Tetanus is caused by Clostridium tetani.

questions.text: Botulism caused by Clostridium botulinum affects the :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Botulism does not primarily affect the lymph glands. While the immune system may respond to the infection, the primary impact is on the central nervous system.

Botulism, caused by Clostridium botulinum, primarily affects the central nervous system. Clostridium botulinum produces a toxin known as botulinum toxin, which can lead to paralysis by blocking nerve signals at neuromuscular junctions in the central nervous system.
Botulism does not primarily affect the spleen. The primary site of action for the botulinum toxin is the central nervous system.

While Clostridium botulinum can be found in contaminated food, the primary effects of botulism occur after the ingestion of botulinum toxin, which is then absorbed into the bloodstream and affects the central nervous system. It is not primarily an intestinal infection.

questions.explanation: Clostridium is a genus of gram-positive bacteria. They are obligate anaerobes capable of producing endospores. Individual cells are rod-shaped. Foodborne disease caused by C. botulinum is referred to as botulism (a muscle-paralyzing disease). It is caused by the ingestion of a neurotoxin (botulinum) produced by the microorganism in the food. Botulinum blocks nerve function, leading to respiratory and musculoskeletal paralysis. Symptoms of botulism include weakness, fatigue, and dizziness, followed by blurred vision and progressive difficulty in speaking and swallowing. Weakening of the respiratory muscles is also observed, and death may occur due to respiratory failure.

questions.text: Which of the following is an opiate narcotic?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amphetamines are not opiates.They are a different class of drugs that stimulate the central nervous system and are used for various medical purposes, including treating attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD).
LSD (Lysergic Acid Diethylamide) is not an opiate. It is a hallucinogenic drug that affects perception, thoughts, and feelings but is not classified as an opiate narcotic.
Barbiturates are a class of drugs that act as central nervous system depressants. They are not opiates but are used as sedatives and hypnotics.
Morphine is an opiate narcotic. It is a potent pain reliever and is derived from the opium poppy plant.

questions.explanation: Opiates are derived from opium along with their synthetic relatives. Opiates have narcotic, analgesic, sedative, and astringent effects. A narcotic is a drug that induces stupor and relieves pain. Morphine is the main opium alkaloid, which is a strong analgesic and also has a sedative and calming effect. It depresses the respiratory center and contributes to the fall in blood pressure. It can cause the release of ADH, reduction in urine output, constipation, and mild hyperglycemia, among other effects. It also leads to addiction.Barbiturates are substituted derivatives of barbituric acid. They reduce anxiety and induce sleep. Amphetamines are synthetic drugs that act as strong stimulants. LSD (Lysergic acid diethylamide) is one of the most powerful hallucinogens, causing severe damage to the central nervous system.

questions.text: Which of the following will be curable in next two decades?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tuberculosis (TB) remains a global health concern, and while there have been advancements in TB treatment and control, complete eradication or a universally effective cure within the next two decades is uncertain.

Cancer may become curable in the next two decades. The completion of the human genome is causing profound changes in the thinking and direction of biomedical research. Cancer is caused by the malfunctioning of genes, either through the activation of cancer-causing oncogenes or through the inactivation of tumor suppressor genes.

Efforts to eradicate polio (Poliomyelitis) have been ongoing for many years, and significant progress has been made. However, complete global eradication remains a complex challenge, and it's uncertain whether it will be fully curable within the next two decades.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Cancer may be curable in the next two decades. The completion of the human genome is causing profound changes in the thinking and direction of biomedical research. Cancer is caused by the malfunctioning of genes, either through the activation of cancer-causing oncogenes or through the inactivation of tumor suppressor genes. By comparing the active genes in the tumor to those of normal cells, the genes causing the cancer can be determined. Side by side, there is significant progress in the fields of genetic engineering and biotechnology. All these aspects give us hope that cancer may be curable in the next two decades. Tuberculosis is curable by taking antitubercular drugs, and polio may be on the verge of eradication if the pulse polio program succeeds.

questions.text: Diphtheria is caused by :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nematodes are roundworms and are not responsible for causing diphtheria. They are not associated with this disease.
Diphtheria is caused by the bacterium Corynebacterium diphtheriae. This bacterium produces a toxin that can lead to severe throat and respiratory tract infections.
Diphtheria is not caused by a virus. It is a bacterial infection, as mentioned above.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Diphtheria is caused by Corynebacterium diphtheriae, a type of bacteria that usually affects children up to the age of five. It may start with symptoms such as a sore throat, chills, mild fever, sometimes vomiting, and headache. The throat and/or tonsils may show a gray membrane, which can spread downward and cause hoarseness and difficulty in breathing. The nose may also be affected, giving rise to a blood-tinged nasal discharge from one nostril. If the disease is not treated early and properly, the toxin produced by the bacteria can affect the heart and the nervous system, leading to a fatal outcome. The germs are present in the discharges from the nose and throat of patients, as well as in healthy people who act as carriers. Both patients and carriers can spread the disease through activities like kissing, talking, coughing, and sneezing. The incubation period is typically 2 to 5 days. If there is no isolation hospital in the town, the patient should be kept in a well-ventilated room. The most important preventive measure against this disease is to ensure that all babies are immunized within the first six weeks of birth using the DPT vaccine.

questions.text: Which of the following diseases is now considered completely eradicated from India?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Smallpox, also known as variola, is an infectious disease caused by the variola virus. It has been eradicated worldwide, thanks to a successful vaccination campaign led by the World Health Organization (WHO). Smallpox was a highly contagious and deadly disease that had devastating effects on populations for centuries.Smallpox symptoms typically included high fever, a rash with pustules (fluid-filled sores), and flu-like symptoms. There were two forms of smallpox: variola major (more severe) and variola minor (less severe).Smallpox was primarily spread from person to person through respiratory droplets, direct contact with infected skin lesions, or contaminated objects.The last naturally occurring case of smallpox was reported in 1977, and a global vaccination campaign effectively eradicated the disease by 1980. This is considered one of the most significant achievements in the history of public health.The smallpox vaccine had some potential side effects, including soreness at the injection site and fever. Serious side effects were rare but possible.Since smallpox has been eradicated, the general population is no longer at risk of contracting the disease. However, knowledge of smallpox remains important for historical and public health reasons, especially in the context of vaccine development and preparedness for other infectious diseases.

Poliomyelitis, commonly known as polio, is an infectious disease caused by the poliovirus. It primarily affects the nervous system and can lead to paralysis.Polio is mainly transmitted through the fecal-oral route, meaning it is often spread through contaminated water or food. It can also be transmitted through direct contact with an infected person's feces or respiratory secretions.Most people infected with poliovirus do not exhibit any symptoms and may not even be aware they are carrying the virus. In some cases, especially among children, polio can cause flu-like symptoms. In a small percentage of cases, the virus invades the nervous system, leading to muscle weakness or paralysis.There are three types of poliovirus: type 1, type 2, and type 3. All three types can cause polio, but type 2 has been eradicated worldwide, and the focus of vaccination efforts is on types 1 and 3.Efforts to eradicate polio are ongoing, with a focus on vaccination campaigns, surveillance, and monitoring to prevent its resurgence. Please note that the status of polio eradication efforts may have evolved since my last update in September 2021, so I recommend checking with authoritative sources for the most current information.

Plague is an infectious disease caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis. It is known for its historical significance as a devastating epidemic, particularly during the Middle Ages when it caused the Black Death.There are several forms of plague, including bubonic, septicemic, and pneumonic. Bubonic plague is the most common and typically occurs when a person is bitten by an infected flea. Septicemic plague can occur as a complication of bubonic plague, while pneumonic plague is the most severe and can be transmitted through respiratory droplets.Plague is primarily transmitted through fleas that infest small mammals, such as rats. Humans can become infected if they are bitten by fleas that carry the bacterium. Pneumonic plague can also be transmitted from person to person through respiratory secretions.Plague has been responsible for several devastating pandemics throughout history, with the most notorious being the Black Death in the 14th century, which is estimated to have killed millions of people in Europe. Modern medicine and sanitation have significantly reduced the impact of plague outbreaks.Plague has been responsible for several devastating pandemics throughout history, with the most notorious being the Black Death in the 14th century, which is estimated to have killed millions of people in Europe. Modern medicine and sanitation have significantly reduced the impact of plague outbreaks.

Kala-azar, also known as visceral leishmaniasis, is a parasitic disease caused by the protozoan parasite Leishmania donovani. It is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected female sandflies. Kala-azar is a severe and potentially fatal disease that primarily affects the internal organs, including the liver, spleen, and bone marrow. Kala-azar is transmitted to humans through the bite of sandflies infected with Leishmania donovani. These sandflies are more common in certain regions of the world, particularly in parts of Asia, East Africa, and South America.The symptoms of kala-azar can include prolonged fever, weight loss, enlargement of the spleen and liver (hepatosplenomegaly), anemia, and a general feeling of weakness. If left untreated, it can be a severe and often fatal disease.Diagnosis typically involves laboratory tests to detect the presence of the parasite in blood or tissue samples, as well as clinical evaluation of symptoms and physical examination.

questions.explanation: Smallpox is an acute, highly communicable disease caused by the Variola virus. It has been eradicated worldwide, including in India. This highly infectious disease typically begins with symptoms like high fever, chills, backache, and headache, followed by the appearance of a rash on the third day of illness. The rash initially appears on the face and then spreads to the rest of the body. It begins as small reddish spots that develop into papules. These papules then progress into small vesicles filled with clear fluid, which later evolve into pustules. Eventually, a scab forms and falls off by the third week, leaving deep pits or scars known as pockmarks. The virus is present in the oral and nasal discharges of patients and is expelled during activities like coughing and sneezing, which can infect healthy individuals.

questions.text: Which of the following symptoms indicate red sickness?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nausea is the sensation of an unsettled stomach, often with an inclination to vomit.It can result from various causes, including viral infections, motion sickness, pregnancy, food poisoning, side effects of medications, chemotherapy, or certain medical conditions.Managing nausea may involve lifestyle changes, dietary modifications, or medications, depending on the underlying cause.Hair loss, or alopecia, can be caused by numerous factors, including genetics, aging, hormonal changes, medical conditions (such as alopecia areata), and treatments like chemotherapy.Treatment options for hair loss vary depending on the cause. For example, in cases of male pattern baldness, medications like minoxidil or finasteride might be considered. Wigs, hairpieces, or hair transplants are options for some individuals. In cases of chemotherapy-induced hair loss, the hair often regrows after treatment ends.

options.text: Ulcerated skin, Nausea and Loss of hair
options.explanation: Ulcerated skin refers to skin that has open sores or lesions, often with tissue loss. These ulcers can be caused by a variety of factors, including infections, venous insufficiency, arterial disease, diabetes, trauma, or autoimmune conditions.Treatment for ulcerated skin depends on the underlying cause and may include wound care, infection management, addressing circulation issues, and sometimes surgical interventions.Nausea is the sensation of an unsettled stomach, often with an inclination to vomit.It can result from various causes, including viral infections, motion sickness, pregnancy, food poisoning, side effects of medications, chemotherapy, or certain medical conditions.Managing nausea may involve lifestyle changes, dietary modifications, or medications, depending on the underlying cause.Hair loss, or alopecia, can be caused by numerous factors, including genetics, aging, hormonal changes, medical conditions (such as alopecia areata), and treatments like chemotherapy.Treatment options for hair loss vary depending on the cause. For example, in cases of male pattern baldness, medications like minoxidil or finasteride might be considered. Wigs, hairpieces, or hair transplants are options for some individuals. In cases of chemotherapy-induced hair loss, the hair often regrows after treatment ends.

"Red and ulcerated skin" typically indicates a medical condition or skin problem that involves inflammation, open sores or lesions, and a reddened appearance of the affected area.Prolonged pressure on specific areas of the body, often in individuals with limited mobility, can lead to pressure ulcers. These sores can become ulcerated and present as red, open wounds. Proper wound care and reducing pressure on the affected areas are essential for management.Bacterial, fungal, or viral skin infections can lead to redness and ulceration of the skin. Examples include cellulitis, impetigo, or certain sexually transmitted infections like herpes.asculitis is an inflammation of the blood vessels that can affect the skin. It can cause skin ulcers and a red or purplish rash.Conditions such as venous insufficiency, arterial disease, or diabetes can lead to leg ulcers, which often appear as open sores with red, inflamed skin.This is a rare skin condition that results in painful ulcers with surrounding red or purple borders.The specific cause of red and ulcerated skin should be determined by a healthcare professional. They will conduct a thorough evaluation, possibly including a skin biopsy, to diagnose the underlying condition. Treatment will depend on the cause and may involve wound care, antibiotics, topical medications, systemic treatments, or surgical interventions, as appropriate. Early diagnosis and treatment are essential for managing such skin conditions effectively.

Nausea is the sensation of an unsettled stomach, often accompanied by the urge to vomit.Nausea can result from various causes, including gastrointestinal issues (such as infections, food poisoning, acid reflux), pregnancy, medications, chemotherapy, motion sickness, or underlying medical conditions.Anemia is a condition characterized by a deficiency of red blood cells or a lack of hemoglobin in the blood. This can result in reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, leading to symptoms like fatigue, weakness, paleness, and shortness of breath.The causes of anemia are diverse and can include nutritional deficiencies (e.g., iron, vitamin B12, or folic acid), chronic diseases, genetic conditions (e.g., sickle cell anemia), or other medical conditions that affect blood cell production.

questions.explanation: Pellagra, also known as red sickness, is a vitamin deficiency disease caused by a chronic lack of Vitamin B3 in the diet. It can also be caused by a decreased intake of niacin or tryptophan. The symptoms of pellagra include ulcerated skin, nausea, and hair loss. Possible symptoms include: Nausea and vomiting. Diarrhea. Headache. Fever. Dizziness and disorientation. Weakness and fatigue. Hair loss. Bloody vomit and stools from internal bleeding.

questions.text: If a person shows production of interferons in his body, the chances are that he has got an infection of :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tetanus, often referred to as "lockjaw," is a potentially serious bacterial infection caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani. The bacteria typically enter the body through cuts, wounds, or puncture injuries, and they produce a powerful neurotoxin that affects the nervous system. Tetanus is not spread from person to person. Instead, it is contracted when the spores of Clostridium tetani enter the body through breaks in the skin, such as wounds, cuts, burns, or animal bites. It can even occur through seemingly minor injuries like stepping on a rusty nail.The initial symptoms of tetanus can include muscle stiffness and spasms, especially in the jaw muscles, leading to difficulty in opening the mouth and giving rise to the term "lockjaw." Muscle stiffness and spasms can progress to affect other parts of the body, leading to generalized muscle rigidity, difficulty swallowing, and severe muscle pain. Preventing tetanus involves proper wound care, regular vaccination, and seeking prompt medical attention for any injury that could potentially lead to tetanus. Booster shots are recommended every ten years.Tetanus is a serious and potentially life-threatening condition, but it is highly preventable through vaccination and proper wound care. If you experience a wound that could expose you to tetanus and you're not up to date with your tetanus vaccinations, seek medical attention promptly to receive the necessary treatment and immunization.

Malaria is a parasitic infectious disease caused by the Plasmodium parasites, which are transmitted to humans through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. It is a significant global health concern, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions.Malaria is primarily transmitted through the bite of infected Anopheles mosquitoes. The parasites multiply in the mosquito's gut and are then injected into the human host's bloodstream during a mosquito bite.The symptoms of malaria can vary but often include high fever, chills, headache, and flu-like symptoms. In severe cases, it can lead to complications such as anemia, organ failure, and death.There are different species of Plasmodium parasites that cause malaria, with Plasmodium falciparum being the most deadly. Other species include Plasmodium vivax, Plasmodium ovale, and Plasmodium malariae.Malaria can be treated with antimalarial medications. The choice of medication and the duration of treatment depend on the type of malaria, its severity, and the patient's age and location. Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are often used to treat uncomplicated malaria.Malaria is a preventable and treatable disease, but it remains a significant public health challenge, particularly in regions where it is endemic. Ongoing efforts to control and eventually eliminate malaria include vaccination, improved diagnostics, and access to effective treatment.

Typhoid fever, often referred to as just typhoid, is a bacterial infection caused by the bacterium Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi. It is a significant infectious disease that primarily affects the digestive system. Typhoid is typically spread through the consumption of contaminated food or water that contains the Salmonella Typhi bacterium. It can also be transmitted through close contact with an infected person.Typhoid symptoms can range from mild to severe and often include high fever, abdominal pain, headache, weakness, and a rash with rose-colored spots. Diarrhea or constipation may also occur. In severe cases, complications can arise, affecting various organs.Diagnosis is usually confirmed through blood or stool cultures to detect the presence of the bacterium. Some individuals can become chronic carriers of the Salmonella Typhi bacterium, even after recovering from the acute illness. These carriers can shed the bacteria in their feces, posing a risk for transmission to others.Typhoid fever is a serious and potentially life-threatening disease, particularly if left untreated. Adequate sanitation, clean water, vaccination, and early diagnosis and treatment are crucial for preventing and controlling typhoid. Travelers to regions where typhoid is prevalent should take precautions to reduce their risk of infection.

Measles, also known as rubeola, is a highly contagious viral infection caused by the measles virus. It primarily affects the respiratory system and is characterized by a distinctive rash. Measles is primarily transmitted through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It is highly contagious and can be easily spread in crowded or unvaccinated populations.Measles symptoms typically begin 10 to 14 days after exposure and include high fever, cough, runny nose, and red, watery eyes. A characteristic red, blotchy rash typically appears a few days later, starting on the face and spreading down the body.The measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR) vaccine is highly effective at preventing measles. It is typically administered in two doses, the first around the age of one, and the second around the age of four. High vaccine coverage in populations helps to control and eliminate the disease.Measles is a vaccine-preventable disease, and vaccination remains the most effective way to control its spread. Timely vaccination not only protects individuals but also contributes to herd immunity, reducing the risk of outbreaks and protecting those who cannot be vaccinated for medical reasons. If you suspect you have been exposed to measles or are showing symptoms, it's essential to seek medical attention to prevent further transmission.

questions.explanation: Interferons are antiviral proteins that increase a cell's resistance to viral attacks. Since measles is a viral disease, the body produces interferons. Measles is an acute infectious eruptive viral disease of childhood caused by the RNA-containing Rubeola virus (Polynosa morbillorum). Typhoid and tetanus are bacterial diseases caused by Salmonella typhi and Clostridium tetani, respectively. Malaria is a protozoan disease caused by Plasmodium species.

questions.text: Which of the following pair of diseases is caused by virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rabies is a viral disease that affects the central nervous system. It is caused by the rabies virus, which is typically transmitted through the saliva of infected animals, most commonly through the bite of an infected animal.Symptoms of rabies in humans can include fever, headache, weakness, and later, more severe symptoms like confusion, hallucinations, paralysis, and difficulty swallowing.If not treated promptly, rabies is almost always fatal. However, post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), which involves a series of rabies vaccinations and, in some cases, rabies immune globulin, can be highly effective in preventing the onset of symptoms if administered soon after a potential rabies exposure.Mumps is a viral infection caused by the mumps virus. It primarily affects the salivary glands, leading to painful swelling of the parotid glands in the cheeks, as well as other symptoms.Symptoms of mumps can include fever, headache, muscle aches, loss of appetite, and swelling of the salivary glands, which can lead to a characteristic "chipmunk cheek" appearance.In addition to parotitis, mumps can sometimes lead to complications such as orchitis (inflammation of the testicles) in males, meningitis, and deafness.Vaccination with the measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR) vaccine is effective in preventing mumps. The MMR vaccine is typically given in two doses during childhood.

Cholera is an acute diarrheal disease caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae. It is typically transmitted through the ingestion of contaminated water or food.Cholera symptoms include severe diarrhea, vomiting, and leg cramps. The loss of fluids through diarrhea can lead to rapid dehydration and electrolyte imbalances.Cholera can be a severe and life-threatening disease if not treated promptly. Rehydration with oral rehydration solutions and, in severe cases, intravenous fluids is a critical part of treatment. Antibiotics can also be used to reduce the duration and severity of symptoms.Tuberculosis is a bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. It primarily affects the lungs but can also involve other parts of the body.TB symptoms can include a persistent cough, fever, night sweats, and weight loss. In pulmonary TB, patients may cough up sputum containing the bacteria.TB is contagious and is spread through the air when an infected person with active TB coughs or sneezes.
