Latent TB infection can also occur, where the bacteria are present but not causing active disease.TB is treated with a combination of antibiotics over a period of several months. It is essential to complete the full course of treatment to prevent drug-resistant strains of TB.

Typhoid, also known as typhoid fever, is a bacterial infection caused by Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi. It is primarily transmitted through the consumption of food or water contaminated with the bacterium.Symptoms of typhoid can include sustained high fever, headache, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. In severe cases, it can lead to complications such as intestinal perforation.Typhoid is treatable with antibiotics. Prevention measures include practicing good hygiene, avoiding contaminated food and water, and vaccination with the typhoid vaccine.Tetanus is a bacterial infection caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani. It typically enters the body through wounds, cuts, or injuries, especially those contaminated with soil or dirt.Symptoms of tetanus can include muscle stiffness, muscle spasms, and difficulty swallowing. In severe cases, it can lead to respiratory failure.Tetanus is preventable through vaccination, and booster shots are recommended every ten years. Treatment includes wound care, tetanus immunoglobulin, and medications to control muscle spasms.

AIDS is a condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). It primarily affects the immune system, leading to a weakened immune response and making the body more susceptible to opportunistic infections and certain cancers.HIV is primarily transmitted through unprotected sexual intercourse, sharing needles for drug use, from mother to child during childbirth or breastfeeding, and through contaminated blood products.Early HIV infection may not produce noticeable symptoms, but over time, without treatment, it can progress to AIDS, characterized by severe immune system impairment and the occurrence of opportunistic infections.Treatment for HIV involves antiretroviral therapy (ART) to control the virus's replication and support the immune system. With proper treatment, HIV can be managed, and individuals can live long and healthy lives.AIDS is a condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). It primarily affects the immune system, leading to a weakened immune response and making the body more susceptible to opportunistic infections and certain cancers.HIV is primarily transmitted through unprotected sexual intercourse, sharing needles for drug use, from mother to child during childbirth or breastfeeding, and through contaminated blood products.Early HIV infection may not produce noticeable symptoms, but over time, without treatment, it can progress to AIDS, characterized by severe immune system impairment and the occurrence of opportunistic infections.Treatment for HIV involves antiretroviral therapy (ART) to control the virus's replication and support the immune system. With proper treatment, HIV can be managed, and individuals can live long and healthy lives.

questions.explanation: Mumps is an infectious disease that causes fever, difficulty in opening the mouth, and painful swelling of the parotid glands, which lie just below the earlobes. It is caused by a Paramyxovirus, which is present in the saliva of the infected person. Rabies (Hydrophobia) is caused by a virus named the rabies virus. It is introduced into the body through the bite of rabid (mad) dogs. The most important characteristic symptom of this disease is the fear of water.

questions.text: Antibodies are produced by :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Leucocytes are a broad category of white blood cells, including various types like neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils. While some white blood cells play a role in immune responses, it is primarily lymphocytes that produce antibodies.
Monocytes are another type of white blood cell involved in the immune system, but they primarily function as phagocytes, which means they engulf and digest pathogens rather than producing antibodies.
Lymphocytes are a specific type of white blood cell that includes B cells and T cells. B cells are responsible for producing antibodies as a crucial part of the immune response.

The spleen is an important organ in the immune system, but it does not directly produce antibodies. It filters blood, removes damaged blood cells, and stores platelets, but antibody production primarily occurs in lymphoid tissues, such as the lymph nodes and the bone marrow.

questions.explanation: Lymphocytes secrete antibodies to destroy microbes and their toxins, reject grafts, and kill tumor cells. Antibodies are protein in nature. Monocytes (a type of white blood cell) are phagocytic in nature and engulf bacteria and cellular debris. The spleen is an organ that produces lymphocytes.

questions.text: The interferons are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antigens are molecules that can trigger an immune response, but interferons are not antigens. Instead, they are produced by cells in response to viral infections to help defend against viruses.

Interferons are indeed antiviral proteins. They are signaling proteins released by infected cells to inhibit the replication of viruses within those cells and stimulate the immune system to mount a defense against the virus.
Interferons are not antibiotic proteins. Antibiotics are substances used to treat bacterial infections, whereas interferons are primarily involved in the immune response against viruses
This option is incorrect

questions.explanation: Interferons are proteins that increase the resistance of a cell to attack by viruses by unmasking genes that synthesize antiviral proteins. In humans, three groups of interferons have been discovered interferons from white blood cells; interferons from connective interferons tissue fibroblasts; and interferons from lymphocytes.

questions.text: Which of the following is the false statement about antibiotics?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Some persons have allergy from antibiotics
options.explanation: This statement is true. Allergic reactions to antibiotics can occur in some individuals, leading to various symptoms, from mild rashes to severe reactions like anaphylaxis.

options.text: Antibiotics are capable of curing any disease
options.explanation: This statement is false. While antibiotics are effective against bacterial infections, they do not work against viral infections or many other types of diseases, such as fungal or parasitic infections. Antibiotics are specific to bacteria and are not a universal cure for all illnesses.

options.text: This term was given by Waksman in 1942
options.explanation: This statement is true. The term "antibiotics" was indeed coined by Selman Waksman in 1942 to describe substances produced by microorganisms that can inhibit or kill other microorganisms.

This statement is true. Antibiotics are natural substances produced by certain microorganisms (e.g., bacteria and fungi) to inhibit the growth of competing microorganisms. These substances have been harnessed for medical use in treating bacterial infections.

questions.explanation: Lymphocytes secrete antibodies to combat microbes and their toxins, reject grafts, and eliminate tumor cells. Antibodies are protein-based in nature. Monocytes, a type of white blood cell, exhibit phagocytic behavior by engulfing bacteria and cellular debris. The spleen is an organ responsible for producing lymphocytes.

questions.text: Nicotine acts as a stimulant, because it mimics the effect of :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Testosterone is a hormone primarily associated with the development of male secondary sexual characteristics and has a different role in the body compared to nicotine.

Dopamine is a neurotransmitter associated with pleasure and reward. Nicotine does influence dopamine release in the brain, contributing to the addictive nature of smoking. However, it's not directly mimicking dopamine's effect but rather indirectly influencing its release.
Thyroxine is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland that plays a crucial role in regulating metabolism. Nicotine does not mimic the effects of thyroxine.

Acetylcholine is a neurotransmitter that plays a role in various functions, including muscle contraction and the transmission of nerve impulses. Nicotine is structurally similar to acetylcholine and can bind to the same receptors in the brain, mimicking its effects. This mimicry leads to increased alertness and a sense of stimulation, making nicotine a central nervous system stimulant.

questions.explanation: Nicotine is the major stimulatory component of tobacco products including cigarettes. Nicotine has a number of effects on the human body similar to acetylcholine. It stimulates the passage of nerve impulses, causes muscles to relax, and causes the release of adrenaline, increasing both blood pressure and heartbeat rate.

questions.text: The blood cancer is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Haemolysis refers to the breaking down of red blood cells, which can occur due to various causes, including certain medical conditions or exposure to toxins. While it involves red blood cells, it is not a type of blood cancer.
Haemophilia is a genetic disorder that affects the blood's ability to clot properly. It is not a form of blood cancer but rather a bleeding disorder caused by deficiencies in clotting factors.
Leukaemia is a type of cancer that affects the bone marrow and blood, leading to the uncontrolled production of abnormal white blood cells. It is indeed a form of blood cancer.
Thrombosis refers to the formation of blood clots within blood vessels, which can obstruct blood flow and cause various medical issues. It is not a type of blood cancer.

questions.explanation: The normal white blood cell (WBC) count ranges from 5,000 to 10,000 per cubic millimeter of blood. Leukemia, or blood cancer, is characterized by an abnormal increase in the WBC count, typically ranging from 20,000 to 1,000,000/mm3 due to their excessive production in the bone marrow. Hemolysis refers to the breakdown of red blood cells (RBCs). Hemophilia is a condition in which blood clots slowly, while thrombosis is the formation of blood clots inside blood vessels.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trichuris trichiura: This pair is not correctly matched. Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum, while Trichuris trichiura is a type of parasitic roundworm that causes an intestinal infection called trichuriasis. These are two entirely different diseases caused by different organisms.

Trypanosoma gambiense: This pair is correctly matched. Sleeping sickness, also known as African trypanosomiasis, is caused by the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma gambiense, which is transmitted by the tsetse fly.
Arbovirus: This pair is correctly matched. Dengue fever is caused by the dengue virus, which is indeed an arbovirus (arthropod-borne virus) transmitted primarily by Aedes mosquitoes.
This pair is correctly matched. Plague is caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis, which is transmitted through fleas that infest rodents.

questions.explanation: Syphilis is caused by a spirochete (spiral bacterium) called Treponema pallidum. Sleeping sickness is a disease found in tropical Africa and is caused by the presence of the parasitic protozoan Trypanosoma gambiense. Plague is an epidemic disease in rats, which is transmitted to humans by rat fleas. Dengue is a disease transmitted to humans by mosquitoes, specifically Aedes aegypti.

questions.text: Which one of the following diseases is due to an allergic reaction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Enteric fever, also known as typhoid fever, is caused by the bacterium Salmonella typhi and is not primarily an allergic reaction. It is a bacterial infection that affects the gastrointestinal system.

Skin cancer is not primarily due to an allergic reaction. It is primarily caused by the cumulative effects of ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure from the sun or tanning beds, which can damage the DNA in skin cells and lead to cancer.
Goitre is typically caused by an iodine deficiency or other thyroid-related issues and is not primarily an allergic reaction. It results in the enlargement of the thyroid gland in the neck.

Hay fever, also known as allergic rhinitis, is due to an allergic reaction. It occurs when a person's immune system overreacts to allergens like pollen or dust, leading to symptoms such as sneezing, runny or stuffy nose, and itchy or watery eyes.

questions.explanation: Hay fever is an allergic reaction to the pollen of grasses, trees, and other plants. It is characterized by inflammation of the membrane lining the nose and sometimes the conjunctiva. The symptoms, including sneezing, a runny or blocked nose, and watering eyes, are a result of histamine released by mast cells.

questions.text: Which of the following causes plague?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trichinella spiralis is a parasitic roundworm that can cause trichinosis, a disease associated with the consumption of undercooked or raw pork containing the larvae of this parasite. It is not the causative agent of plague.
Salmonella typhimurium is a bacterium known for causing various types of food poisoning and gastroenteritis, but it is not the causative agent of plague.
Yersinia pestis is the bacterium responsible for causing plague, a potentially deadly infectious disease. Plague can manifest in several forms, including bubonic, septicemic, and pneumonic plague.
Leishmania donovani is a protozoan parasite responsible for causing a disease known as visceral leishmaniasis or kala-azar. It is not related to plague.

questions.explanation: Plague is an acute epidemic disease of rats and other wild rodents caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis. It is transmitted to humans by rat fleas. The symptoms include headache, fever, weakness, aching limbs, and delirium, followed by acute painful swellings of the lymph nodes. Bleeding under the skin, resulting in black patches, can lead to ulcers, which may prove fatal. Treatment with tetracycline, streptomycin, and chloramphenicol is effective.Trichinosis is caused by Trichinella spiralis, which lives as an endoparasite in the human intestine. Salmonella typhimurium causes enteric fevers, and Leishmania donovani is responsible for kalaazar.

questions.text: Antigens are present :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antigens are not typically found inside the cytoplasm of a cell. They are molecules or molecular structures that can stimulate an immune response when they interact with immune cells. This interaction occurs on the cell's surface.
Antigens are not typically associated with the nuclear membrane. The nuclear membrane separates the cell's nucleus from the cytoplasm, and antigens are not usually found in this location.
Antigens are not typically located inside the nucleus of a cell. The nucleus contains genetic material (DNA) and is not the primary site for antigen presentation to the immune system.

Antigens are most commonly found on the cell surface. They can be proteins, glycoproteins or other molecules that are recognized by the immune system, particularly by immune cells like T-cells and B-cells, to initiate an immune response when the body encounters foreign substances or pathogens.

questions.explanation: An antigen is any foreign substance, such as a protein or polysaccharide, present on the external coating of a pathogen, a component of vegetables, fruits, meats, drugs, chemicals, tissues, or organ transplants, which induces the immune system to produce antibodies.

questions.text: A cell coded protein that is formed in response to infection, with most animal viruses, is called :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Histones are proteins found in cell nuclei that help package and condense DNA. They are not directly involved in the immune response to viral infections.

Antibodies are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of foreign substances (antigens) like viruses. Antibodies are essential for the immune system's ability to recognize and neutralize viruses, but they are not typically produced by infected cells.

Interferons are cell-coded proteins produced and released by infected cells as part of the innate immune response to viral infections. They play a crucial role in signaling neighboring cells to defend themselves against viral replication.

An antigen is a molecule that can stimulate an immune response, such as the production of antibodies. While antigens are associated with viruses and other pathogens, they are not the proteins directly produced in response to infection.

questions.explanation: Interferons are proteins that enhance a cell's resistance to viral attacks by unmasking genes responsible for synthesizing antiviral proteins. In humans, three groups of interferons have been discovered: 'a-interferons' from white blood cells, 'b-interferons' from connective tissue fibroblasts, and 'g-interferons' from lymphocytes.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly matches a sexually transmitted disease with its pathogen?

options.explanation: Treponema pallidum: This is the correct match. Syphilis is caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum.

questions.explanation: Syphilis is caused by a spirochete (spiral bacterium) Treponema pallidum. Gonorrhoea is caused by Neisseria gonorrhoea.Urethritis is inflammation of urethra.

questions.text: Which one engulfs pathogens rapidly?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acidophils, in the context of endocrinology and histology, refer to a type of cells found in the anterior pituitary gland of the brain. These cells are also known as acidophilic cells or acidophilic pituitary cells. They are so named because they readily stain with acidic dyes in laboratory preparations. Acidophils are responsible for producing and releasing specific hormones, including growth hormone (GH) and prolactin (PRL), both of which play essential roles in regulating various physiological processes within the body.

Monocytes are a type of white blood cell (leukocyte) that is part of the body's immune system. They are produced in the bone marrow and are released into the bloodstream, where they circulate throughout the body. Monocytes play a crucial role in the immune response, particularly in the defense against infections and inflammation.Monocytes are known for their phagocytic ability, meaning they can ingest or engulf foreign particles, such as bacteria, viruses, and other microorganisms. They are often considered as the body's first line of defense against invading pathogens. Once monocytes encounter harmful agents in the bloodstream, they can migrate into tissues and differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells, which play key roles in immune surveillance, antigen presentation, and initiating immune responses.In summary, monocytes are a vital component of the immune system, contributing to the body's ability to fight infections and maintain overall health.

Basophils are a type of white blood cell (leukocyte) that plays a role in the immune system's response to allergens and certain infections. They are a subset of granulocytes, which also includes neutrophils and eosinophils. Basophils are characterized by the presence of large, dark-staining granules in their cytoplasm, which contain various substances, including histamine.Basophils release histamine in response to allergens or foreign substances. Histamine is a chemical that plays a significant role in allergic reactions and inflammatory responses.When basophils release histamine, it can cause blood vessels to dilate (widen) and become more permeable. This leads to increased blood flow to the affected area and allows immune cells to reach the site of infection or injury.

Neutrophils are a type of white blood cell (leukocyte) and a crucial component of the body's immune system. They are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the bloodstream and play a primary role in defending the body against bacterial and fungal infections.Neutrophils are highly effective at phagocytosis, which is the process of ingesting and destroying microorganisms like bacteria and fungi. They are equipped with specialized structures called lysosomes that contain enzymes to break down these invaders.Neutrophils are often the first immune cells to arrive at the site of an infection or injury. They rapidly migrate to the affected area, guided by chemical signals, and work to eliminate the threat.Overall, neutrophils are essential for the body's defense against infections and are part of the innate immune system. They play a key role in protecting the body from bacterial and fungal invaders.

questions.explanation: Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cells and are an integral part of the immune system. These phagocytes are normally found in the bloodstream. However, during the acute phase of inflammation, particularly as a result of bacterial infection, neutrophils leave the vasculature and migrate toward the site of inflammation in a process called chemotaxis. They are the predominant cells in pus, accounting for its whitish/yellowish appearance. Neutrophils respond within an hour of tissue injury and are the hallmark of acute inflammation. Monocytes are also phagocytes, but they take approximately 78 hours to reach the site of injury. Acidophils and basophils are not phagocytic in nature.

questions.explanation: An analgesic is a substance that reduces pain without causing unconsciousness, either by lowering the pain threshold or by increasing pain tolerance. There are several categories of analgesic drugs, including morphine and its derivatives, which produce analgesia by acting on the central nervous system; nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (e.g., aspirin); and local anesthetics.

questions.text: Give the correct matching of causative agent/germ and disease.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Anopheles is a genus of mosquitoes, and it is particularly known for its role in the transmission of malaria. Female Anopheles mosquitoes are responsible for transmitting the Plasmodium parasite, which causes malaria, when they bite humans or animals.Female Anopheles mosquitoes are vectors for the Plasmodium parasite, which causes malaria. When an infected Anopheles mosquito bites a person, it injects the parasite into the bloodstream, leading to malaria infection.Preventing malaria involves various strategies, including the use of bed nets, insect repellents, and antimalarial medications. Efforts to control the mosquito population and reduce breeding sites also play a crucial role in malaria prevention.

Leishmania and sleeping sickness are two distinct diseases caused by different parasites. Leishmania is a protozoan parasite responsible for causing a group of diseases collectively known as leishmaniasis.Leishmaniasis is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected sandflies. The disease can manifest in different forms, including cutaneous leishmaniasis, mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, and visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar).Visceral leishmaniasis, or kala-azar, is the most severe form and affects internal organs, particularly the spleen and liver. It is often fatal if left untreated.Sleeping sickness is caused by a different type of protozoan parasite, Trypanosoma brucei, and is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected tsetse flies.The disease is characterized by two stages: an early hemolymphatic stage with symptoms like fever, joint pain, and itching, and a late encephalitic stage in which the parasites invade the central nervous system, leading to neurological symptoms such as confusion, sleep disturbances, and coma.

Glossina, also known as tsetse flies, and kala-azar (visceral leishmaniasis) are two separate entities with different disease associations. Glossina, commonly referred to as tsetse flies, is a type of blood-feeding fly found in certain regions of sub-Saharan Africa.These flies are known for their role as vectors in the transmission of trypanosomes, which are parasites responsible for causing African trypanosomiasis, commonly known as sleeping sickness. There are two forms of sleeping sickness: Trypanosoma brucei gambiense and Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense.Kala-azar, also known as visceral leishmaniasis, is a severe parasitic disease caused by protozoa of the Leishmania genus.The disease is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected sandflies, not tsetse flies. Kala-azar primarily affects internal organs, such as the spleen and liver, and is characterized by symptoms like prolonged fever, weight loss, anemia, and an enlarged abdomen.

Wuchereria bancrofti is a parasitic nematode worm that causes lymphatic filariasis, commonly known as elephantiasis. This parasitic worm is one of the primary causative agents of lymphatic filariasis, a neglected tropical disease. It primarily affects the lymphatic system in the human body.Lymphatic filariasis is characterized by the presence of adult worms in the lymphatic vessels and the accumulation of lymphatic fluid. This can lead to various symptoms, including swelling and enlargement of body parts, such as the legs and scrotum. In advanced stages, it can cause severe disfigurement and disability.In summary, Wuchereria bancrofti is a parasitic worm that causes lymphatic filariasis, a debilitating disease that affects millions of people in endemic regions. It is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected mosquitoes, and efforts are made to control its spread and provide treatment to those affected.

questions.explanation: Wuchereria bancrofti is a parasitic filarial nematode worm transmitted by a mosquito vector. It is one of the three parasites that cause lymphatic filariasis. If left untreated, it can lead to elephantiasis. Treatment options are limited, and no vaccines have been developed.Malaria is caused by protozoan parasites of the genus Plasmodium and is transmitted by female Anopheles mosquitoes.Sleeping sickness, also known as African trypanosomiasis, is a parasitic disease affecting both people and animals. It is caused by protozoa of the genus Trypanosoma and transmitted by the tsetse fly.Kala-azar, caused by the protozoan Leishmania, is transmitted by sandflies.

questions.text: Which of the following are found in extreme saline conditions?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Eubacteria, also known as true bacteria or simply bacteria, are one of the two major domains of prokaryotes, the other being Archaea. They represent a vast and diverse group of microorganisms that are characterized by their simple cell structure and lack of a cell nucleus.Eubacteria are characterized by a single-celled, prokaryotic structure. They lack a defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.Eubacteria display a wide range of metabolic capabilities. They can be autotrophic (producing their own food through photosynthesis or chemosynthesis) or heterotrophic (relying on organic substances for nutrition).The diversity of eubacteria is immense, with various species exhibiting different shapes, sizes, and metabolic abilities. They include well-known bacteria such as Escherichia coli, Bacillus subtilis, and Streptococcus pyogenes.Eubacteria are a crucial group of microorganisms that have a significant impact on various aspects of life on Earth. Their study is vital in fields such as microbiology, ecology, and biotechnology.

Cyanobacteria, often referred to as blue-green algae, are a group of photosynthetic bacteria that are capable of producing oxygen through photosynthesis.Cyanobacteria are photosynthetic microorganisms. They contain pigments, including chlorophyll, that enable them to capture light energy from the sun and convert carbon dioxide and water into organic compounds, releasing oxygen as a byproduct.Cyanobacteria are commonly found in various aquatic environments, such as freshwater lakes, ponds, rivers, and oceans. They can thrive in a wide range of conditions, from tropical to polar regions.Cyanobacteria can exist as single cells or form colonies and filaments. Some species are capable of nitrogen fixation, converting atmospheric nitrogen into a form usable by plants.Cyanobacteria are ecologically significant microorganisms with a rich evolutionary history and a profound impact on the Earth's atmosphere and ecosystems.

Mycobacteria are a group of bacteria that belong to the genus Mycobacterium. They are characterized by unique cell wall structures and are known for their association with various diseases, including tuberculosis and leprosy. Mycobacteria have a distinctive cell wall structure that contains high levels of lipids, such as mycolic acids. This unique cell wall composition contributes to their resistance to many antibiotics and staining techniques, leading to the development of acid-fast staining to identify them.The genus Mycobacterium comprises a wide range of species, some of which are pathogenic to humans and animals, while others are non-pathogenic and can be found in various environmental niches.Mycobacteria include several species that can cause diseases in humans. Two of the most well-known human pathogens are Mycobacterium tuberculosis, which causes tuberculosis, and Mycobacterium leprae, which causes leprosy (Hansen's disease).

Archaebacteria, often referred to as Archaea, are a major domain of single-celled microorganisms that constitute one of the three domains of life, the other two being Bacteria and Eukarya. Archaea are one of the three primary domains of life, alongside Bacteria and Eukarya. They are distinct from both bacteria and eukaryotes and are classified as a separate domain.Archaea share similarities with bacteria in terms of their single-celled, prokaryotic nature. However, they exhibit unique features in their cellular structure, such as different cell membrane lipids and the absence of peptidoglycan in their cell walls.Some archaea are known for their ability to produce methane gas as a metabolic byproduct. Methanogenic archaea play a crucial role in the decomposition of organic matter in anaerobic environments and can be found in the digestive systems of animals.Archaea are important players in nutrient cycling and the decomposition of organic matter in various ecosystems. They contribute to the overall functioning of ecosystems and the recycling of elements.Archaebacteria, or Archaea, are a fascinating group of microorganisms with unique features and adaptations that have expanded our understanding of life's diversity and ecological roles on our planet.

questions.explanation: Halophiles, a type of archaea, typically thrive in extreme saline environments such as salt pans, salt beds, and salt marshes.

questions.text: The primitive prokaryotes responsible for the production of biogas from the dung of ruminant animals, include the
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Methanogens are a type of archaea, which are primitive prokaryotic microorganisms. They are responsible for the production of biogas from the dung of ruminant animals. Methanogens are unique in that they produce methane (CH4) as a metabolic byproduct. They are often found in the digestive systems of ruminant animals like cows, where they help break down cellulose and other organic matter, producing methane in the process.

Sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) are a group of bacteria responsible for the production of hydrogen sulfide gas (H2S) as part of their metabolism. While they play a role in biogas production, they are not as directly involved as methanogens. SRB can compete with methanogens in anaerobic environments and can influence the composition of biogas.

Halophiles are prokaryotes that thrive in high-salinity environments, such as salt flats or saline lakes. They are not directly involved in biogas production from ruminant dung but have unique adaptations to saline conditions.

Thermoacidophiles are prokaryotes that can thrive in extremely high-temperature and acidic environments, such as hot springs or volcanic areas. They are not typically associated with biogas production from ruminant dung but have adapted to harsh conditions.

They are not directly involved in biogas production from ruminant dung but have unique adaptations to saline conditions.4. **Thermoacidophiles**: Thermoacidophiles are prokaryotes that can thrive in extremely high-temperature and acidic environments, such as hot springs or volcanic areas. They are not typically associated with biogas production from ruminant dung but have adapted to harsh conditions.To summarize, methanogens are the primary prokaryotes responsible for producing biogas (methane) from the dung of ruminant animals due to their ability to digest organic matter and produce methane gas during their metabolic processes. This sets them apart from viruses, which typically have larger and more complex genomes.

"They lack a protein coat": This statement is correct. Viroids do not have a protein coat, which is another characteristic that distinguishes them from viruses. Viruses usually have a protein coat called a capsid that surrounds their genetic material, while viroids consist solely of the RNA molecule itself.

"They are smaller than viruses": This statement is correct. Viroids are indeed smaller than viruses. They are among the smallest infectious agents known and consist of a single circular RNA molecule, typically around a few hundred nucleotides in length, whereas viruses can be much larger and more complex in structure.

questions.text: One of the major components of cell wall of most fungi is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cellulose is a structural polysaccharide found in the cell walls of plants and some algae, but it is not a major component of fungal cell walls. Fungi have chitin in their cell walls, which is different from cellulose.
Hemicellulose is another complex carbohydrate found in plant cell walls, but it is not a primary component of fungal cell walls. Fungal cell walls primarily consist of chitin.

Chitin is the primary structural component of fungal cell walls. It is a complex carbohydrate made up of long chains of N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc) molecules. Chitin provides strength and rigidity to the fungal cell wall, similar to how cellulose functions in plant cell walls.

Peptidoglycan is a structural component of bacterial cell walls, not fungal cell walls. Fungi and bacteria have distinct cell wall compositions, with fungi having chitin in their cell walls and bacteria having peptidoglycan.

questions.text: Chrysophytes, Euglenoids, Dinoflagellates and Slime moulds are included in the Kingdom:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fungi: None of the mentioned organisms belong to the Fungi kingdom. Fungi are a separate kingdom that includes organisms like mushrooms, yeasts, and molds. They are characterized by their cell walls made of chitin and their mode of nutrition, which is typically absorptive.

Animalia: Chrysophytes, Euglenoids, Dinoflagellates, and Slime molds are not part of the Animalia kingdom. Animals are multicellular, heterotrophic organisms that exhibit a wide range of characteristics, including the ability to move and a lack of cell walls.

Monera: The Monera kingdom, which is now considered obsolete, included unicellular prokaryotic organisms like bacteria. The organisms you mentioned are eukaryotic and thus do not fall under the Monera category.

Protista: The Protista kingdom is where you'll find Chrysophytes, Euglenoids, Dinoflagellates, and Slime molds. These are all eukaryotic microorganisms that don't fit neatly into the categories of animals, plants, or fungi. Protists are a diverse group, and they can be unicellular or multicellular, photosynthetic or heterotrophic. This kingdom is often considered a "catch-all" for eukaryotes that don't fit elsewhere in the classification system.

questions.text: The imperfect fungi which are decomposers of litter and help in mineral cycling belong to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phycomycetes are commonly referred to as water molds and belong to the phylum Zygomycota.While some Zygomycetes are parasitic, many can act as saprophytes, decomposing dead organic matter, including litter, and participating in mineral cycling.

Ascomycetes are a diverse group of fungi that include the morels, truffles, and yeast, among others.Some ascomycetes, such as those in the genus Penicillium, are well-known decomposers of organic material and play a significant role in breaking down litter and recycling nutrients.

Deuteromycetes, also known as fungi imperfecti, are fungi that do not have a known sexual reproductive stage and have been classified based on their asexual structures.Some deuteromycetes are decomposers and can contribute to the decomposition of litter, although their classification is based on the absence of a sexual reproductive cycle.

Basidiomycetes are a diverse group that includes mushrooms, toadstools, and bracket fungi.Many basidiomycetes are involved in the decomposition of organic matter, particularly in forest ecosystems. They help break down litter and recycle nutrients.

questions.text: Select the wrong statement.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The term ‘contagium vivum fluidum' was coined by M. W. Beijerinck.
options.explanation: "The term ‘contagium vivum fluidum' was coined by M. W. Beijerinck." - This statement is correct. Martinus Beijerinck, a Dutch microbiologist, coined the term "contagium vivum fluidum" to describe infectious agents that are smaller than bacteria, which we now know as viruses.

options.text: Mosaic disease in tobacco and AIDS in human being are caused by viruses.
options.explanation: "Mosaic disease in tobacco and AIDS in human beings are caused by viruses." - This statement is incorrect. Mosaic disease in tobacco is indeed caused by a virus, but AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is caused by HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus), not a virus in general. It's a more specific virus that leads to AIDS.

options.text: The viroids were discovered by D.J.Ivanowsky.
options.explanation: "The viroids were discovered by D.J. Ivanowsky." - This statement is incorrect. The discovery of viroids is credited to the American pathologist Theodor O. Diener, not D.J. Ivanowsky. Viroids are small, infectious RNA molecules that are distinct from viruses.

options.text: W.M.Stanley showed that viruses could be crystallised.
options.explanation: "W.M. Stanley showed that viruses could be crystallized." - This statement is correct. Wendell Meredith Stanley, an American biochemist, successfully crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus in 1935. His work provided important evidence that viruses are distinct entities that can be purified and studied like other biological molecules.

questions.text: In which group of organisms the cell walls form two thin overlapping shells which fit together?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dinoflagellates:Dinoflagellates are a group of protists, specifically algae. questions.text: True nucleus is present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vaucheria: Vaucheria is a multicellular green alga belonging to the class Xanthophyceae. It lacks a true nucleus because it has a unique type of nucleus known as a coenocytic nucleus. In coenocytic organisms, the nuclear material is not enclosed within a membrane-bound nucleus.
Volvox, on the other hand, is a green alga that possesses a true nucleus. It belongs to the Chlorophyta group of green algae, which are eukaryotic organisms with well-defined, membrane-bound nuclei.

Anabaena: Anabaena is a genus of filamentous cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) that also lacks a true nucleus. Like many prokaryotes, cyanobacteria have a nucleoid region where their genetic material is located, but it is not enclosed within a membrane-bound nucleus.

Mucor: Mucor is a genus of fungi, and like other fungi, it lacks a true nucleus. Fungi have a different type of nucleus compared to eukaryotic cells, known as a nucleoid, where the genetic material is not enclosed within a nuclear membrane.

questions.text: Which of the following shows coiled RNA strand and capsomeres?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Polio Virus: The Polio virus is an enterovirus with a single-stranded RNA genome, but it is not typically associated with a coiled RNA strand and capsomeres.

Tobacco Mosaic Virus: This virus is known for its distinctive appearance, featuring a coiled or helical RNA strand surrounded by protein subunits called capsomeres. This virus primarily infects plants, causing characteristic mosaic patterns on leaves.

Measles Virus: Measles is caused by a paramyxovirus with a single-stranded RNA genome. It does not exhibit a coiled RNA strand and capsomeres; instead, it has a lipid envelope with surface glycoproteins.

Retrovirus: Retroviruses, such as HIV, have a single-stranded RNA genome, but they are characterized by the reverse transcription process, where RNA is converted into DNA. They do not have a coiled RNA strand and capsomeres.

questions.explanation: So, in this context, the Tobacco Mosaic Virus fits the description you provided.Tobacco mosaic virus is a RNA virus that causes tobacco mosaic disease. It has single stranded coiled RNA molecule as its genetic material a part of which hangs outside the protein coat. Protein coat consists of approximately 2130 capsomeres which are helically arranged to form a hollow cylinder of about 4 nm diameter.

questions.text: Viruses have
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: DNA enclosed in a protein coat
options.explanation: DNA enclosed in a protein coat:This describes the structure of many viruses. Viruses are genetic entities that can contain either DNA or RNA as their genetic material. This genetic material is enclosed in a protein coat called a capsid. Some viruses also have an outer lipid envelope derived from the host cell's membrane.

Prokaryotic nucleus:Viruses do not have a nucleus, and they are not classified as prokaryotic or eukaryotic because they are not considered living organisms. Instead, they are considered obligate intracellular parasites, relying on host cells to replicate.

Single chromosome:Viruses typically have a single chromosome (either DNA or RNA), which carries the genetic information necessary for their replication. However, the term "chromosome" is more commonly associated with the genetic material found in living cells rather than viruses.

Both DNA and RNA:Some viruses have DNA as their genetic material (DNA viruses), while others have RNA as their genetic material (RNA viruses). They do not contain both DNA and RNA simultaneously. The choice of DNA or RNA depends on the specific type of virus.

Instead, they are considered obligate intracellular parasites, relying on host cells to replicate.3. **Single chromosome**: - Viruses typically have a single chromosome (either DNA or RNA), which carries the genetic information necessary for their replication. However, the term "chromosome" is more commonly associated with the genetic material found in living cells rather than viruses.4. **Both DNA and RNA**: - Some viruses have DNA as their genetic material (DNA viruses), while others have RNA as their genetic material (RNA viruses). They do not contain both DNA and RNA simultaneously. The choice of DNA or RNA depends on the specific type of virus.So, the most accurate statement about viruses is that they contain genetic material (either DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat, and they lack a prokaryotic or eukaryotic nucleus.Viruses are nucleoprotein entities which are able to utilize synthetic machinery of a living cell of the host organism for its multiplication which does not involve growth and division. They have either RNA or DNA as genetic material and a protein coat.

questions.text: Archaebacteria differ from eubacteria in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell Membrane Structure: Archaebacteria have unique cell membrane structures that are distinct from the typical phospholipid bilayer found in eubacteria. They often have ether-linked lipids in their cell membranes, whereas eubacteria have ester-linked lipids. This distinction is important because it impacts the stability and function of the cell membrane in different environmental conditions.
Mode of Nutrition: Both archaebacteria and eubacteria can be heterotrophic (obtaining energy from organic compounds) or autotrophic (producing their own energy through processes like photosynthesis).

Cell Shape: Archaebacteria can have diverse cell shapes, similar to eubacteria. Both groups can be rod-shaped, spherical, spiral, or pleomorphic. The distinction between the two groups is not typically based on cell shape.

Mode of Reproduction: Both archaebacteria and eubacteria reproduce asexually through processes like binary fission. Some archaebacteria also exhibit genetic recombination through mechanisms like horizontal gene transfer. The modes of reproduction are not a primary distinguishing feature between the two groups.

questions.explanation: The archaebacteria are the ‘ancient’ bacteria that include extremophiles like methanogens, halophiles and thermophiles. They represent some of the most ancient of life forms that persist today. They have both eubacterial and eukaryotic characters besides the features unique to them. Their mode of reproduction, nutrition and cell shape and size resembles a typical eubacteria. Lipids of their cytoplasmic membranes are ether linked unlike eubacteria which contain glycerol ester lipids in their cell membrane.Archaebacteria have unique cell membrane structures that are distinct from the typical phospholipid bilayer found in eubacteria. They often have ether-linked lipids in their cell membranes, whereas eubacteria have ester-linked lipids. This distinction is important because it impacts the stability and function of the cell membrane in different environmental conditions.

questions.text: The motile bacteria are able to move by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fimbriae: Fimbriae are short, hair-like appendages found on the surface of some bacterial cells. They are involved in attachment and adherence to surfaces rather than movement. Fimbriae help bacteria stick to host cells or other surfaces, facilitating colonization and infection.

Flagella: Flagella are long, whip-like appendages that protrude from the surface of some bacteria. These structures are primarily responsible for bacterial motility. Bacterial flagella rotate like a propeller, creating thrust that propels the bacterium through its environment. The rotation of flagella allows bacteria to move towards or away from various stimuli in their surroundings.

Cilia: Cilia are relatively short, hair-like projections found on the surface of some eukaryotic cells, including certain types of single-celled organisms like paramecia. Bacteria do not typically possess cilia. Cilia function in a manner similar to flagella, with coordinated beating motions that enable movement.

Pili: Pili are thin, filamentous structures found on the surface of some bacteria. They serve multiple functions, including adhesion to surfaces and other cells. Pili, however, are not primarily involved in bacterial motility. Instead, they facilitate processes like conjugation, which allows for the exchange of genetic material between bacterial cells.

questions.explanation: In summary, among the options you mentioned, flagella are the primary structures responsible for the motility of bacteria, while fimbriae and pili play roles in attachment and adherence, and cilia are typically found in certain eukaryotic cells rather than bacteria.

questions.text: Anoxygenic photosynthesis is characteristic of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rhodospirillum is a type of purple non-sulfur bacteria.It is characteristic of anoxygenic photosynthesis.In anoxygenic photosynthesis, Rhodospirillum uses various electron donors other than water (like hydrogen sulfide or organic compounds) and does not produce oxygen during the process.

Spirogyra is a green filamentous alga.It does not perform anoxygenic photosynthesis; instead, it engages in oxygenic photosynthesis, similar to most green plants.During oxygenic photosynthesis, Spirogyra, like other photosynthetic organisms, utilizes water as an electron donor and releases oxygen as a byproduct.
Chlamydomonas is a unicellular green alga.It also performs oxygenic photosynthesis, just like Spirogyra, and produces oxygen during the process.
Ulva is a multicellular green seaweed.Like Spirogyra and Chlamydomonas, Ulva carries out oxygenic photosynthesis, producing oxygen as a byproduct.

questions.explanation: In Rhodospirillum, the electron donor is an organic compound instead of water hence no oxygen is released, i.e., anoxygenic photosynthesis occurs. In other plants, water is used as an electron donor and H+In summary, Rhodospirillum is the organism characteristic of anoxygenic photosynthesis, while Spirogyra, Chlamydomonas, and Ulva are examples of organisms that engage in oxygenic photosynthesis and produce oxygen during the process.

questions.text: Which the following are likely to be present in deep sea water?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Blue green algae are photosynthetic microorganisms capable of living in various aquatic environments, including deep sea water.They can thrive in the deep sea if there is sufficient light penetration, and they may form microbial mats or live near hydrothermal vents where there is some light and a source of nutrients.But there are certain conditions and not all are adapted to it so it is incorrect.

Saprophytic fungi are decomposers that obtain nutrients from dead organic matter.Deep sea water typically lacks abundant organic material, as the primary source of organic matter is derived from surface waters. Therefore, it is unlikely that saprophytic fungi are present in deep sea water

Archaebacteria, specifically extremophiles, can thrive in extreme environments, such as the deep sea.Some types of archaea can live in the deep sea, especially around hydrothermal vents, where they may use chemosynthesis to derive energy from chemical reactions involving minerals in the vent fluids.
Eubacteria are a diverse group of bacteria that can adapt to various environmental conditions. They can also play roles in the decomposition of organic matter.

questions.explanation: Archaebacteria belong to a group of prokaryotic organisms called Monera. These include the methanogens, which produce methane; the thermoacidophilic bacteria, which live in extremely hot and acidic environments (such as hot springs); and the halophilic bacteria, which can only function at high salt concentrations and are abundant in the world’s oceans.

questions.text: Pigment containing membranous extensions in some cyanobacteria are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pneumatophores are specialized structures found in some mangrove plants, not in cyanobacteria. These structures are aerial roots that emerge from the ground and extend above the water's surface to facilitate gas exchange in waterlogged and anaerobic environments.

Chromatophores are pigment-containing organelles found in various photosynthetic organisms, including some cyanobacteria. These organelles contain pigments like chlorophyll, carotenoids, and phycobilins, which are involved in photosynthesis and give cyanobacteria their characteristic colors.

Heterocysts are specialized cells found in some filamentous cyanobacteria. They play a crucial role in nitrogen fixation, converting atmospheric nitrogen into a form usable by the organism. Heterocysts have a thick cell wall and lack photosynthetic pigments to maintain an anaerobic environment for nitrogen fixation.

Basal bodies are cylindrical structures found in the base of flagella and cilia in various organisms, including some cyanobacteria. They serve as anchoring points and play a role in the movement of these structures. Basal bodies are not directly related to pigments or photosynthesis.

questions.explanation: Chromatophore is a pigmented lamellar or vesicular structure that can be isolated from disrupted photosynthetic bacteria or cyanobacteria. Their plasma membrane may be projected in folds into the cytoplasm forming lamellae that have, therefore, double unit membrane structure. The pigments and most of the enzymes required for the light-induced electron transport and phosphorylation processes of photosynthesis are located in the plasma membrane and lamellae.

questions.text: Why is a capsule advantageous to a bacterium?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It protects the bacterium from desiccation.
options.explanation: It protects the bacterium from desiccation: This protective capsule helps pathogens survive outside of a host. When a bacterium is expelled from a host, it may face harsh environmental conditions, including drying out (desiccation). The capsule acts as a shield, retaining moisture and preventing the bacterium from drying up, enhancing its ability to persist in the environment and potentially infect new hosts.

In the context of pathogenicity, these advantages of a bacterial capsule contribute to the bacterium's ability to evade host defenses, survive in various environments, and initiate infections within the host's body.

options.text: It allows bacterium to “hide” from host’s immune system.
options.explanation: It allows the bacterium to "hide" from the host's immune system: Pathogenic bacteria often possess capsules composed of materials that are not easily recognized by the host's immune system. This camouflage effect makes it difficult for immune cells to detect and eliminate the bacterium. By "hiding" from the immune system, the bacterium can establish infections and continue to cause disease.

options.text: It allows the bacterium to attach to the surface.
options.explanation: It allows the bacterium to attach to a surface: Bacterial capsules can aid in adhesion to host tissues and surfaces. This is a critical step in the pathogenic process. For instance, many pathogenic bacteria need to adhere to host cells or tissues to initiate an infection. The capsule can facilitate this attachment, allowing the bacterium to colonize and establish infections in the host.

questions.explanation: A bacterial capsule is advantageous to a bacterium, particularly in terms of pathogenicity, for the following reasons:1. **It protects the bacterium from desiccation:** This protective capsule helps pathogens survive outside of a host. The capsule acts as a shield, retaining moisture and preventing the bacterium from drying up, enhancing its ability to persist in the environment and potentially infect new hosts.2. **It allows the bacterium to "hide" from the host's immune system:** Pathogenic bacteria often possess capsules composed of materials that are not easily recognized by the host's immune system. This camouflage effect makes it difficult for immune cells to detect and eliminate the bacterium. The capsule can facilitate this attachment, allowing the bacterium to colonize and establish infections in the host.In the context of pathogenicity, these advantages of a bacterial capsule contribute to the bacterium's ability to evade host defenses, survive in various environments, and initiate infections within the host's body.

questions.text: Which one of the following is true for fungi?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: They lack a rigid cell wall.
options.explanation: "They lack a rigid cell wall": This statement is false. Fungi do have a rigid cell wall, primarily composed of a complex sugar called chitin. The cell wall provides structural support and protection to fungal cells.
The correct statement is that fungi are heterotrophs because they obtain their nutrients by consuming organic matter in various forms.

"They lack nuclear membrane": This statement is false. Fungi, like most eukaryotic organisms, have a well-defined nucleus enclosed by a nuclear membrane. This membrane separates the genetic material from the cytoplasm of the cell.

"They are phagotrophs": This statement is false. Fungi are not phagotrophs. They are saprophytic or parasitic heterotrophs. Fungi obtain nutrients by secreting enzymes that break down organic matter in their environment, and then they absorb the resulting small organic molecules. This process is distinct from phagotrophy, which involves engulfing and digesting whole particles.

This membrane separates the genetic material from the cytoplasm of the cell.3. "They are phagotrophs": This statement is false. Fungi are not phagotrophs. They are saprophytic or parasitic heterotrophs. Fungi obtain nutrients by secreting enzymes that break down organic matter in their environment, and then they absorb the resulting small organic molecules. This process is distinct from phagotrophy, which involves engulfing and digesting whole particles.So, the correct statement is that fungi are heterotrophs because they obtain their nutrients by consuming organic matter in various forms.

questions.text: Which statement is wrong for viruses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: "All are parasites": This statement is correct. Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning they depend on a host cell to replicate and reproduce.

options.text: All of them have helical symmetry.
options.explanation: "All of them have helical symmetry": This statement is incorrect. Viruses can have different symmetries, including helical, icosahedral, or complex structures. The structure of a virus depends on its type and can vary.

options.text: They don't have ability to synthesize nucleic acids and proteins.
options.explanation: "They don't have the ability to synthesize nucleic acids and proteins": This statement is correct. Viruses do not have the cellular machinery to synthesize nucleic acids and proteins on their own. They must rely on the host cell's machinery to replicate and produce their genetic material and proteins.

options.text: Antibiotics have no effect on them.
options.explanation: "Antibiotics have no effect on them": This statement is correct. Antibiotics are generally ineffective against viruses. Antibiotics target bacteria and not viruses because viruses lack the cellular structures and metabolic processes that antibiotics are designed to disrupt.

questions.text: Maximum nutritional diversity is found in the group:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fungi: Fungi are known for their nutritional diversity. They can be saprophytic, decomposing dead organic matter; parasitic, obtaining nutrients from other organisms; or mutualistic, forming symbiotic relationships with plants. Some fungi are even involved in predation, capturing and consuming small animals. This variety of nutritional modes makes fungi one of the most nutritionally diverse groups.

Animalia: While animals display a wide range of feeding strategies, they are generally more limited in their nutritional diversity compared to fungi. Animals are primarily classified as heterotrophs, meaning they obtain nutrients by consuming other organisms, whether through predation, herbivory, or parasitism. While there are variations in how they obtain food, it is not as diverse as the nutritional strategies seen in fungi.

Monera: Monera is an outdated taxonomic group that used to encompass all prokaryotes, including bacteria and archaea. Prokaryotes are primarily heterotrophic, relying on different sources of organic matter as their food. While there is some diversity in the types of organic matter they can utilize, it doesn't match the nutritional diversity found in fungi.

Plantae: Plants are primarily autotrophic, meaning they produce their own food through photosynthesis. They can also be classified as photoheterotrophic if they supplement their diet with some organic compounds. While plants show diversity in their ability to adapt to different environments and growth forms, their nutritional diversity is relatively limited compared to fungi.

questions.text: The most abundant prokaryotes helpu l to humans in making curd from milk and in production of antibiotics are the ones categorised as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cyanobacteria, also known as blue-green algae, are photosynthetic prokaryotes that can be found in various environments, including freshwater and marine ecosystems. While they are not directly involved in making curd from milk, they can be important in the production of oxygen and fixing nitrogen in certain ecosystems. They are not typically used in antibiotic production.

Archaebacteria, a type of prokaryotic microorganism, are known for their ability to thrive in extreme environments, such as hot springs and deep-sea hydrothermal vents. While they have unique metabolic pathways and are used in biotechnology research, they are not commonly associated with curd production or antibiotic production.

Chemosynthetic autotrophs are organisms that use inorganic compounds as an energy source to produce organic compounds. They are not typically involved in making curd from milk. These organisms are more commonly found in extreme environments like hydrothermal vents and are not typically used in antibiotic production.

Heterotrophic bacteria are prokaryotes that obtain energy by consuming organic compounds. In the context of curd production, certain species of heterotrophic bacteria play a crucial role in fermentation, converting lactose in milk to lactic acid, which is essential in curd formation. Additionally, many antibiotics, such as penicillin, are produced by various heterotrophic bacteria.

questions.text: Which one single organism or the pair of organisms is correctly assigned to its or their named taxonomic group?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Paramecium and Plasmodium belong to the same kingdom as that of Penicillium.
options.explanation: Paramecium and Plasmodium belong to the same kingdom as that of Penicillium: This statement is incorrect. Paramecium and Plasmodium belong to the Kingdom Protista, while Penicillium belongs to the Kingdom Fungi. They are not in the same kingdom.

options.text: Lichen is a composite organism formed from the symbiotic association of an algae and a protozoan.
options.explanation: Lichen is a composite organism formed from the symbiotic association of an algae and a protozoan: This statement is partially incorrect. Lichens are composite organisms formed from the symbiotic association of an alga or cyanobacterium with a fungus, not a protozoan. Lichens are classified as mutualistic associations between a photosynthetic partner (alga or cyanobacterium) and a fungal partner.

options.text: Yeast used in making bread and beer is a fungus.
options.explanation: Yeast used in making bread and beer is a fungus: This statement is correct. Yeast, commonly used in baking bread and fermenting beer, is indeed a type of fungus. It belongs to the Kingdom Fungi.

options.text: Nostoc and Anabaena are examples of protista.
options.explanation: Nostoc and Anabaena are examples of protista: This statement is incorrect. Nostoc and Anabaena are both examples of cyanobacteria, which belong to the Kingdom Bacteria, specifically in the phylum Cyanobacteria. They are not classified as protists.

questions.text: Which one of the following microbes forms symbiotic association with plants and helps them in their nutrition?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Azotobacter: Azotobacter is a free-living nitrogen-fixing bacterium. It doesn't form symbiotic associations with plants but can contribute to nitrogen availability in the soil.

Aspergillus: Aspergillus is a common filamentous fungus, but it is not typically known for forming symbiotic relationships with plants. Instead, it's often associated with various types of decomposition and growth in non-symbiotic environments.

Glomus: Glomus is a type of mycorrhizal fungus. It forms a mutualistic symbiotic association with the roots of most plants. This association, known as arbuscular mycorrhiza, enhances the plant's ability to absorb water and essential nutrients, particularly phosphorus, from the soil. It's a crucial contributor to plant nutrition.

Trichoderma: Trichoderma is a genus of fungi known for its ability to act as a biocontrol agent against plant pathogenic fungi. It can help protect plants from certain diseases but doesn't typically form direct symbiotic relationships with plants for nutritional benefits.

questions.explanation: The microbe that forms a symbiotic association with plants and aids in their nutrition is **Glomus**. It can help protect plants from certain diseases but doesn't typically form direct symbiotic relationships with plants for nutritional benefits.So, the correct option is **Glomus** when it comes to forming a symbiotic association with plants and assisting in their nutrition.

questions.text: In the five kingdom classification, Chlamydomonas and Chlorella have been included in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Protista: Both Chlamydomonas and Chlorella are classified under the Protista kingdom because they are unicellular eukaryotic organisms that do not fit into the categories of animals, plants, or fungi. They exhibit characteristics of protists, such as having a single cell with a nucleus and various organelles.

Algae: Chlamydomonas and Chlorella are also considered algae before five kingdom classification system. They are green algae, which are photosynthetic and contain chlorophyll for the process of photosynthesis. While they are not classified as plants, they share some similarities with plant cells due to their photosynthetic capabilities.

Plantae: Chlamydomonas and Chlorella are not classified as members of the Plantae kingdom, even though they perform photosynthesis, because they lack many of the complex structures and tissues found in true plants. True plants are typically multicellular and have more specialized features for terrestrial life.

Monera: Monera is a kingdom that includes prokaryotic organisms, such as bacteria. Chlamydomonas and Chlorella are eukaryotic, meaning their cells have a defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, so they do not belong to the Monera kingdom.

questions.explanation: In order to develop phylogenetic classification, R.H. Whittaker (1969), an American taxonomist, divided all the organisms into five kingdoms. Whittaker has used five criteria for delimiting the different kingdoms. (i) Complexity of cell structure, prokaryotic and eukaryotic (ii) 19 Complexity of body structure or structural organization, unicellular and multicellular. (iii) Mode of nutrition which is divergent in multicellular kingdoms. (iv) Ecological life style like producers (plantae), decomposers (fungi) and consumers (animalia), (v) Phylogenetic relationship. When such characteristics were considered, the fungi were placed in a separate kingdom – Kingdom Fungi. All prokaryotic organisms were grouped together under Kingdom Monera and the unicellular eukaryotic organisms were placed in Kingdom Protista. Kingdom Protista has brought together Chlamydomonas, Chlorella (earlier placed in Algae within Plants and both having cell walls) with ParamoeciumandAmoeba(which were earlier placed in the animal kingdom which lack cell wall). It has put together organisms which, in earlier classifications, were placed in different kingdoms. This happened because the criteria for classification changed

questions.text: Which one of the following also act as as a catalyst in a bacterial cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 5S rRNA: 5S ribosomal RNA is a component of the ribosome, which is involved in protein synthesis in both bacterial and eukaryotic cells. It plays a structural role in the ribosome but does not act as a catalyst.
snRNA (small nuclear RNA): snRNAs are a class of RNA molecules found in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. They are involved in processes like pre-mRNA splicing and do not typically act as catalysts.

hnRNA (heterogeneous nuclear RNA): hnRNA is an intermediate molecule in the processing of pre-mRNA in eukaryotic cells. It undergoes modifications to become mature mRNA and does not act as a catalyst.

23S rRNA: 23S ribosomal RNA is part of the large subunit of the bacterial ribosome. It has peptidyl transferase activity, which catalyzes the formation of peptide bonds during protein synthesis. This catalytic activity makes it an essential component for the translation of genetic information into proteins in bacterial cells.

questions.explanation: Among the options you provided, 23S rRNA also acts as a catalyst in a bacterial cell. Here's an explanation of each option:1. 5S rRNA: 5S ribosomal RNA is a component of the ribosome, which is involved in protein synthesis in both bacterial and eukaryotic cells. It plays a structural role in the ribosome but does not act as a catalyst.2. snRNA (small nuclear RNA): snRNAs are a class of RNA molecules found in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. They are involved in processes like pre-mRNA splicing and do not typically act as catalysts.3. hnRNA (heterogeneous nuclear RNA): hnRNA is an intermediate molecule in the processing of pre-mRNA in eukaryotic cells. It undergoes modifications to become mature mRNA and does not act as a catalyst.4. 23S rRNA: 23S ribosomal RNA is part of the large subunit of the bacterial ribosome. questions.text: In eubacteria, a cellular component that resembles eukaryotic cell is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasma Membrane: The plasma membrane, also known as the cell membrane, is a structure shared by both eubacteria (prokaryotes) and eukaryotic cells. It serves as a selective barrier that controls the passage of substances in and out of the cell, just like in eukaryotic cells.

Nucleus: Eubacteria do not possess a true nucleus. Unlike eukaryotic cells, which have a distinct membrane-bound nucleus housing their genetic material, eubacteria have a nucleoid region where their DNA is located, but it lacks a membrane.

Ribosomes: Ribosomes are cellular structures responsible for protein synthesis. Both eubacteria and eukaryotic cells have ribosomes, but there can be some differences in their ribosome structure and organization.

Cell Wall: Eubacteria typically have a cell wall, which is a rigid outer layer providing structural support and protection to the cell. This is a feature shared with some eukaryotic cells, such as plant cells and fungi, but not all eukaryotic cells have cell walls.

questions.text: Which one of the following organisms is not an eukaryote?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Paramecium caudatum: Paramecium caudatum is an eukaryotic organism. Eukaryotes are characterized by having a true nucleus enclosed within a membrane, and Paramecium fits this description.

Escherichia coli: Escherichia coli, commonly known as E. coli, is a prokaryotic organism. It is a bacterium, which means it lacks a true nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles. Instead, its genetic material is found in a single circular chromosome in the cytoplasm.
Euglena viridis: Euglena viridis is a eukaryotic organism. It is a single-celled organism with a well-defined nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles, which are characteristics of eukaryotes.
Amoeba proteus: Amoeba proteus is also a eukaryotic organism. Like Paramecium and Euglena, it possesses a true nucleus enclosed by a nuclear membrane, confirming its classification as a eukaryote.

questions.text: The pathogen Microsporum responsible for ringworm disease in humans belongs to the same kingdom of organisms as that of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Taenia, a tapeworm:Taenia is a genus of parasitic flatworms, commonly known as tapeworms. These tapeworms belong to the kingdom Animalia. They are multicellular organisms within the animal kingdom.

Wuchereria, a filarial worm:Wuchereria is a genus of filarial worms, which are roundworms responsible for causing diseases like lymphatic filariasis. Filarial worms are also part of the kingdom Animalia.

questions.text: Virus envelope is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The capsid is the protein coat that surrounds the genetic material (either DNA or RNA) of a virus. It protects the genetic material and helps the virus attach to and enter host cells. As it is outer covering in most viruses so it is the correct answer.
A virion is a complete, infectious virus particle. It consists of the genetic material (either DNA or RNA) enclosed within a protein capsid.

Nucleoprotein is a term often used to describe proteins associated with nucleic acids (DNA or RNA) in various biological contexts. In the case of viruses, nucleoproteins are proteins that interact with and help package the viral genetic material.
The core of a virus typically refers to the genetic material, which can be DNA or RNA, along with associated proteins. It is the central part of the virus particle.

questions.explanation: The nucleic acid of a virus is surrounded by a protein coat called the capsid. The capsid is composed of protein subunits called capsomeres. In some viruses, the capsid is covered by an envelope, which usually consists of some combination of lipids, proteins and carbohydrates.

questions.text: Single celled eukaryotes are included in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Protista is a kingdom in the classification of life forms. It consists of a diverse group of eukaryotic microorganisms that are primarily single-celled but can also be multicellular.Protists include various organisms like amoebas, paramecia, diatoms, and algae. Many of these single-celled eukaryotes do not fit neatly into the other major kingdoms (Animalia, Plantae, and Fungi).

Fungi are a separate kingdom of life that includes eukaryotic organisms that are primarily multicellular. However, some fungi can exist as single-celled forms known as yeast.Yeasts are unicellular fungi that are commonly used in baking and brewing, and they are characterized by their ability to convert sugars into alcohol and carbon dioxide.

Archaea are a distinct domain of life, separate from bacteria and eukaryotes. They are single-celled prokaryotes, not eukaryotes.Archaea are known for their ability to thrive in extreme environments, such as hot springs and deep-sea hydrothermal vents. They are also essential in various biogeochemical cycles.

The term "Monera" is an outdated classification that was once used for all prokaryotic organisms, which included both bacteria and archaea.However, in modern biological classification, archaea and bacteria are recognized as two separate domains. This classification no longer exists.

questions.explanation: Protista is a kingdom in the classification of life forms. Many of these single-celled eukaryotes do not fit neatly into the other major kingdoms (Animalia, Plantae, and Fungi).

questions.text: Some hyperthermophilic organisms that grow in highly acidic (pH 2) habitats belong to the two groups:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Eubacteria and Archaea are two distinct domains of microorganisms. questions.text: One of the freeliving, anaerobic nitrogen fixer is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Beijerinckia is a genus of bacteria from the family of Beijerinckiaceae. Beijerinckia is a free living nitrogen-fixing aerobic microbe. It has abundant of nitrogenase enzyme capable of nitrogen reduction.

Rhodospirillum is a gram negative purple nonsulfur bacteria generally pink in colour. It is the facultative anaerobe. It is free-living bacteria which helps in nitrogen fixation by converting atmospheric nitrogen into ammonia by virtue of nitrogenase enzyme in the soil.
Rhizobium is a motile rod-shaped proteobacterium. It is an aerobic, free-living soil organism that infects leguminous plants.
The Azotobacter and Rhizobium are aerobic bacteria. Azotobacter fixes nitrogen under aerobic conditions in the presence of oxygen.

questions.explanation: Rhodospirillum is a free living photosynthetic anaerobic nitrogen fixing nonsulphur bacteria. It is capable of synthesizing its organic food in presence of light and in absence of O2 by a process known as bacterial photosynthesis. Beijernickia and Azotobacter are free living but aerobic nitrogen fixing bacteria. Rhizobium is a symbiotic nitrogen fixing bacteria.

questions.text: Black(stem) rust of wheat is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Alternaria solani: Alternaria solani is a fungus that causes early blight in tomatoes and potatoes. It is not the causal agent of black stem rust in wheat.
Ustilago nuda: Ustilago nuda is a fungus that causes loose smut in barley. It is not responsible for black stem rust in wheat.

Puccinia triticina: Puccinia triticina, also known as wheat leaf rust, is the primary pathogen responsible for black stem rust in wheat. It's a fungal rust disease that affects the leaves, stems, and grains of wheat plants.
Xanthomonas oryzae: Xanthomonas oryzae is a bacterial pathogen that causes bacterial leaf blight in rice and does not relate to black stem rust in wheat.

questions.text: Phylogenetic system of classification is based on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Morphological Features: This aspect of classification is based on the physical characteristics of organisms, such as their shape, size, structure, and other visible traits. Taxonomists use these features to group organisms that share similar physical characteristics into the same taxonomic category. For example, animals with backbones are classified as vertebrates based on their morphological feature of having a spinal column.

Chemical Constituents: Some organisms have distinct chemical compounds or constituents that are unique to their group. These chemicals can be used for classification. For instance, the presence of specific chemical compounds in the cell walls of bacteria, like peptidoglycan, is a key feature used to differentiate between two major groups of bacteria, the Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

Floral Characters: Floral characters are specific to plants and refer to the characteristics of their flowers. These characters can include features like petal number, arrangement of floral parts, and other flower-related traits. Botanists use floral characters to classify plants into various taxonomic groups, including families, genera, and species.

Evolutionary Relationships: This is a fundamental concept in phylogenetic classification. Instead of focusing solely on superficial characteristics, phylogenetic classification aims to group organisms based on their evolutionary history. It considers the genetic and ancestral relationships between species. This approach often involves the use of molecular data, such as DNA and RNA sequencing, to construct phylogenetic trees that depict the evolutionary connections between different organisms.
