questions.text: When a virus is in the lysogenic cycle, which of these will occur?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Viral DNA becomes incorporated into the host DNA.
options.explanation: Viral DNA becomes incorporated into the host DNA:In the lysogenic cycle, the viral DNA integrates into the host cell's DNA. This integration is typically a stable and long-term event. The viral DNA becomes part of the host genome, and this integrated viral DNA is called a prophage in the case of bacteriophages or a provirus in the case of animal viruses. The viral genes are essentially dormant at this stage and do not immediately command the host cell to produce new viruses.

options.text: Host cell produces many new viruses before it breaks apart.
options.explanation: Host cell produces many new viruses before it breaks apart:This statement describes the lytic cycle, not the lysogenic cycle. In the lytic cycle, the host cell is hijacked by the viral genetic material to replicate and produce many new viruses before eventually bursting (lysing) and releasing the viral progeny.

options.text: The viral DNA replicates and it is separated by the cell's spindle apparatus
options.explanation: The viral DNA replicates and is separated by the cell's spindle apparatus:The separation by the cell's spindle apparatus is a process that occurs during cell division (mitosis or meiosis) and is not directly related to the viral life cycle. In the lysogenic cycle, viral DNA is integrated into the host DNA and does not undergo replication or separation by the spindle apparatus until the cell decides to enter the lytic cycle.

options.text: Antiviral defenses of the cell expel the viral DNA
options.explanation: Antiviral defenses of the cell expel the viral DNA:Typically, in the lysogenic cycle, the viral DNA is not expelled by the host cell's antiviral defenses. Instead, the viral DNA becomes a part of the host's genome and remains dormant until certain triggers prompt it to enter the lytic cycle.

questions.explanation: The virus uses the host to help copy its DNA or genetic information, but it is not producing any proteins.In the lysogenic cycle of a virus, the following events occur:1. Viral DNA becomes incorporated into the host DNA: In the lysogenic cycle, the viral DNA integrates into the host cell's DNA. This integration is typically a stable and long-term event. The viral DNA becomes part of the host genome, and this integrated viral DNA is called a prophage in the case of bacteriophages or a provirus in the case of animal viruses. The viral genes are essentially dormant at this stage and do not immediately command the host cell to produce new viruses.2. Host cell produces many new viruses before it breaks apart:This statement describes the lytic cycle, not the lysogenic cycle. In the lytic cycle, the host cell is hijacked by the viral genetic material to replicate and produce many new viruses before eventually bursting (lysing) and releasing the viral progeny.3. The viral DNA replicates and is separated by the cell's spindle apparatus: The separation by the cell's spindle apparatus is a process that occurs during cell division (mitosis or meiosis) and is not directly related to the viral life cycle. In the lysogenic cycle, viral DNA is integrated into the host DNA and does not undergo replication or separation by the spindle apparatus until the cell decides to enter the lytic cycle.4. Antiviral defenses of the cell expel the viral DNA: Typically, in the lysogenic cycle, the viral DNA is not expelled by the host cell's antiviral defenses. Instead, the viral DNA becomes a part of the host's genome and remains dormant until certain triggers prompt it to enter the lytic cycle.So, the correct statement for the lysogenic cycle is the first one: "Viral DNA becomes incorporated into the host DNA."

questions.text: Most commonly, lesions can be seen around mouth, lips and at other skin sites in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Influenza: Influenza, commonly known as the flu, primarily affects the respiratory system. It typically doesn't cause lesions around the mouth or on the skin. Instead, it's characterized by symptoms like fever, cough, sore throat, muscle aches, and fatigue.

Polio: Polio, also known as poliomyelitis, is a viral infection that primarily affects the nervous system. It does not typically cause lesions around the mouth, lips, or skin. Instead, it can lead to muscle weakness and, in severe cases, paralysis.

Mumps: Mumps is a viral infection caused by the mumps virus. It can cause swelling of the salivary glands, which are located near the jaw and can lead to swelling of the cheeks. While mumps doesn't cause skin lesions, the swelling of the salivary glands can give the appearance of facial lesions.

It is correct.Herpes is a viral infection caused by the herpes simplex virus (HSV). There are two main types of herpes infections, HSV-1 and HSV-2. HSV-1 is commonly associated with oral herpes and can cause lesions or cold sores around the mouth and lips. HSV-2 typically causes genital herpes, but it's possible for either virus to cause lesions on other skin sites.

questions.explanation: Lesions around the mouth and lips are most commonly associated with herpes (specifically HSV-1). Mumps can also lead to facial swelling, but it's not considered skin lesions in the traditional sense. Influenza and polio do not typically cause such lesions.

questions.text: All of the following are viral diseases except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Influenza: Influenza, commonly known as the flu, is a viral respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses. It is a highly contagious viral infection that affects the respiratory system, leading to symptoms such as fever, cough, sore throat, and body aches.

Polio: Polio, short for poliomyelitis, is a viral disease caused by the poliovirus. It primarily affects the nervous system, leading to muscle weakness and paralysis. Thanks to vaccination efforts, polio has been nearly eradicated worldwide.
Mumps:Mumps is a contagious viral infection caused by the mumps virus. It leads to swollen salivary glands, fever, and other flu-like symptoms. Mumps can be prevented through vaccination.

Tetanus is a bacterial disease.Tetanus is not a viral disease; it is a bacterial infection. It is caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani, which produces a toxin affecting the nervous system. Tetanus is often associated with wound contamination and is preventable through vaccination.

questions.explanation: So, among the options, "Tetanus" is not a viral disease; it's a bacterial infection caused by Clostridium tetani. The other three options (Influenza, Polio, and Mumps) are indeed viral diseases.

questions.text: Mad cow disease is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mad cow disease is not caused by a virus. Viruses are distinct entities that consist of genetic material (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat, and they replicate by infecting host cells.
A virion is a complete viral particle, and it is not the causative agent of mad cow disease.
Viroids are small, infectious RNA molecules, but they are not responsible for mad cow disease. Viroids typically affect plants, causing diseases in crops.

Mad cow disease, also known as bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE), is caused by prions. Prions are misfolded proteins that can induce normal proteins in the brain to become misfolded, leading to neurological damage. The disease can be transmitted to humans through the consumption of infected cattle products, resulting in a human form of the disease known as variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (vCJD).

questions.explanation: Prions are infectious particles made up of only proteins containing information for replication and cause mad cow infection and mysterious brain infection in man.

questions.text: Hepatitis C is also known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatitis A is known as infectious hepatitis .
Hepatitis B is known as scrum hepatitis.
Hepatitis C virus is RNA enveloped virus that causes infusion hepatitis.
Hepatitis D is also called Delta Hepatitis .

questions.explanation: Hepatitis C virus is RNA enveloped virus that causes infusion hepatitis. Hepatitis A is known as infectious hepatitis, while the common name of hepatitis B is scrum hepatitis.

questions.text: Pigs are reservoir for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatitis A is primarily transmitted through contaminated food and water. Pigs are not considered reservoirs for Hepatitis A.
Hepatitis B is transmitted through blood and other bodily fluids. Pigs are not recognized as reservoirs for Hepatitis B.
Hepatitis C is primarily spread through blood-to-blood contact. Pigs are not reservoirs for Hepatitis C.

Hepatitis E is known to infect pigs, and they can serve as a reservoir for the virus. Humans can contract Hepatitis E through consumption of undercooked pork or contact with infected pigs, making pigs a potential source of the virus.

questions.explanation: Most recent work of Halbur and coworker (2001) reveals that pig could be the source of infection of hepatitis E.

questions.text: The enzyme involved in viral replication are synthesized:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses lack the cellular machinery required for protein synthesis. Therefore, viral enzymes, essential for replication, are synthesized by the host cell's ribosomes using viral genetic instructions. The host cell is essentially "hijacked" by the virus to produce the necessary components for viral replication.

options.text: On the interior surface of viral membrane
options.explanation: Viral membranes do not typically contain the machinery for enzyme synthesis. The viral membrane is mainly composed of lipids and proteins, and it functions in host cell entry and exit.

Viruses lack their own ribosomes and rely on the host cell's ribosomes for protein synthesis. Viral genetic material provides the instructions for the host cell's ribosomes to manufacture viral enzymes.

options.text: On the interior surface of viral coat
options.explanation: The viral coat, or capsid, is a protective protein shell that surrounds the genetic material of the virus. It does not serve as the site for enzyme synthesis. The genetic material contains the instructions for enzyme synthesis, and these instructions are executed by the host cell's machinery.

questions.explanation: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites. They use host biosynthetic machinery for protein synthesis.

questions.text: Ribavirin is used to treat:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribavirin is an antiviral medication used in combination with other drugs to treat Hepatitis C. It is often part of a regimen for managing chronic Hepatitis C infections.

Ribavirin is not typically used as a primary treatment for Hepatitis B. Hepatitis B is usually managed with antiviral drugs specific to this virus, such as nucleoside or nucleotide analogs like lamivudine or tenofovir.
Ribavirin is not a standard treatment for Hepatitis A. Hepatitis A is typically a self-limiting infection, and supportive care is provided to manage symptoms.

Ribavirin is sometimes used in the treatment of chronic Hepatitis D, but its effectiveness is limited. Combining it with interferon-alpha is a common approach in managing Hepatitis D, although this combination may have side effects.

questions.explanation: Ribavirin is an antiviral medication used in combination with other drugs to treat Hepatitis C. It is often part of a regimen for managing chronic Hepatitis C infections.

questions.text: Virus that affects genital organs:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HCV primarily affects the liver and is transmitted through blood. It is not known for causing direct genital organ symptoms.
HSV (Herpes simplex virus) particularly HSV-2, is a virus that commonly affects the genital organs. It causes genital herpes, characterized by painful sores or blisters in the genital and anal areas.
While HIV can affect various parts of the body, including genital organs, it primarily targets the immune system. It is not classically considered a virus that causes direct genital lesions.
Various poxviruses, such as molluscum contagiosum virus, can cause lesions on the genital skin. However, they are not as commonly associated with genital infections as HSV.

questions.explanation: Different viruses attack different parts of human body such as hepatitis virus affects liver and polio virus affects nervous system. There are two types of herpes simplex viruses. HSV-1 affects lips and mouth parts and is called oral herpes. HSV-2 damages genital organs and is called as genital herpes.

questions.text: Which of the following pair of diseases is viral?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Both Rabies and Measles are viral diseases. Rabies is caused by the rabies virus, while Measles is caused by the measles virus.
Cholera is caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae, and Tuberculosis is caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
Typhoid fever is caused by the bacterium Salmonella Typhi, and Tetanus is caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani.
AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), which is a virus. Syphilis, on the other hand, is caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum.

questions.explanation: Cholera, typhoid, tuberculosis, syphilis and tetanus are bacterial diseases.

questions.text: Which virus that infects humans has no vector and is unable to survive outside human body?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Polio virus primarily spreads through direct person-to-person contact, usually via contaminated feces or oral secretions. It does not require an external vector (like mosquitoes) for transmission. Polio can survive in the environment for a limited time, but it is not as hardy as some other viruses.
The Mumps virus is primarily spread through respiratory droplets. Like polio, it doesn't have a vector and is not known for long-term survival outside the human body.
The Chickenpox virus (Varicella-zoster virus) is primarily transmitted through respiratory droplets and direct contact. It does not have a vector, and its survival outside the human body is limited.

The correct answer is "All of these" because all the mentioned viruses (Polio virus, Mumps virus, and Chickenpox virus) lack a vector for transmission and have limited viability outside the human body.

questions.explanation: All viruses cause infection in human beings and get transferred by specific vector. Viruses always depends on living host for their survival.

questions.text: Polio vaccine is given by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The polio vaccine is not typically administered through a spray. The usual methods of administration are different.
The oral polio vaccine (OPV) is often given in the form of liquid drops rather than tablets. This liquid contains weakened live poliovirus strains.
The oral polio vaccine (OPV) is administered orally in the form of drops. It is a liquid vaccine given by mouth.
The vaccine is orally administered, so it involves direct application in the oral cavity. However, the primary route of administration is oral, not localized to a specific area.

questions.explanation: Live vaccine of polio is given orally, while killed vaccines given by injection.

questions.text: Which of the following is the simplest forms of pathogens causing disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are considered simpler than bacteria but are more complex than other pathogens like prions. They consist of genetic material (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat, lacking cellular structures.

Prions are the simplest infectious agents. They are misfolded proteins and lack genetic material. Prions can induce other normal proteins to misfold, leading to disease. They are associated with neurodegenerative diseases.
Fungi are more complex than viruses and prions. They are eukaryotic organisms with a cellular structure, including a nucleus. Fungi can cause various diseases in humans, such as fungal infections.
Amoebas are single-celled eukaryotic organisms. While they are more complex than viruses and prions, they are not typically pathogens causing diseases in humans.

questions.explanation: Prions are simplest form of pathogens because they are only proteins structurally. Viruses are nucleoproteins. Fungi is multicellular and eukaryotic organisms. Amoeba is eukaryotic and unicellular organism.

Chicken pox is caused by the Varicella-zoster virus. Therefore, chicken pox is a viral disease.
Influenza, commonly known as the flu, is caused by influenza viruses. Influenza is a viral respiratory infection.

questions.text: Which of the following diseases is caused by a viroid?
questions.subject: Biology

Hepatitis B is caused by the Hepatitis B virus, which is a DNA virus. It is not caused by a viroid.
Hepatitis C is caused by the Hepatitis C virus, which is an RNA virus. It is not caused by a viroid.
Hepatitis D is caused by the Hepatitis D virus, which is a defective RNA virus that requires the presence of Hepatitis B virus for its replication. It is not caused by a viroid.

questions.explanation: The only known human disease caused by a viroid is hepatitis D.

questions.text: _ is known to cause cancers in animals.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Polio virus primarily causes polio, a disease affecting the nervous system. It is not known for causing cancers in animals.
Chronic infection with Hepatitis B virus is associated with an increased risk of liver cancer (hepatocellular carcinoma). HBV is considered a carcinogenic virus.
HIV primarily causes immunodeficiency, leading to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). While it weakens the immune system, it is not a direct cause of cancer in animals.

Rous sarcoma virus is a retrovirus known to cause tumors in chickens, particularly sarcomas. It was the first virus identified as oncogenic (cancer-causing) and has provided insights into cancer research.

questions.explanation: Rous sarcoma virus (RSV) is an Oncovirus, which is known to cause sarcomas (tissue cancers) in fowls.

questions.text: Major Cells that are infected by HIV are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: B-lymphocytes are not the major target cells for HIV infection. HIV primarily targets cells of the immune system known as T-lymphocytes.

T-helper cells, specifically CD4+ T cells, are the primary target cells for HIV. The virus binds to the CD4 receptor on the surface of T-helper cells, leading to their infection and subsequent destruction.

Natural killer cells are not the primary target for HIV infection. While HIV can interact with various immune cells, T-helper cells are the main cells affected during the progression of the infection.

T-lymphocytes, particularly CD4+ T-helper cells, are major cells infected by HIV. These cells play a crucial role in the immune response, and their depletion contributes to the immunodeficiency observed in HIV/AIDS.

questions.explanation: HIV attacks on specific type of immune cell in the body, known as T-helper cells. When HIV destroys these cells, then it becomes difficult for the body to fight against the infections.

questions.text: It acts as a template strand for reverse transriptase:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viral DNA is not used as a template strand for reverse transcriptase. Reverse transcription occurs when the viral RNA genome is transcribed into complementary DNA (cDNA).
Host DNA is not directly involved in reverse transcription. Instead, reverse transcriptase synthesizes complementary DNA based on the sequence of the viral RNA genome.
Viral RNA serves as the template strand for reverse transcriptase. The enzyme uses the viral RNA as a guide to synthesize a complementary DNA strand during the process of reverse transcription.

T-lymphocytes, particularly CD4+ T cells, are the target cells for HIV infection. While these cells are infected by the virus, they are not the template strand for reverse transcriptase. The viral RNA within the infected T-lymphocytes serves as the template.

questions.explanation: HIV is equipped with reverse transcriptase along with the other essential enzymes. Reverse transcriptase is responsible for reverse transcription, during which viral genomic ssRNA is converted into viral dsDNA in the cytoplasm of herlper T-cells.

questions.text: The phenomenon of transcription in HIV life cycle occurs in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: takes place in the cytoplasm of the host cell. After entry, the viral RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the viral enzyme reverse transcriptase. This DNA is then transported into the nucleus for integration into the host cell's genome.

The viral capsid protects the viral genome during entry and is not the site of transcription. Transcription occurs after the virus has entered the host cell and the reverse transcription process has taken place.

Transcription in the HIV life cycle takes place in the cytoplasm of the host cell. After entry, the viral RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the viral enzyme reverse transcriptase. This DNA is then transported into the nucleus for integration into the host cell's genome.
Incorrect answer.

questions.explanation: In HIV infected helper T-cells, the process of transcription occurs in nucleus while the process of reverse transcription occurs in cytoplasm.

questions.text: Attachment of HIV DNA with host DNA is done via action of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Integrase is the enzyme responsible for the attachment of HIV DNA with host DNA. After reverse transcription in the cytoplasm, the viral DNA is integrated into the host cell's genome by integrase in the nucleus.
Reverse transcriptase is not directly involved in the attachment of HIV DNA with host DNA. It plays a role in the synthesis of viral DNA from the viral RNA template.
Protease is responsible for cleaving viral polyproteins into functional proteins during the late stages of the HIV life cycle. It is not involved in the attachment of HIV DNA to host DNA.
Nuclease is a general term for enzymes that cleave nucleic acids. In the context of HIV integration, integrase is the specific enzyme responsible for integrating viral DNA into host DNA.

questions.explanation: HIV integeration is the insertion of HIV genetic material into the genome of the infected cell. This process is completed with the help of integrase.

questions.text: HIV can be transmitted by all of the following sources except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV can be transmitted through sexual intercourse, making intimate sexual contact a source of transmission.

HIV is present in blood, and contact with infected blood can lead to transmission. This can occur through sharing needles or other sharp objects, as well as exposure to contaminated blood during accidents or medical procedures.

HIV can be transmitted through breastfeeding if the mother is HIV-positive. However, antiretroviral medications can significantly reduce the risk of transmission, and in some regions, alternative feeding methods are recommended for HIV-positive mothers.

HIV transmission through saliva is considered extremely rare. While the virus may be present in saliva, the concentration is much lower compared to blood and other bodily fluids. Therefore, saliva is not a common route of transmission.

questions.explanation: Modes of transmission of HIV are blood transfusion, sexual contacts and breast-feeding. Saliva is not the source of transmission of HIV.

questions.text: Uncoating of HIV virion occurs:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Uncoating of HIV does not occur outside the cell. The viral envelope fuses with the host cell membrane during entry, leading to the release of the viral capsid into the host cell.

Uncoating of HIV occurs in the cytoplasm of the host cell. After fusion with the host cell membrane, the viral capsid is released into the cytoplasm, allowing the viral genome to undergo reverse transcription.

Uncoating does not take place in the nucleus. Once in the cytoplasm, the viral capsid disassembles, and reverse transcription occurs before the viral DNA is transported into the nucleus for integration.

Uncoating specifically occurs in the cytoplasm after the viral envelope fuses with the host cell membrane. It is a critical step in the early stages of the HIV life cycle, allowing the viral genome to proceed with reverse transcription.

questions.explanation: Uncoating of HIV occurs in the cytoplasm of the host cell. After fusion with the host cell membrane, the viral capsid is released into the cytoplasm, allowing the viral genome to undergo reverse transcription.

questions.text: Protein of HIV that helps in its fusion with T cell:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: There is no specific protein named gp31 in the context of HIV. This option is not applicable.

gp120 is a viral envelope glycoprotein of HIV. It binds to the CD4 receptor on the surface of the T-helper cell, initiating the fusion process. It plays a key role in the attachment of the virus to the host cell.
There is no specific protein named gp141 in the context of HIV. This option is not applicable.

gp41 is another viral envelope glycoprotein of HIV. After the initial binding of gp120 to the CD4 receptor, gp41 facilitates the fusion of the viral envelope with the host cell membrane, allowing the viral genome to enter the host cell.

questions.explanation: In the context of fusion with T cells, gp120 plays a crucial role in the attachment to the CD4 receptor, while gp41 is involved in the subsequent fusion of the viral and host cell membranes, facilitating the entry of the viral genome into the T cell.

questions.text: Common among all viruses during their infection cycles:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: DNA replication in viruses can occur in both the nucleus and cytoplasm, depending on the type of virus. Some DNA viruses replicate in the nucleus, while certain RNA viruses replicate their genomic RNA in the cytoplasm. This option is not universally common among all viruses.

Translation, the process of protein synthesis, generally occurs in the cytoplasm for both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including viral infections. Viral proteins are commonly synthesized in the host cell cytoplasm using the cellular machinery.

Transcription, the synthesis of RNA from a DNA template, typically occurs in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. Many DNA viruses undergo transcription in the host cell nucleus. However, RNA viruses often replicate and transcribe their RNA genomes in the cytoplasm.

Reverse transcription is a specific process observed in retroviruses, such as HIV. During reverse transcription, the viral RNA genome is reverse transcribed into DNA in the cytoplasm. This process is unique to retroviruses and is not common among all viruses.

questions.explanation: Viruses attack target cells and use biosynthetic machinery (ribosomes: present in cytoplasm) of host for protein synthesis. DNA replication and transcription of different viruses take place in cytoplasm and nucleus of host cells.

questions.text: Receptors proteins present on T helper lymphocytes where an HIV particle binds:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: CD4 is the receptor protein on T helper lymphocytes to which the HIV particle binds during the initial stages of infection. The interaction between the viral envelope glycoprotein gp120 and the CD4 receptor is a crucial step in the attachment of HIV to T helper cells.
CD8 is a co-receptor found on cytotoxic T cells. It is not the receptor used by HIV for entry into T helper cells. HIV primarily binds to the CD4 receptor on T helper cells.
CD2 is a co-stimulatory molecule on T cells. It is not the primary receptor for HIV binding to T helper cells.
CD1 is a group of antigen-presenting molecules involved in presenting lipid antigens to T cells. It is not the primary receptor for HIV binding on T helper cells.

questions.explanation: CD means cluster of differentiation. There are many types of CD proteins such as CD4 and CD8 etc. attached to cell membrane of different types of T cells. HIV attaches with CD4 that is part of T helper cell membrane.

questions.text: Which one is not an opportunistic disease related to HIV infection?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is a general characteristic of HIV infection but not a specific opportunistic disease. HIV gradually destroys the immune system, particularly T helper cells, leading to immunodeficiency.
Recurrent pneumonia can be an opportunistic infection associated with advanced HIV/AIDS. As the immune system weakens, individuals become more susceptible to infections, including pneumonia.

Pulmonary tuberculosis is considered an opportunistic infection in the context of HIV/AIDS. HIV increases the risk of developing active tuberculosis in individuals who are infected with Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
Toxoplasmosis is an opportunistic infection related to HIV/AIDS. It is caused by the parasite Toxoplasma gondii and can affect the brain and other organs in individuals with weakened immune systems.

questions.explanation: This statement is a general characteristic of HIV infection but not a specific opportunistic disease. HIV gradually destroys the immune system, particularly T helper cells, leading to immunodeficiency.

questions.text: Numerous opportunistic diseases might attack a person suffering from which of the following diseases:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Measles is a viral infection caused by the measles virus. While measles itself does not lead to opportunistic diseases, it can weaken the immune system temporarily, making the person susceptible to secondary infections. However, the term "opportunistic diseases" is more commonly associated with conditions involving severe immunosuppression.
Hepatitis A is a viral infection affecting the liver. While hepatitis A can lead to significant health issues, it is not typically associated with severe immunosuppression and opportunistic diseases.

Influenza, commonly known as the flu, is caused by influenza viruses. Similar to measles, severe cases of influenza can weaken the immune system, potentially increasing the risk of opportunistic infections.

AIDS is a condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV specifically targets and weakens the immune system, particularly T-helper cells (CD4+ cells). Individuals with advanced HIV/AIDS are more susceptible to opportunistic infections, including various bacterial, viral, and fungal diseases.

questions.explanation: AIDS is a condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV specifically targets and weakens the immune system, particularly T-helper cells (CD4+ cells). Individuals with advanced HIV/AIDS are more susceptible to opportunistic infections, including various bacterial, viral, and fungal diseases.

questions.text: In which step of the life cycle of bateriophage, tail releases enzyme lysozyme?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During this step, the bacteriophage attaches to the bacterial cell wall. The release of lysozyme does not occur during attachment.
Adsorption involves the specific binding of the bacteriophage to the surface of the bacterial cell. Lysozyme is not released during this step.

In the traditional description of the bacteriophage life cycle, the release of lysozyme occurs during the penetration step. After the bacteriophage attaches to the bacterial cell and injects its genetic material, lysozyme is released to weaken the bacterial cell wall, allowing the injected genetic material to enter the host cell.
The release of lysozyme doesn't occurs during the injection step. In this step the virus injects its DNA into the cell just as the syringe is used to inject the vaccine.

questions.explanation: In the traditional description of the bacteriophage life cycle, the release of lysozyme occurs during the penetration step. After the bacteriophage attaches to the bacterial cell and injects its genetic material, lysozyme is released to weaken the bacterial cell wall, allowing the injected genetic material to enter the host cell.

questions.text: It is an RNA non-enveloped virus:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The poliovirus is an RNA virus that causes poliomyelitis, a highly infectious disease affecting the nervous system. It is a non-enveloped virus, meaning it lacks an outer lipid membrane.

Poxviruses are a family of large DNA viruses known for causing diseases such as smallpox (Variola virus) and molluscum contagiosum. Unlike RNA viruses, poxviruses have a double-stranded DNA genome. However, poxviruses are enveloped, meaning they have an outer lipid membrane.
Influenza viruses are RNA viruses that cause the flu in humans and other animals. Influenza viruses are enveloped, meaning they have a lipid membrane derived from the host cell.

Herpes viruses are a family of large DNA viruses known for causing diseases like herpes simplex (HSV), chickenpox (Varicella-zoster virus), and others. Like pox viruses, herpes viruses have a double-stranded DNA genome and are enveloped.

questions.explanation: So, the correct option for an RNA non-enveloped virus from the given choices is A) Poliovirus.

questions.text: The unifying character of all bacteria:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Peptidoglycan is a major component of bacterial cell walls. It is a polymer made up of sugars and amino acids that provide structural support to the bacterial cell. Peptidoglycan is a defining characteristic of most bacteria, but there are exceptions, such as the group of bacteria known as Archaea, which do not have peptidoglycan in their cell walls.

Conjugation is a mechanism of horizontal gene transfer in bacteria. It involves the transfer of genetic material, such as plasmids, from one bacterium to another through direct cell-to-cell contact. While conjugation is an essential process for genetic exchange among bacteria, it is not the unifying characteristic of all bacteria.

Haploid refers to a cell or organism that has a single set of chromosomes. In the context of bacteria, they are haploid because they have a single circular chromosome in their cytoplasm. This chromosome contains all the genetic information necessary for the bacterium to function and reproduce. However, being haploid is not the unifying characteristic of all bacteria.

A capsule is a protective layer that surrounds the cell wall of some bacteria. It is usually composed of polysaccharides and provides protection against the host's immune system and other environmental factors. While capsules are a common feature in many bacteria, they are not present in all bacterial species.

questions.explanation: The correct answer, based on the options provided, is C) Haploid. All bacteria have a single set of chromosomes, which makes them haploid organisms. This characteristic is universal among bacteria.

questions.text: When cocci form a Iong chain of cells then the arrangement is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: "Diplococci" refers to the arrangement of cocci bacteria when they occur in pairs, side by side. The term "diplo" means double, so diplococci are two cocci bacteria attached together.
"Sarcina" refers to the arrangement of cocci bacteria when they occur in cubical packets or groups of eight. The cells are arranged in a 3x3 pattern, forming a cube-like structure.
"Streptococci" are cocci bacteria that form a chain-like arrangement. The cells are attached to one another in a linear manner, like a chain.
"Staphylococci" are cocci bacteria that occur in clusters. The cells are arranged in irregular grape-like clusters.

questions.explanation: So, when cocci form a long chain of cells, the arrangement is called "Streptococci."

questions.text: Which of the following is commonly present in aII bacteria.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleoid is a region within bacterial cells where the genetic material (DNA) is located. Unlike the nucleus in eukaryotic cells, the nucleoid is not surrounded by a membrane. It contains the bacterial chromosome, which is a single, circular DNA molecule that carries essential genetic information.

Pili (singular: pilus) are hair-like appendages on the surface of some bacterial cells. They are involved in various functions, including adherence to surfaces, motility, and transfer of genetic material during conjugation. However, not all bacteria have pili, as they are only present in certain species.

Plasmids are small, circular, extrachromosomal DNA molecules found in some bacteria. They can carry non-essential genes that may provide certain advantages, such as antibiotic resistance or the ability to produce toxins. Plasmids are not universally present in all bacteria; they are commonly found in certain bacterial strains.

The cell wall is a rigid outer layer that surrounds the cell membrane in many bacterial species. It provides structural support and protection to the bacterial cell. However, not all bacteria have a cell wall.

questions.explanation: The nucleoid is a region within bacterial cells where the genetic material (DNA) is located. Unlike the nucleus in eukaryotic cells, the nucleoid is not surrounded by a membrane. It contains the bacterial chromosome, which is a single, circular DNA molecule that carries essential genetic information.

questions.text: The type of glycocalyx that is loosely attached to the bacterial cell is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cell wall is a rigid structure that surrounds the bacterial cell membrane in many bacterial species. It is essential for providing structural support and protection to the cell. The cell wall is not a type of glycocalyx, so it is not the correct answer for this question.

A bacterial capsule is a type of glycocalyx that is a well-organized and firmly attached layer surrounding the bacterial cell. It is composed of a slimy and gel-like substance, often composed of polysaccharides. The capsule serves various functions, including protection against the host's immune system and helping the bacterium adhere to surfaces. Since it is firmly attached, it is not the correct answer for this question.

Slime is another type of glycocalyx, but unlike the capsule, it is loosely attached to the bacterial cell surface. It is also composed of polysaccharides, but it is less organized and more diffuse than the capsule. The slime layer can aid bacteria in adhering to surfaces and can help protect them from desiccation (drying out).

The cell membrane, also known as the plasma membrane, is a thin semi-permeable barrier that surrounds the cytoplasm of the bacterial cell. It controls the movement of substances in and out of the cell. The cell membrane is not a type of glycocalyx, so it is not the correct answer for this question.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) Slime, as it is the type of glycocalyx that is loosely attached to the bacterial cell.

questions.text: The particles which don't have nucleic acid as their hereditary material:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Virions are complete viral particles that consist of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat known as a capsid. They are responsible for the transmission and replication of viruses. Virions do have nucleic acids as their hereditary material, so this is not the correct answer.

Prions are unique infectious agents that consist solely of proteins. They do not contain any nucleic acid as their genetic material. Prions can cause misfolding of normal cellular prion proteins, leading to various neurodegenerative diseases in animals and humans.

Viroids are small infectious agents that consist of a short, circular, single-stranded RNA molecule. Unlike viruses, they do not have a protein coat (capsid). Viroids infect plants and can cause various diseases in them.

Non-enveloped viruses, also known as naked viruses, have a protein capsid that protects their genetic material (DNA or RNA). Unlike enveloped viruses, they do not have an additional outer lipid membrane. Non-enveloped viruses do contain nucleic acids as their hereditary material, so this is not the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B) Prions, as they are particles that don't have nucleic acid as their hereditary material.

questions.text: Pathogenicity of bacteriophage is due to its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bacteriophages are viruses that infect and replicate within bacteria. Unlike many animal viruses, most bacteriophages do not have an envelope. The envelope is a lipid membrane that surrounds some viruses, allowing them to enter host cells more easily. Since bacteriophages typically lack an envelope, this is not the correct answer.

The tail of a bacteriophage is a structure that extends from the capsid (head) and plays a crucial role in the infection process. The tail attaches to specific receptors on the surface of bacterial cells, enabling the phage to inject its genetic material (nucleic acid) into the bacterium. The tail is essential for bacteriophage pathogenicity, as it allows them to infect and replicate within their bacterial hosts.

The nucleic acid of a bacteriophage, either DNA or RNA, carries the genetic information necessary for the replication and production of new phage particles. Once inside the bacterial host, the phage's nucleic acid takes control of the host's cellular machinery, leading to the synthesis of new viral components. The nucleic acid is crucial for the pathogenicity of bacteriophages as it enables them to hijack bacterial cells for their reproduction.

The capsid is the protein shell that encloses the genetic material (nucleic acid) of the bacteriophage. It protects the nucleic acid from the external environment and aids in the attachment to bacterial cells during the infection process. The capsid is an essential component of bacteriophage pathogenicity, as it houses the genetic material necessary for the virus to reproduce inside the host cell.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) Nucleic acid. The pathogenicity of bacteriophages is primarily due to their nucleic acid, which carries the genetic information necessary for their replication and infection of bacterial cells.

questions.text: Antibiotics are mostly obtained from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Many antibiotics are derived from bacteria themselves. These antibiotics are often produced by certain species of bacteria as a natural defense mechanism against other bacteria or microorganisms. Some well-known antibiotics, such as penicillin, streptomycin, and tetracycline, are derived from bacteria.

Angiosperms are flowering plants, and they are not a common source of antibiotics. While some plants do produce compounds with antibacterial properties, the majority of antibiotics used in medicine are not obtained from angiosperms.

Viruses are not a common source of antibiotics. In fact, antibiotics specifically target bacteria and do not have any effect on viruses. Antiviral medications are used to treat viral infections, not antibiotics.

Many antibiotics are also obtained from fungi. Fungi produce a wide range of bioactive compounds, including antibiotics. Examples of antibiotics derived from fungi include penicillin (from Penicillium fungi) and cephalosporins (from Acremonium fungi).

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A) Bacteria, as many antibiotics are obtained from bacteria themselves.

questions.text: The uniqueness of bacterial photosynthesis is because it can occur:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is a crucial component for photosynthesis in plants and many photosynthetic organisms. However, some bacterial photosynthetic processes, like anoxygenic photosynthesis, can occur without using CO2 as the carbon source. Instead, these bacteria use other carbon compounds like organic acids or alcohols.

Chlorophyll a is the primary photosynthetic pigment found in plants and cyanobacteria. However, some bacteria utilize other types of photosynthetic pigments, such as bacteriochlorophylls, to carry out photosynthesis. These bacteria are often referred to as anoxygenic photosynthetic bacteria.

All photosynthetic organisms, including bacteria, need photosynthetic pigments to capture light energy and initiate the photosynthetic process. Therefore, bacterial photosynthesis cannot occur without pigments. This option is not the correct answer.

Some bacteria can carry out photosynthesis in the absence of light through a process known as chemosynthesis. In chemosynthesis, instead of using light as an energy source, these bacteria use energy derived from the oxidation of inorganic compounds, such as hydrogen sulfide (H2S) or iron.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B) Without chlorophyll a, as bacterial photosynthesis can occur using different photosynthetic pigments, such as bacteriochlorophylls, which differ from chlorophyll a found in plants and cyanobacteria.

questions.text: Gram-positive bacteria differ from Gram-negative bacteria in the structure of their:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleoid is a region within bacterial cells where the genetic material (DNA) is located. Both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria have a nucleoid, and there is no difference in the structure ofNucleoid the nucleoid between the two groups. Therefore, this option is not the correct answer

This is the key difference between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick cell wall that consists mainly of peptidoglycan, a polymer of sugars and amino acids. On the other hand, Gram-negative bacteria have a thinner peptidoglycan layer surrounded by an additional outer membrane composed of lipopolysaccharides.

The cytoplasm is the gel-like substance inside bacterial cells where various cellular processes take place. There is no significant difference in the structure of the cytoplasm between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, so this option is not the correct answer.

Ribosomes are cellular organelles responsible for protein synthesis. Both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria have ribosomes, and their structure is generally similar between the two groups. Therefore, this option is not the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B) Cell wall, as Gram-positive bacteria have a different cell wall structure (thick peptidoglycan layer) compared to Gram-negative bacteria (thin peptidoglycan layer surrounded by an outer membrane).

questions.text: Bacteria that need oxygen but can also live in the absence of oxygen are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Obligate aerobes are bacteria that require oxygen to grow and carry out their metabolic processes. They cannot survive in the absence of oxygen. Here is an image representing obligate aerobes:
Obligate anaerobes are bacteria that cannot tolerate oxygen and can only survive in the absence of oxygen. Oxygen is toxic to these bacteria, and exposure to it can be lethal.
Microaerophilic bacteria are bacteria that require lower levels of oxygen to grow compared to obligate aerobes. They can survive in environments with reduced oxygen levels.

Facultative anaerobes are bacteria that can grow in the presence or absence of oxygen. They can switch between aerobic respiration (using oxygen) and anaerobic respiration or fermentation (without oxygen) depending on the availability of oxygen. Facultative anaerobes can survive in a wide range of environments.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) Facultative anaerobes, as they can grow in the presence or absence of oxygen.

questions.text: A nitrogen-fixing organism that can be symbiotic is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dinoflagellates are a group of unicellular, photosynthetic organisms that are mostly marine plankton. While some dinoflagellates are capable of nitrogen fixation, they are not typically known for forming symbiotic relationships with other organisms.

Liverworts are small, non-vascular plants that can often be found in damp and shady environments. While they play essential roles in ecosystems, they are not known for nitrogen fixation or forming symbiotic relationships for nitrogen fixation.

Rhizobium is a group of soil bacteria that are well-known for their ability to fix nitrogen in symbiotic relationships with certain leguminous plants, such as beans, peas, and clover. The bacteria form nodules on the roots of the host plant, where they convert atmospheric nitrogen into ammonia, which can be used by the plant as a nutrient.

Mosses are small, non-vascular plants commonly found in moist environments. While they play essential ecological roles, they are not known for nitrogen fixation or forming symbiotic relationships for nitrogen fixation.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) Rhizobium, as it is a nitrogen-fixing organism that forms symbiotic relationships with certain leguminous plants.

questions.text: Mouth, lips, and skin are affected due to the infection of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pox viruses are a family of viruses that can cause various diseases, including smallpox (caused by the variola virus) and molluscum contagiosum. Smallpox, for example, can cause lesions on the skin and mucous membranes, including the mouth and lips.
Herpes viruses, such as herpes simplex virus (HSV), can cause infections that manifest as cold sores or fever blisters on or around the mouth and lips.

Paramyxoviruses, such as measles virus, mumps virus, and parainfluenza viruses, can cause a range of symptoms including rashes and lesions on the skin, but they do not typically affect the mouth and lips in the same way as the viruses mentioned in other options.

Adenoviruses can cause respiratory infections, conjunctivitis (pink eye), and other illnesses, but they do not typically cause specific lesions on the skin or affect the mouth and lips in the same way as the viruses mentioned in other options.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B) Herpes Virus, as herpes viruses can cause infections that lead to cold sores or fever blisters on or around the mouth and lips.

questions.text: Tumor causing virus:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: DNA enveloped viruses are viruses that have a DNA genome enclosed within an envelope derived from the host cell membrane. While some DNA enveloped viruses can cause tumors in animals, they are not specifically known as "tumor-causing viruses."

DNA non-enveloped viruses are viruses that have a DNA genome but lack an outer lipid envelope. These viruses are also known as naked viruses. While some DNA non-enveloped viruses can cause tumors in animals, they are not specifically known as "tumor-causing viruses" either.

RNA enveloped viruses are viruses that have an RNA genome enclosed within an envelope derived from the host cell membrane. Some RNA enveloped viruses are known to cause tumors in animals. Notably, retroviruses, a type of RNA enveloped virus, have been associated with tumor formation. The retrovirus human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) is an example of a tumor-causing RNA enveloped virus.

RNA non-enveloped viruses are viruses that have an RNA genome but lack an outer lipid envelope. While some RNA non-enveloped viruses can cause tumors in animals, they are not specifically known as "tumor-causing viruses" either.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) RNA enveloped, as some RNA enveloped viruses, particularly retroviruses like HTLV-1, are known to cause tumors in animals and humans.

questions.text: Which of the following are the smallest bacteria?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Escherichia coli, commonly known as E. coli, is a type of bacterium that belongs to the family Enterobacteriaceae. While E. coli is a relatively small bacterium, it is not considered the smallest.

Mycoplasmas are a group of bacteria that are characterized by the absence of a cell wall, which makes them naturally smaller in size compared to other bacteria. They are among the smallest known bacteria. One well-known species is Mycoplasma genitalium.

Spirochetes are a group of bacteria that are characterized by their spiral or helical shape. While they can vary in size, they are typically larger than Mycoplasma and are not considered the smallest bacteria. One example of a spirochete is Treponema pallidum, which causes syphilis.
Streptococci are spherical bacteria that often form chains. While they can vary in size depending on the species, they are generally larger than Mycoplasma and not considered the smallest bacteria.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B) Mycoplasma, as they are among the smallest known bacteria due to the absence of a cell wall, which contributes to their smaller size compared to other bacteria.

questions.text: Plasma membrane and everything in it is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Leucoplasts are a type of plastid found in plant cells. They are involved in the synthesis and storage of various substances, including starch, oils, and proteins. Leucoplasts are not related to the plasma membrane or its contents.

The phragmoplast is a structure that forms during plant cell division. It is involved in the organization of the cell plate, which eventually becomes the cell wall that separates the two daughter cells. The phragmoplast is not related to the plasma membrane or its contents.

The protoplast refers to the living part of the cell, including the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and all the organelles inside the cell. It is often used to describe the entire cell without considering the cell wall (in the case of plant cells) or the cell membrane (in the case of animal cells). The cytoplasm refers to the gel-like substance that fills the cell between the plasma membrane and the nucleus. It contains various organelles, including the endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, and other structures. The cytoplasm, along with the plasma membrane, makes up the cell's protoplasm.

The cytoplasm refers to the gel-like substance that fills the cell between the plasma membrane and the nucleus. It contains various organelles, including the endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, and other structures. The cytoplasm, along with the plasma membrane, makes up the cell's protoplasm.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) Protoplast, as it refers to the living part of the cell, including the plasma membrane and everything in it.

questions.text: Hepatitis A and E are transrnitted by contact with _ from infected individuals.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: "Scrum" is not related to the transmission of hepatitis A and E. Scrum typically refers to a management framework used in software development.

Hepatitis A and E viruses are primarily transmitted via the fecal-oral route. Contact with blood is not the main mode of transmission for these viruses. Hepatitis B, C, and D are the hepatitis viruses that are primarily transmitted through blood contact.

Both Hepatitis A and E viruses are transmitted primarily through contact with fecal matter from infected individuals. This can occur through contaminated food, water, or close person-to-person contact.

Hepatitis A and E can be transmitted through close person-to-person contact, which may involve exposure to an infected individual's saliva. However, the main mode of transmission is through contact with contaminated fecal matter, not saliva.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) Faeces, as Hepatitis A and E are primarily transmitted through contact with fecal matter from infected individuals.

questions.text: Viruses can infect and parasitize _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Yes, viruses can infect and parasitize mammals. Viruses are capable of infecting a wide range of mammalian species, including humans, dogs, cats, cows, and many others.
Yes, viruses can infect and parasitize humans. Many viral diseases affect humans, such as the common cold, influenza, HIV/AIDS, COVID-19, and more.

Yes, viruses that infect bacteria are known as bacteriophages. Bacteriophages are viruses that can parasitize and replicate within bacterial cells, ultimately leading to the destruction of the bacteria.

Correct option. Viruses are incredibly diverse and can infect and parasitize not only mammals and humans but also other organisms like bacteria, plants, and even other viruses. They are versatile infectious agents capable of interacting with a wide range of hosts and cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) All of these, as viruses can infect and parasitize mammals, humans, bacteria, and various other hosts.

questions.text: Which one is not a living character of the virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mutation refers to changes in the genetic material (DNA or RNA) of an organism, including viruses. Viruses can undergo mutations over time, leading to the development of new strains or variants. However, mutation is not a living characteristic itself; rather, it is a process that occurs in living organisms, including viruses.

The genome of a virus refers to its genetic material, which can be either DNA or RNA. The genome contains all the information necessary for the virus to replicate and produce new viral particles. The viral genome is a critical component for the virus's functioning and is considered a living characteristic of the virus.

Replication is the process by which a virus makes copies of its genetic material and produces new viral particles. It is a fundamental aspect of the virus's life cycle and is considered a living characteristic of the virus.

Crystallization is the process of forming crystals, which is not a living characteristic. In the context of viruses, crystallization refers to the process of purifying and obtaining a virus in a crystal form for structural studies and research purposes. Crystals of viruses can be analyzed using X-ray crystallography to determine their three-dimensional structures. Therefore, this option is accurate

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) Crystallization, as it is not a living characteristic of the virus

questions.text: Stanley crystallized:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV is a virus that attacks the immune system, leading to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). While research and crystallography studies have been done on HIV, it was not specifically Stanley's experiment.
In 1935, Wendell Stanley successfully crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV). His work was groundbreaking and provided important insights into the nature of viruses
Hepatitis B virus is a virus that causes liver infections. While it has been studied extensively, there is no specific information linking Stanley to the crystallization of HBV.
T2 is a bacteriophage, which is a virus that infects and replicates within bacteria. However, there is no specific information regarding Stanley's work with the T2 bacteriophage.

questions.explanation: Based on the information provided in the options, the correct answer is B) TMV, as Wendell Stanley is associated with crystallizing the tobacco mosaic virus.

questions.text: Phage attaches to bacterial cell surface by its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sheath is a component found in some bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria). It is a tube-like structure that contracts during the process of injecting the phage's genetic material into the bacterial cell. However, the sheath is not involved in the initial attachment of the phage to the bacterial cell surface.

The core of a bacteriophage typically refers to its genetic material, which can be either DNA or RNA. It contains the phage's genetic instructions for replicating itself inside the bacterial cell. The core is not directly involved in the attachment process.

The head of a bacteriophage is the structure that contains its genetic material (DNA or RNA). It is often surrounded by a protein coat called the capsid. The head is not the part of the phage that attaches to the bacterial cell surface.

The tail of a bacteriophage is the part responsible for attaching to the bacterial cell surface. It typically consists of a baseplate, which binds to specific receptors on the bacterial cell wall, and a tail fiber or tail spike, which helps in the attachment process. After attachment, the phage injects its genetic material into the bacterial cell through the tail.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) Tail, as the tail of the bacteriophage is responsible for attaching to the bacterial cell surface.

questions.text: Which process/es take/s place during the lytic cycle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During the lytic cycle, the viral genetic material (DNA or RNA) replicates inside the host cell, producing multiple copies of the viral genome. These copies serve as templates for the synthesis of new viral components.
Transcription is the process by which the viral genetic material is used as a template to produce viral messenger RNA (mRNA). This mRNA carries the instructions for the synthesis of viral proteins.
Translation is the process by which the viral mRNA is used by the host cell's ribosomes to synthesize viral proteins. The viral proteins are essential for the assembly of new viral particles.

Correct option. During the lytic cycle, all of these processes (replication, transcription, and translation) take place. The virus utilizes the host cell's machinery to replicate its genetic material, transcribe viral mRNA, and translate the mRNA into viral proteins for the assembly of new viral particles.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) All of these, as replication, transcription, and translation all take place during the lytic cycle of viral infection.

questions.text: Pick the false statement about AIDS:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) is the virus that causes AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome). HIV is the primary causative agent responsible for AIDS, and it infects certain cells of the immune system, particularly CD4+ T cells, weakening the immune system over time.

One of the unique features of HIV is that it is a retrovirus, which means it uses reverse transcription to convert its RNA genome into DNA. This process occurs inside the host cell after the virus enters it. The viral RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the enzyme reverse transcriptase. This newly formed viral DNA is then integrated into the host cell's genome.

HIV primarily targets and infects CD4+ T cells, which are a type of white blood cell crucial for the proper functioning of the immune system. As the virus replicates within these cells, it damages and destroys them. The progressive loss of CD4+ T cells weakens the immune system, leading to AIDS

This statement is false. As of my last knowledge update in September 2021, there is no cure for HIV/AIDS. While antiretroviral therapy (ART) is available, it helps to control viral replication and allows individuals with HIV to lead healthier lives. However, ART does not cure the infection but is a lifelong treatment. Research to find a cure is ongoing, but no definitive cure has yet been discovered.

questions.explanation: The false statement is D) Cure after proper medication, as there is currently no cure for HIV/AIDS. While antiretroviral therapy (ART) can control the infection, it does not provide a cure.

questions.text: Virus attacks and hijacks host's _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses typically do not target mitochondria directly. Mitochondria are cellular organelles responsible for energy production. Viruses mainly focus on cellular machinery involved in replication and protein synthesis.

Many viruses target the nucleus, as it houses the host cell's genetic material (DNA). Viruses may replicate their genetic material within the host nucleus or manipulate the host cell's machinery to produce viral components.
Viruses often hijack host cell ribosomes for protein synthesis. They use the host's ribosomal machinery to produce viral proteins, which are essential for the assembly of new virus particles.

Some viruses manipulate the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) to facilitate their replication and assembly. The ER is involved in protein synthesis and folding, and viruses may exploit these cellular processes for their replication and survival.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) Ribosomes, as many viruses hijack the host cell's ribosomes to produce viral proteins required for the replication and assembly of new viral particles.

questions.text: It is anti-viral:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Penicillin is an antibiotic that is effective against bacteria, not viruses. It works by interfering with the synthesis of bacterial cell walls, leading to their destruction. However, penicillin does not have any antiviral activity

Streptomycin is also an antibiotic that targets bacteria. It disrupts bacterial protein synthesis, leading to cell death. Like penicillin, streptomycin is not effective against viruses and does not possess antiviral properties.

Tetracycline is another antibiotic that primarily targets bacteria. It inhibits bacterial protein synthesis, affecting bacterial growth and replication. Tetracycline does not have antiviral effects and is not used to treat viral infections.

Correct option. Interferon is a protein produced by the body's immune cells in response to viral infections. It has potent antiviral activity and plays a crucial role in the body's defense against viruses. Interferon helps inhibit viral replication, limiting the spread of the virus within the body. It is used as a therapeutic agent to treat certain viral infections, such as hepatitis and some forms of cancer caused by viruses.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) Interferon, as it is an antiviral protein produced by the body's immune system to combat viral infections.

questions.text: _ is the second major form of hepatitis.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatitis A is a viral infection caused by the Hepatitis A virus (HAV). It is usually transmitted through contaminated food or water and is typically a short-term, acute infection.

Correct option. Hepatitis B is a viral infection caused by the Hepatitis B virus (HBV). It is transmitted through contact with infected blood or bodily fluids, as well as through sexual contact. Hepatitis B can cause acute and chronic infections.

Hepatitis C is a viral infection caused by the Hepatitis C virus (HCV). It is primarily transmitted through contact with infected blood, often associated with intravenous drug use or unsafe medical procedures. Hepatitis C can lead to chronic liver disease.

Hepatitis D, also known as delta hepatitis, is caused by the Hepatitis D virus (HDV). It can only infect individuals who are already infected with Hepatitis B. HDV is considered a "satellite virus" as it requires HBV to replicate and cause infection.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B) Hepatitis B, as it is the second major form of hepatitis caused by the Hepatitis B virus (HBV).

questions.text: Which structure is involved in the transfer of genetic material from one bacterium to another?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Flagella are whip-like appendages found on the surface of some bacteria that allow them to move. While flagella are essential for bacterial motility, they are not directly involved in the transfer of genetic material between bacteria.

Correct option. Pili are thin, hair-like structures found on the surface of certain bacteria. They are involved in the process of conjugation, which is a mechanism of horizontal gene transfer. During conjugation, one bacterium transfers genetic material, often in the form of plasmids, to another bacterium through the pilus. This allows for the exchange of genetic information between bacterial cells.

The cytoplasm is the gel-like substance inside a bacterial cell where various cellular processes take place. While genetic material is present in the cytoplasm, it is not directly involved in the transfer of genetic material between bacteria.

questions.text: Salmonella typhi exhibits which shape?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spherical bacteria are called cocci (singular: coccus). They are round or spherical. Salmonella typhi, however, does not exhibit a spherical shape.
Spiral bacteria are called spirilla. They have a spiral or helical shape. Salmonella typhi is not a spiral-shaped bacterium.
Rod-shaped bacteria are called bacilli (singular: bacillus). They have a cylindrical or rod-like shape. Salmonella typhi exhibits a rod-shaped morphology, which is typical of many bacteria.
Comma-shaped bacteria are called vibrios. They have a curved or comma-like shape. Salmonella typhi is not a comma-shaped bacterium.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) Rod-shape, as Salmonella typhi exhibits a rod-shaped morphology.

Mycoplasma is a type of bacteria that lacks a rigid cell wall. As a result, it doesn't have a definite shape like other bacteria. This group of bacteria is often referred to as "pleomorphic," meaning they can take on various shapes or sizes, ranging from spherical to filamentous. The absence of a cell wall is a unique feature of Mycoplasma, which distinguishes it from other bacteria.

questions.text: RNA viruses appear to have higher rates of mutation because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: RNA nucleotides are more unstable than DNA nucleotides
options.explanation: This statement is not entirely accurate. While RNA nucleotides might be slightly more chemically unstable than DNA nucleotides, this instability is not the primary reason for the higher mutation rate of RNA viruses.

options.text: Replication of their nucleic acid does not involve the proofreading steps of DNA replication
options.explanation: The primary reason for the higher mutation rate in RNA viruses is that their replication process lacks the proofreading mechanisms that are present in DNA replication. DNA replication includes proofreading and error-correcting mechanisms that help maintain the accuracy of the genetic code. RNA viruses, on the other hand, use RNA-dependent RNA polymerases (enzymes responsible for copying RNA) that cannot correct errors. As a result, mistakes made during replication are more likely to become permanent mutations in the viral genome.

While some RNA viruses might replicate faster than some DNA viruses, replication speed itself is not the primary reason for the higher mutation rate. The speed of replication is influenced by various factors and can vary widely among different viruses.

options.text: RNA viruses respond more to mutagens
options.explanation: This statement is not generally true. The susceptibility of viruses to mutagens can vary, but it's not a defining characteristic of RNA viruses that would explain their higher mutation rates.

DNA replication includes proofreading and error-correcting mechanisms that help maintain the accuracy of the genetic code. questions.text: Reverse transcriptase is:

This enzyme is responsible for synthesizing DNA from a DNA template. It's involved in DNA replication and repair processes in both cells and some viruses.

The term "reverse transcriptase" refers to an enzyme found in retroviruses (a type of RNA virus) that can synthesize a complementary DNA strand (cDNA) from an RNA template. This is a unique process because it involves the synthesis of DNA from an RNA template, which is the reverse of the typical flow of genetic information.

questions.text: Bacteria that convert nitrates into free nitrogen are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These bacteria convert organic nitrogen compounds into ammonia (NH3) or ammonium ions (NH4+), but they don't convert nitrates into free nitrogen gas.

Denitrifying bacteria are responsible for converting nitrates (NO3-) into free nitrogen gas (N2) through a process known as denitrification. This process helps to return nitrogen in the environment back to its atmospheric form, completing the nitrogen cycle. Denitrification occurs in anaerobic conditions (environments with little to no oxygen), where these bacteria use nitrates as an alternative source of oxygen, leading to the production of nitrogen gas.

Nitrifying bacteria are responsible for converting ammonia (NH3) or ammonium ions (NH4+) into nitrates (NO3-). They do the opposite of what the question asks for, as they convert ammonia-based compounds into nitrates.

Nitrogen-fixing bacteria are responsible for converting atmospheric nitrogen gas (N2) into ammonia (NH3) or other organic nitrogen compounds. This process occurs mainly in the roots of leguminous plants or through industrial processes, but it doesn't involve the conversion of nitrates into free nitrogen gas.

questions.text: A bacterium divides every 36 minutes. If a culture containing 105 cells/ml is grown for 180 minutes, what will be the cell concentration after that period?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: As per the calculations (refer to the explanation below) the correct answer is 32x105. Hence, this option is incorrect.
As per the calculations (refer to the explanation below) the correct answer is 32x105. Hence, this option is incorrect.
As per the calculations (refer to the explanation below) the correct answer is 32x105. Hence, this option is incorrect.

To calculate the cell concentration after a certain period of growth, you can use the formula: Final cell concentration = Initial cell concentration × 2(number of divisions) In this case, the bacterium divides every 36 minutes, so it doubles its population every 36 minutes. The initial cell concentration is 105 cells/ml, and the growth period is 180 minutes. Number of divisions = Growth period / Division time = 180 minutes / 36 minutes/division = 5 divisions. Now, plug the values into the formula: Final cell concentration = 105 cells/ml × 25 = 105 cells/ml × 32 = 32 x 105 cells/ml. So, the correct answer is 32 x 105 cells/ml.

The initial cell concentration is 105 cells/ml, and the growth period is 180 minutes. Number of divisions = Growth period / Division time = 180 minutes / 36 minutes/division = 5 divisions. Now, plug the values into the formula: Final cell concentration = 105 cells/ml × 25 = 105 cells/ml × 32 = 32 x 105 cells/ml. So, the correct answer is 32 x 105 cells/ml.

questions.text: Which vitamin is synthesized by bacteria in the human gut?

options.explanation: Vitamin A is not synthesized by bacteria in the human gut. It is obtained through the consumption of foods like liver, carrots, spinach, and other sources rich in retinol and carotenoids.

Vitamin C, also known as ascorbic acid, is not synthesized by bacteria in the human gut. It is obtained through the consumption of various fruits and vegetables, such as citrus fruits, strawberries, and bell peppers.

Vitamin K is synthesized by bacteria in the human gut. Specifically, the bacteria found in the colon are responsible for producing vitamin K. This vitamin plays a crucial role in blood clotting and bone health.

questions.text: The double-stranded viral DNA can be incorporated into the host T4 genome as a:

options.explanation: A prophage is a phage DNA that has been integrated into the DNA of a bacterial host cell. This is a common phenomenon in bacterial viruses like the T4 bacteriophage. The head of the T4 phage is not oval. It is a prolate icosahedron, which means that it is shaped like a sphere that has been stretched out along one axis. The head of T4 phage is about 50 nanometers in diameter and 100 nanometers long.

questions.text: “Two triangular structures with common base” is the description of the structure of _ of T4 phage.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tail is the part of the phage that attaches to the host cell and injects the phage's DNA. It is about 90 nanometers long and 20 nanometers wide. The tail is made up of several different parts, including the baseplate, the sheath, the tail fiber, and the whiskers.

The head is the part of the phage that contains the genetic material. It is a proteinaceous capsule that is about 50 nanometers in diameter. The head of the T4 phage is filled with about 150,000 base pairs of DNA, which is about 30% of the DNA in the E. coli cell that the T4 phage infects.

The two triangular structures with a common base are called whiskers. They are located on the collar of the T4 phage, which is a ring of proteins that surrounds the base of the tail. The whiskers help T4 phage attach to the host cell by binding to specific molecules on the cell surface.

The baseplate is a hexagonal structure that is located at the end of the tail of the T4 phage. It is made up of about 60 copies of a protein called gp23. The baseplate has six hollow tubes that extend from it. These tubes are used to inject the phage's DNA into the host cell.

questions.explanation: The two triangular structures with a common base that you are referring to are called whiskers. They are located on the collar of T4 phage, which is a ring of proteins that surrounds the base of the tail. Each triangular structure is made up of about 20 copies of a protein called gp12. The gp12 proteins are arranged in a helix that forms a rigid structure that helps the whiskers to penetrate the host cell.The whiskers are a vital part of the infection process for T4 phage. They allow the phage to attach to the host cell and inject its DNA, which is essential for the phage to replicate and infect new host cells.

questions.explanation: Within the head of T4 phage, a double-stranded DNA molecule is present.Double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) is a molecule of DNA that consists of two complementary strands of nucleotides that are held together by hydrogen bonds. The two strands of dsDNA are arranged in an antiparallel fashion, meaning that the 5' end of one strand is paired with the 3' end of the other strand.The dsDNA in the head of T4 phage is about 150,000 base pairs long. It is tightly packed into a helix that is about 20 nanometers in diameter. The dsDNA is held in place by proteins called capsid proteins. questions.text: A bacteriophage is attached to the receptor site on the host cells:

options.explanation: Slime is a general term that refers to any type of extracellular matrix that is produced by bacteria. It can be a thick, well-defined layer, such as a capsule, or it can be a thinner, more loosely associated layer, such as a slime layer.

A bacteriophage is a virus that infects bacteria. It attaches to the receptor site on the host cell's cell membrane. The receptor site is a specific molecule on the cell membrane that the bacteriophage can recognize and bind to.
