The fungus provides the alga with water and minerals, and the alga provides the fungus with food. Lichens are able to grow on bare rock because they secrete acids that break down the rock. This process is called lichenolysis.As lichens break down the rock, they create a small pocket of soil. This soil provides a habitat for other organisms, such as mosses and small plants. These organisms further break down the rock, creating more soil. This process continues until a mature ecosystem is established.Lichens are therefore important pioneer species in ecological succession. They are the first organisms to colonize bare rock, and they set the stage for the establishment of other organisms.

questions.text: Candida albicans cause:

options.explanation: Candida albicans can infect the mouth, resulting in a condition called oral thrush. It typically appears as white, creamy patches on the tongue, inner cheeks, and the back of the throat. It is more common in infants, the elderly, and individuals with weakened immune systems.

Candida albicans can also cause vaginal yeast infections, often referred to as vaginal thrush or candidiasis. Symptoms may include itching, burning, and unusual discharge in the vaginal area. This condition is common in women.
It is possible for Candida albicans to simultaneously affect both the mouth and the vaginal area, leading to symptoms of both oral and vaginal thrush.

Candida albicans is not responsible for ringworm. Ringworm is a fungal infection caused by a different group of fungi known as dermatophytes, not Candida albicans. Ringworm typically affects the skin, scalp, and nails and appears as circular, red, itchy rashes.

questions.text: Histoplasmosis becomes fatal:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Histoplasmosis typically does not lead to fatal outcomes in the majority of cases, especially in individuals with healthy immune systems.
Histoplasmosis is not always fatal; the outcome depends on various factors, including the individual's immune status.
While histoplasmosis is generally not fatal, very occasionally, it can lead to severe complications, especially in individuals with compromised immune systems.

In most cases, histoplasmosis is a self-limiting disease, and individuals with healthy immune systems recover without severe complications. However, in rare instances, especially in individuals with weakened immune systems, histoplasmosis can become severe and potentially fatal.

questions.explanation: Very Occasionally Fatal: This option is more accurate. Histoplasmosis is very occasionally fatal, primarily in cases where it spreads to other parts of the body and when individuals have weakened immune systems, such as those with HIV/AIDS or organ transplant recipients. With timely diagnosis and appropriate treatment, the risk of fatality is significantly reduced.

questions.text: Pick up the correct sequence:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the correct option.
This is not the correct option.
This is an incorrect sequence.
This is the correct option.

questions.explanation: Answer is “AIDS – Aspergillus –Aspergillosis – Death”Explanation: Aspergillosus occurs in those persons who suffers from any type of immune deficiency e.g. AIDS

questions.text: Improperly stored grains such as peanuts and corn etc. are contaminated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Aspergillus is a mold that commonly contaminates improperly stored grains, such as peanuts and corn. Some species of Aspergillus produce mycotoxins, which can harm humans and animals if consumed.

Candida is a genus of yeast, and it is not typically associated with the contamination of grains during storage. Candida is more commonly known for causing opportunistic infections in humans, particularly in immunocompromised individuals.
Neurospora is a genus of fungi, but it is not a common contaminant of improperly stored grains. Neurospora is often used in genetic and biochemical research due to its well-studied biology.
Agaricus is a genus of mushrooms, and it is not known for contaminating stored grains. Some species of Agaricus are cultivated for consumption, such as the common button mushroom (Agaricus bisporus).

questions.explanation: Answer is “Aspergillus”Explanation: It is decomposer and saprobic fungus.

questions.text: It is caused by eating bread made from purple ergot-contaminated rye flour:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Histoplasmosis is a respiratory disease caused by the inhalation of spores from the fungus Histoplasma capsulatum. It is not associated with the consumption of ergot-contaminated rye flour.

Eating bread made from purple ergot-contaminated rye flour can lead to ergotism. Ergot is a fungus (Claviceps purpurea) that infects rye and other cereal grains. Consuming contaminated grains can result in the ingestion of alkaloids produced by the fungus, leading to symptoms such as hallucinations, gangrene, and other neurological and vascular effects.
Ringworm is a fungal infection of the skin, and it is not caused by eating bread made from ergot-contaminated rye flour.

Aspergillosis is an infection caused by the fungus Aspergillus. It is not related to the consumption of ergot-contaminated rye flour but is rather associated with the inhalation of Aspergillus spores, particularly in individuals with weakened immune systems.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Egotism”Explanation: Ergot fungi refers to a group of fungi of genus Claviceps. The most prominent member in Claviceps pupurea. This fungus grows on rye and related plants and produces alkaloids that can cause ergotism in humans and other mammals who consume grains contaminated with its fruiting structure called ergot sclerotuim.

questions.text: Which one of the following is wood rotting fungi:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bracket fungi, also known as shelf fungi, are often associated with wood rot. They grow on the surface of trees, causing decay and decomposition of the wood.

Truffles and morels are types of fungi, but they are not typically associated with wood rot. Truffles are often found underground in association with tree roots, and morels are usually found in forested areas but are not directly involved in wood decay.

Mushrooms and truffles are diverse fungi, but they are not specifically known for causing wood rot. They play various ecological roles, including mycorrhizal associations with plants or decomposing organic matter.

options.text: Black bread mold or pink bread mold
options.explanation: Neither black bread mold (Rhizopus stolonifer) nor pink bread mold (Neurospora crassa) are wood-rotting fungi. They are molds that are commonly associated with the decomposition of bread and other organic materials but not with wood decay.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Bracket fungi or shelf fungi”Wood rotting fungi destroy not only living trees but also structural timber. Bracket fungi/shelf fungi cause lot of damage to stored cut lumber as well as stands of tumber of living trees.These types of fungi are commonly associated with wood rotting and can be found growing on tree trunks or dead wood. The other options are not typically known as wood rotting fungi.

questions.text: Who used the term bacteriophage for the first time?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Frederick Twort, a British bacteriologist, also made significant contributions to the early understanding of bacteriophages. However, he did not use the term "bacteriophage" and is not associated with its introduction.

Félix d'Herelle, a French-Canadian microbiologist, is credited with coining the term "bacteriophage." He discovered bacteriophages independently of Frederick Twort and extensively researched their properties and applications.

Louis Pasteur, a renowned French microbiologist, made groundbreaking contributions to the field of microbiology, including the development of vaccines and pasteurization. However, he did not discover or name bacteriophages.

Charles Chamberland, a French microbiologist, worked closely with Louis Pasteur and made contributions to the development of sterilization techniques. However, he is not associated with the discovery or naming of bacteriophages.

questions.explanation: Answer is “D’ Herelle”Explanation: As far as discovery is conserned, Towrt discovered the bacterial viruses earlier than D’Herelle, however the term bacteriophage was used by D’Herelle for the first time. Bacteriophage means bacteria eater.Félix d'Herelle is credited with coining the term "bacteriophage" to describe viruses that infect bacteria.

questions.text: In Charles Chamberland’s experiment one of the following represented filterable viruses:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bacteria are not filterable viruses. They are living microorganisms that are much larger compared to viruses. Bacteria can be filtered out using standard filters, and they were not the focus of Charles Chamberland's experiment. Therefore, option a) is incorrect.

Rabies virus is an example of a filterable virus. In Charles Chamberland's experiment, he worked with filterable viruses, also known as "filterable agents" or "ultramicroscopic agents." These agents were smaller than bacteria and could pass through the finest filters available at that time. The discovery of filterable viruses played a significant role in the development of virology as a scientific field. Therefore, option b) is correct.

TMV is another example of a filterable virus. It is a plant virus that causes mosaic disease in tobacco plants. TMV was one of the early viruses studied by scientists, and its filterability was demonstrated in various experiments.

HIV is not a filterable virus. It is a larger-sized enveloped virus that cannot pass through standard filters. HIV requires specialized techniques, such as centrifugation or ultrafiltration, for isolation and purification. Therefore, option d) is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Rabies virus”Explanation: Those days word virus was generally used for any poisonous fluid which caused any disease or death. That is why Chamberland used the term filterable viruses for rabies causing agents as they passed through the filter.

questions.text: The virus that was first ever obtained in purified form was:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV) was the first virus ever to be obtained in a purified form. In 1935, Wendell Meredith Stanley, an American biochemist, successfully isolated and crystallized the TMV. This groundbreaking achievement marked the first time a virus had been purified and its structure studied in detail.
The Pox Virus, including the Variola virus responsible for smallpox, was not the first virus to be obtained in a purified form.
The Rabies Virus was not the first virus to be obtained in a purified form.
The Polio Virus, also known as the Poliovirus, was not the first virus to be obtained in a purified form.

questions.explanation: Answer is “TMV”Explanation: TMV was crystallized by W. M Stanley.

questions.text: The size of the largest virus is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not correct. There are viruses larger than 250 nm in size.
This option is not correct. While there are viruses smaller than 20 nm, the largest virus is larger than this size.
This option is not correct either. The largest virus is larger than 150 nm.
This option is correct. The size of the largest virus is approximately 200 nm.

questions.explanation: Answer is “200nm”Explanation: Pox virus is the largest virus with 200 nm size.

questions.text: The transmittable nature of TMV was first observed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Wendell Meredith Stanley was an American biochemist who, along with his colleagues, crystallized the Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV) for the first time in 1935. He received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1946 for his work on the purification and characterization of viruses.

Dmitri Ivanovsky was a Russian biologist who is considered one of the pioneers of virology. In 1892, he conducted experiments with the Tobacco Mosaic Disease and observed that the disease could be transmitted to healthy plants through a filter that blocked the passage of bacteria. His work laid the foundation for the discovery of viruses, although he did not identify the virus itself.

Charles Chamberland was a French microbiologist who worked with Louis Pasteur and played a significant role in the development of sterilization techniques. However, he did not observe the transmittable nature of TMV.

Frederick Twort was an English bacteriologist who discovered bacteriophages, which are viruses that infect bacteria. He is known for his work on the discovery of these bacteriophages, but he did not observe the transmittable nature of TMV.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Ivanowsky”Explanation: Ivanowsky took bacteria free extract from infected tobacco leaves and sprinkled it over healthy plants which suffered from TMV later on.

questions.text: The protein subunits that make the protein coat of viruses are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not correct. Capsids are the protein coats that surround the genetic material (DNA or RNA) of viruses. They are composed of protein subunits called capsomers or capsid proteins.

This option is correct. Capsomeres are the protein subunits that make up the protein coat (capsid) of viruses. These capsomeres self-assemble to form the protective shell around the viral genetic material.

This option is not correct. Monomers are individual units of a polymer that can be linked together to form larger molecules. In the context of viruses, the protein subunits are called capsomeres, not monomers.

This option is not correct either. Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins, and they are the components that make up the capsomeres. Capsomeres, in turn, come together to form the protein coat (capsid) of a virus.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Capsomeres”Explanation: Capsomeres are those protein subunits whereas capsid is entire protein coat.

questions.text: The capsid of herpes virus and that of adenovirus differ from each other concerning:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The chemical nature of the capsid refers to the composition of the proteins forming the structure. While both herpes virus and adenovirus have proteinaceous capsids, the specific amino acid sequences and arrangements may differ.

The capsid of herpes virus and adenovirus differ concerning the type of subunits. The capsid is the protein coat that encases the viral genetic material. Different viruses have capsids composed of distinct arrangements and types of protein subunits.
The number of subunits in the capsid can vary between different viruses. Herpes virus and adenovirus may have different numbers and arrangements of protein subunits in their respective capsids.

The capsid plays a crucial role in protecting the viral genome and facilitating its entry into host cells. While the general role of the capsid in the life cycle is similar for herpes virus and adenovirus, specific mechanisms and interactions with host cells may differ.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Number of subunits”Explanation: Herpes virus have 162 capsomeres, whereas adenovirus have 252 capsomeres. Net difference is that of 90 capsomeres.

questions.text: How many additional capsomeres are found in adenovirus, as compared to those in herpes virus:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the correct option.
This is not the correct option.
This is the correct option.
This is not the correct option.

questions.explanation: Answer is “90”Explanation: Herpes virus have 162 capsomeres, whereas adenovirus have 252 capsomeres. Net difference is that of 90 capsomeres

questions.text: Which one of the following characters does not belong to fungi?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This character does belong to fungi. Fungi are heterotrophic organisms, which means they obtain their nutrients by absorbing organic material from their environment. They secrete enzymes that break down complex organic matter into smaller molecules, which are then absorbed through their cell walls.

This character does belong to fungi. Fungi are eukaryotic organisms, meaning they have complex cells with a true nucleus enclosed in a membrane. They also possess other membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria, Golgi apparatus, and endoplasmic reticulum.

This character does not belong to fungi. Centrioles are small, cylindrical structures found in animal cells and some protists, but they are not present in fungi. Instead, fungi have a unique microtubule-organizing center called the spindle pole body, which functions similarly during cell division.

This character does belong to fungi. Fungi, like other eukaryotic organisms, undergo nuclear mitosis during cell division. In nuclear mitosis, the nucleus of the cell divides to ensure that each daughter cell receives the correct number of chromosomes.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Centriole present”Explanation: Fungi like plants lack centrioles.

questions.text: The body of a fungus is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A hypha is a thread-like, filamentous structure that makes up the main body of most fungi. Hyphae are thin, long, and often branching, and they collectively form the mycelium. These structures are responsible for nutrient absorption and the growth of the fungus.

The mycelium is the vegetative part of a fungus, and it is made up of a network of hyphae. It is the main body of the fungus, and its primary function is to absorb nutrients from the environment. Mycelium can spread widely through the substrate it inhabits, providing a large surface area for nutrient acquisition.

The fruiting body, also known as the sporocarp or mushroom, is the reproductive structure of the fungus. It is the part of the fungus that produces and releases spores, which are essential for the dispersal and survival of the fungus. Fruiting bodies come in various shapes and sizes depending on the type of fungus.

A coenocyte is a multinucleate cell that lacks septa (cross-walls). While some fungi, like the hyphal form of fungi, have septate hyphae with individual cells separated by septa, coenocytic fungi do not have such divisions. Instead, they have a continuous cytoplasmic mass with multiple nuclei.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Mycelium”Explanation: The body of fungus, called mycelium, consists of long, slender, branched tubular thread like filaments called the hyphae.

questions.text: Non-hyphal unicellular fungi are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Yeast is the correct answer. Non-hyphal unicellular fungi are referred to as yeasts. Yeasts are single-celled fungi that belong to the division Ascomycota or Basidiomycota. They are typically oval or spherical and reproduce asexually by budding or fission.

Mushrooms are not non-hyphal unicellular fungi. Mushrooms are macroscopic fruiting bodies of certain fungi, and they are typically composed of tightly packed hyphae. They are reproductive structures that release spores for dispersal.

Toadstools are also not non-hyphal unicellular fungi. The term "toadstool" is often used informally to describe certain types of mushrooms, especially poisonous ones. Like mushrooms, toadstools are macroscopic fruiting bodies with tightly packed hyphae.

"Fruiting bodies" are not non-hyphal unicellular fungi. This term refers to the reproductive structures of fungi, including both mushrooms and toadstools, that produce and release spores. Fruiting bodies can be macroscopic or microscopic, but they are not unicellular.

questions.explanation: Option a) Yeast: Correct! Non-hyphal unicellular fungi are called yeast. Option b) Mushrooms: Mushrooms are macroscopic fruiting bodies, not non-hyphal unicellular fungi. Option c) Toadstools: Toadstools are also macroscopic fruiting bodies, not non-hyphal unicellular fungi. Option d) Fruiting bodies: "Fruiting bodies" is a general term for reproductive structures of fungi, not non-hyphal unicellular fungi. Answer is “Yeast”Explanation: Yeast are non-hyphal unicellular fungi.

questions.text: The only exception concerning sexual reproduction in fungi is that of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Conjugation fungi, also known as Zygomycota, are a group of fungi that typically reproduce asexually by forming sporangia, which release spores. However, they can also undergo sexual reproduction by a process called "zygotic meiosis." During sexual reproduction, two hyphae of different mating types come together and form a specialized structure called a zygospore. The zygospore contains the genetic material from both mating types and eventually undergoes meiosis to produce new spores.

Imperfect fungi, also known as Deuteromycota, are a diverse group of fungi that are characterized by their lack of a known sexual reproductive stage. Unlike many other fungi, they have not been observed to undergo sexual reproduction or produce sexual spores (ascospores or basidiospores). As a result, their sexual life cycle remains unknown or incomplete.

Sac fungi, also known as Ascomycota, are a large and diverse group of fungi that undergo sexual reproduction by forming sac-like structures called asci. These asci contain ascospores, which are the sexual spores produced after the fusion of two mating types. Sac fungi have a well-defined sexual life cycle, making them distinct from the exception.

Club fungi, also known as Basidiomycota, are a diverse group of fungi that undergo sexual reproduction by forming club-shaped structures called basidia. These basidia produce sexual spores called basidiospores after the fusion of compatible mating types. Club fungi also have a well-defined sexual life cycle, making them distinct from the exception.

questions.explanation: Option a) Conjugation fungi: Not the exception, as they undergo sexual reproduction by forming zygospores. Option b) Imperfect fungi: The exception, as they lack a known sexual reproductive stage. Option c) Sac fungi: Not the exception, as they undergo sexual reproduction by forming asci and ascospores. Option d) Club fungi: Not the exception, as they undergo sexual reproduction by forming basidia and basidiospores. Answer is “Imperfect fungi”Explanation: Perfect stage is sexual stage which is absent in Deuteromycota. That is why it is called imperfect fungi or fungi imperfecti.

questions.text: Conidia are also called as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Conidia are also called "naked spores." Conidia are a type of asexual spore produced by various fungi. They are formed at the tips or sides of specialized hyphae called conidiophores. Unlike some other types of spores, conidia are not enclosed within a protective structure such as a sporangium or ascus.

Zygospores are a different type of spore produced during sexual reproduction in certain fungi, particularly in the group known as Zygomycota. Zygospores are formed by the fusion of two specialized hyphae of different mating types, resulting in a thick-walled, resistant structure.

Zoospores are a type of motile asexual spore produced by some fungi and protists. Unlike conidia, zoospores have flagella that allow them to swim in water, facilitating their dispersal. They are commonly found in water molds and some chytrid fungi.

Sexual spores refer to the spores produced during the sexual reproduction of fungi. These spores are the result of the fusion of specialized cells or hyphae from different mating types. Zygospores, as mentioned earlier, are an example of sexual spores produced during the sexual reproduction of certain fungi.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Naked spores”Explanation: Conidia being produced on conidrophore, without any covering like sporangial wall are called naked spores. These are haploid and asexual spores.

questions.text: Heterokaryotic hyphae/cell contains:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option refers to a condition where a heterokaryotic hypha or cell contains two nuclei, and both nuclei are of the same genetic type. In other words, both nuclei have identical genetic material, and they may have originated from the same parent or through some other mechanism. However, this option is not typical of heterokaryotic hyphae or cells.

This option is correct. In heterokaryotic hyphae or cells, the term "heterokaryotic" itself implies the presence of two or more genetically distinct nuclei. In this case, the hypha or cell contains two nuclei, and these nuclei are of different genetic types. They might have come from different parents or have different genetic compositions.

This option refers to a condition where a hypha or cell contains multiple nuclei, and all the nuclei are of the same genetic type. This scenario is typical of a dikaryotic state in some fungi, where two genetically similar nuclei coexist within the same cell but do not fuse.
This option is not typical of heterokaryotic hyphae or cells. Heterokaryotic structures are characterized by containing genetically different nuclei, not many nuclei of different types.

questions.explanation: Option a) Two nuclei of the same types: Not typical of heterokaryotic hyphae/cells. Option b) Two nuclei of different types: Correct! Heterokaryotic hyphae/cells contain two or more genetically distinct nuclei. Option c) Many nuclei of the same types: Typical of dikaryotic states in some fungi, not heterokaryotic hyphae/cells. Option d) Many nuclei of different types: Not typical of heterokaryotic hyphae/cells. Answer is “Two nuclei of different types”Explanation: Heterokaryotic hyphae/cells also called dikaryotic hyphae/cells contain two nuclei of different genetic types or mating types.

questions.text: The word virus was generally referred to as a poison associated with disease and death at the time of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Louis Pasteur and Charles Chamberland were renowned scientists who made significant contributions to the field of microbiology and immunology. They collaborated on the development of vaccines, including the rabies vaccine, and contributed to the understanding of infectious diseases. However, the term "virus" being referred to as a poison associated with disease and death was not particularly associated with their time.

Ivan Ivanovsky, a Russian botanist, is credited with the discovery of viruses in the late 19th century. He researched the tobacco mosaic disease and observed that the sap of infected plants remained infectious even after passing through a filter that retained all known bacteria. He hypothesized the presence of an unknown agent smaller than bacteria, which he referred to as a "contagion vivum fluidum" or "filterable living germ." His work laid the foundation for the later discovery and understanding of viruses.

Wendell M. Stanley, an American biochemist, played a crucial role in the field of virology. In 1935, he and his colleagues successfully crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV), providing the first evidence that viruses were distinct biological entities with a defined chemical composition. This work helped establish that viruses were not poisons but rather entities with structural and chemical properties.

Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch were pioneers in the field of microbiology and played significant roles in establishing the germ theory of disease. They focused on bacteria and their roles in causing infectious diseases. Although they made groundbreaking discoveries in microbiology, the specific term "virus" as we know it today was not generally associated with them.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch”Explanation: as microscopic techniques were not well developed at the time of Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch, the study about virus was very limited and the only thing that is known about it was that it is a poison associated with disease and death.

questions.text: A particle of nucleic acid wrapped in a protein coat is recognized as a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Cells can be single-celled, like bacteria, or multicellular, like in plants and animals. They are enclosed by a membrane and contain genetic material (DNA) that governs their activities and reproduction. Cells are the building blocks of life and are responsible for carrying out various processes essential for an organism's survival.

A bacterium (plural: bacteria) is a type of single-celled microorganism that belongs to the domain of prokaryotes. They are characterized by having no membrane-bound nucleus or other membrane-bound organelles. Bacteria have a simple structure and are typically much smaller than eukaryotic cells. They can be found in various environments and play important roles in nature, some being beneficial, while others can cause diseases.

A virus is a small infectious agent that cannot replicate on its own and requires a host cell to reproduce. It consists of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat called a capsid. Viruses can infect various organisms, including animals, plants, fungi, and bacteria. They are responsible for a wide range of diseases, such as the common cold, influenza, and COVID-19.

Prions are unique infectious agents composed solely of protein. Unlike viruses, prions do not contain any nucleic acid (DNA or RNA). They can cause certain neurodegenerative diseases in animals and humans by converting normal, healthy proteins into abnormal forms. The misfolded prion proteins can then aggregate and cause damage to the nervous system, leading to conditions like Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease in humans or Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy (BSE) in cattle.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Virus”Explanation: Viruses are actually particles of nucleoproteins i.e. a nucleic acid core (DNA or RNA) have been coated in a protein coat called capsid.

questions.text: The study of nucleocapsids is carried out in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Microbiology is the broader field that encompasses the study of various microorganisms, including bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and viruses. While virology is a specialized branch of microbiology that specifically focuses on viruses, the study of nucleocapsids falls under the realm of virology.

Mycology is the branch of biology that deals with the study of fungi. Nucleocapsids are not typically associated with fungi; instead, they are a characteristic feature of viruses. Therefore, the study of nucleocapsids is not carried out in mycology.

The study of nucleocapsids is primarily carried out in virology. Nucleocapsids are structures formed by the association of viral genetic material (either DNA or RNA) with the protein coat (capsid). Virology is the branch of science dedicated to the study of viruses, including their structure, classification, replication, and interaction with host cells.

While cell biology encompasses the study of cellular structures and functions, the specific study of nucleocapsids, which are viral structures, is more closely aligned with virology. Cell biology may be involved in understanding the interactions between nucleocapsids and host cell components during the viral life cycle.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Virology”Explanation: That branch of biology which deals with the study of viruses is called virology and as structurally viruses are nucleocapsids so it may be study of nucleocapsids as well.

questions.text: For the synthesis of their proteins, a virus uses:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses lack the cellular structures and metabolic machinery required for independent metabolism. They are obligate intracellular parasites and must utilize the host cell's metabolism for their replication.

While viruses affect the metabolism of the entire host organism during infection, the actual synthesis of viral proteins occurs within the infected host cells. Viruses manipulate the cellular metabolism of the host cells for their own replication.

Viruses do not have their own cellular machinery for protein synthesis. Instead, they rely on the metabolic processes of the host cell they infect to replicate and produce viral proteins. Once inside a host cell, viruses hijack the cellular machinery, including ribosomes and enzymes, to carry out the synthesis of viral proteins and replicate their genetic material.
This option is not accurate, as viruses indeed rely on the metabolism of the host cell for protein synthesis and other essential processes during their replication cycle.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Metabolism of its host cell”Explanation: Viruses are acellular entities having no cellular/metabolic machinery. Thus they have to rely upon the biosynthetic machinery of host for this purpose.

questions.text: Which one of the following organisms can pass through the porcelain filter?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are the only organisms among the options that can pass through a porcelain filter. Porcelain filters have relatively small pore sizes, allowing the passage of particles on the order of viruses. Bacteria, fungi, and protozoans are larger and typically cannot pass through such fine filters.
Bacteria are larger than viruses and would be retained by the pores of a porcelain filter.
Fungi, including individual fungal cells or spores, are generally larger than viruses and would be retained by a porcelain filter.
Protozoans, being larger unicellular organisms, would also be unable to pass through the fine pores of a porcelain filter.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Virus”Explanation: Viruses having smaller size as compared to bacteria can pass through the porcelain filter and it was first proved by Charles Chamberland by filtering rabies virus through porcelain filter.

questions.text: Who determined that, viruses are smaller than bacteria?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dmitry Ivanovsky, a Russian scientist, was one of the pioneers in the discovery of viruses. In 1892, he conducted experiments on tobacco plants and observed that a disease affecting them could be transmitted through a filter that blocked the passage of bacteria. He hypothesized the presence of a "filterable agent," which we now know as viruses.

Edward Jenner was an English physician and is renowned for developing the smallpox vaccine. While his work significantly contributed to immunology, he is not credited with determining the size relationship between viruses and bacteria.

Charles Chamberland, a French microbiologist, collaborated with Louis Pasteur and made contributions to the field of microbiology, including the development of the Chamberland filter, which was crucial in the study of viruses. However, he did not specifically determine the size difference between viruses and bacteria.

Wendell Meredith Stanley was an American biochemist who, in 1935, crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) and demonstrated that it was composed of a nucleoprotein. This work contributed to the understanding of the nature of viruses and their smaller size compared to bacteria. Stanley was awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1946 for his research on the structure of viruses.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Charles Chamberland”Explanation: Charles Chamberlandt called the filtrate as filterable viruses and residue as non-filterable viruses.

questions.text: Taxonomically fungi were a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fungi were traditionally classified as a separate kingdom in biological taxonomy. This kingdom, called the Fungi Kingdom, includes diverse organisms such as molds, yeasts, mushrooms, and other fungi. Fungi differ significantly from plants, animals, and protists in terms of their cellular structure, reproduction, and nutritional strategies.

Fungi are distinct from plants and were recognized as a separate kingdom due to differences in their cell walls (composed of chitin instead of cellulose), nutritional modes (heterotrophic and absorptive), and other key characteristics.

Fungi are also distinct from animals. While both fungi and animals are heterotrophic, fungi absorb nutrients externally through the release of enzymes, whereas animals ingest and internally digest their food.

Fungi are not considered part of the protist kingdom. Protists are a diverse group of eukaryotic microorganisms that include various single-celled organisms, algae, and protozoans. Fungi were classified as a separate kingdom due to their unique characteristics and evolutionary divergence from other eukaryotic groups.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Group of plant kingdom”Explanation: Fungi was included in Kingdom plantae due to the presence of cell wall and absence of centrioles. However, later on due to its heterotrophic mode of nutrition it was excluded from plants and given the status of an independent kingdom.

questions.text: Unlike plants and like animals, fungi lack:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not accurate. Fungi do have cell walls, but their composition differs from that of plants. Fungal cell walls contain chitin, providing structural support to the cells.

Unlike plants and similar to animals, fungi lack cellulose in their cell walls. Plant cell walls are primarily composed of cellulose, while fungal cell walls are made up of chitin, a different structural polysaccharide. Animals, including fungi, do not have cell walls made of cellulose.

Fungi, like animals, have cell walls containing chitin. Chitin is a complex polysaccharide that provides strength and rigidity to the fungal cell wall. This distinguishes fungi from plants, which have cell walls composed of cellulose.

Fungi, like plants and animals, have definite nuclei. They are eukaryotic organisms, meaning their cells contain membrane-bound nuclei that house the genetic material (DNA). The presence of definite nuclei is a shared characteristic among eukaryotes.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Cellulose”Explanation: Cellulose is found in the cell wall of plants and algae, fungi have chitinous cell wall. Animals cell lack cellulose as a structure component

questions.text: Fungi resemble arthropods unlike the rest of the animals concerning:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fungi do not resemble arthropods concerning the presence of an exoskeleton. Arthropods, such as insects and crustaceans, have an external skeleton (exoskeleton) made of chitin. Fungi, on the other hand, have cell walls made of chitin, but it is a different structural component and not an exoskeleton.

Both fungi and arthropods are heterotrophic, meaning they obtain nutrients by consuming organic matter. However, many other groups of animals also share this characteristic, so it does not specifically differentiate fungi from the rest of the animals.

Fungi, like arthropods and other animals, lack chloroplasts. Chloroplasts are the organelles responsible for photosynthesis, a process found in plants and some protists. Heterotrophic organisms, including fungi and animals, do not perform photosynthesis.

options.text: The chemical found in external covering of the body
options.explanation: Arthropods have an exoskeleton made of chitin, whereas fungi have chitin in their cell walls. While both contain chitin, the specific structures (exoskeleton in arthropods and cell walls in fungi) are distinct. The chemical composition of the external covering differs between these groups.

questions.explanation: Answer is “The chemical found in external covering of the body”Explanation: Fungi have chitinous cell wall as external covering and insects have chitinous exoskeleton as external covering.

questions.text: It has been estimated by mycologists that fungi and animals probably arose from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not supported by the current understanding of fungal and animal evolution. Molecular and genetic evidence suggests a shared ancestry between fungi and animals.

It has been suggested by mycologists and supported by molecular and genetic evidence that fungi and animals likely share a common ancestor. Both fungi and animals are classified as eukaryotes and exhibit similarities in their genetic makeup, particularly in genes related to key cellular processes.

Both fungi and animals are heterotrophic organisms, meaning they obtain nutrients by consuming organic matter. However, the term "ingestive heterotrophs" does not specifically address the evolutionary relationship between fungi and animals.

Fungi are characterized as absorptive heterotrophs because they obtain nutrients by secreting enzymes into their surroundings and then absorbing the resulting small organic molecules. This term emphasizes the mode of nutrient acquisition in fungi but does not address the evolutionary relationship with animals.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Common ancestors”Explanation: As both animals and fungi are heterotrophs and both have chitin as a structural component.

questions.text: Mention some unique characteristic of the fungi:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fungi exhibit absorptive heterotrophy, meaning they secrete enzymes into their surroundings to break down complex organic compounds, and then absorb the resulting smaller molecules. This is in contrast to plants, which perform photosynthesis, and animals, which ingest food.

Chitin is a unique structural polysaccharide found in the cell walls of fungi. It provides strength and rigidity to the fungal cell wall, distinguishing it from the cellulose present in plant cell walls.

Fungi display various reproductive strategies, including both sexual and asexual reproduction. Asexual reproduction often involves the formation of spores, while sexual reproduction may involve the fusion of specialized reproductive structures.

Fungi are known for having a unique type of mitosis called closed or intranuclear mitosis. Unlike animals and plants, fungi undergo mitosis without disassembling the nuclear envelope. The spindle apparatus forms inside the intact nucleus during nuclear division, which is a distinctive feature of fungal mitosis.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Nuclear mitosis”Explanation: Nuclear mitosis is a unique feature of fungi as no other group of organisms carries out nuclear mitosis.

questions.text: The body of fungus consists of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mycelium is the vegetative, thread-like structure of a fungus, consisting of a network of hyphae. It is the main body of the fungus responsible for nutrient absorption and growth.
Hyphae are the individual filaments that make up the mycelium. They are elongated, thread-like structures that form the structural and functional components of the fungal body.

The fruiting body is the reproductive structure of some fungi. It is responsible for producing and releasing spores, allowing the fungus to disperse and reproduce. Examples of fruiting bodies include mushrooms and puffballs.

Rhizoids are root-like structures found in some fungi, particularly in the Zygomycota group. They anchor the fungus to the substrate and assist in nutrient absorption. Rhizoids are not as complex as true roots found in plants but serve a similar function in anchoring the fungus.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Hyphae”Explanation: Hyphae are the structural units of fungi except yeast.

questions.text: It is more resistant to decay than cellulose and lignin:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glycogen is a polysaccharide that serves as a storage form of energy in animals and some fungi. While it is important for energy storage, it is not known for its resistance to decay.
Amylose is a component of starch, a polysaccharide found in plants. It is a form of energy storage in plants but is not particularly resistant to decay.

Chitin is a polysaccharide found in the cell walls of fungi and the exoskeletons of arthropods. It is more resistant to decay than cellulose and lignin, making it a durable structural component in fungal cell walls and arthropod exoskeletons.
Amylopectin is another component of starch, serving as an energy storage polysaccharide in plants. Like amylose, it is not known for being more resistant to decay than cellulose and lignin.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Chitin”Explanation: Chitin in more resistant to decay than cellulose and lignin.

questions.text: The resting spore of fungi is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ascospores are a type of fungal spore produced within sac-like structures called asci. They are characteristic of fungi in the Ascomycota phylum.
Basidiospores are produced externally on specialized structures called basidia. They are characteristic of fungi in the Basidiomycota phylum, including mushrooms.
Conidia are asexual spores produced at the tips or along the sides of specialized hyphae called conidiophores. They are common in various fungal groups, including Ascomycota and some other phyla.

The resting spore of fungi is called a zygospore. It is formed through the sexual reproduction process in fungi belonging to the Zygomycota phylum. Zygospores result from the fusion of specialized sexual structures called zygophores, and they can withstand adverse environmental conditions until more favorable conditions for germination arise.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Zygospore”Explanation: Zygospore formed in zygomycota is a resistant body which can withstand the unfavorable conditions and upon arrival of favorable conditions starts germination.

questions.text: Non-motile asexual spores produced uncovered on hyphal tips are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Teliospores are thick-walled resting spores produced in the life cycle of certain fungi, particularly those belonging to the rust and smut fungi (Basidiomycota). Teliospores are involved in the sexual reproduction of these fungi.
Basidiospores are sexually produced spores formed externally on specialized structures called basidia. They are characteristic of fungi in the Basidiomycota phylum, including mushrooms.

Zygospores are thick-walled resting spores produced through sexual reproduction in fungi belonging to the Zygomycota phylum. They result from the fusion of specialized sexual structures called zygophores.

Non-motile asexual spores produced uncovered on hyphal tips are called conidia. They are typically formed at the tips or along the sides of specialized hyphae called conidiophores. Conidia are a common form of asexual reproduction in various fungal groups.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Conidia”Explanation: Conidia are also called naked spores as they lack any sporangial cover.

questions.text: The appearance of mold on Jams and jellies indicates the fungal resistance against:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While extreme pH levels can affect the growth of many microorganisms, molds on jams and jellies are more directly related to their ability to withstand osmotic pressure from high sugar concentrations rather than extreme pH.

The appearance of mold on jams and jellies indicates the fungal resistance against immense osmotic pressure. Jams and jellies typically have a high sugar content, creating an environment with low water activity. Fungi that can resist this osmotic stress can thrive in such conditions.

Mold growth on jams and jellies is not primarily about decomposition. Instead, it involves the ability of certain fungi to utilize the available nutrients in the preserved food without being inhibited by the high sugar content.

Desiccation refers to the process of drying, and it is not the primary factor in the resistance of molds on jams and jellies. The high sugar content in these products creates a low water activity environment, and fungi that can resist desiccation under these conditions can thrive.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Immense osmotic pressure”Explanation: Jams and jellies are hyperosmotic media with saturation level of sugar and to grow in such medium requires immense osmotic resistance.

questions.text: The fruiting body of a mushroom is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ascocarp is the term used for the fruiting body of fungi belonging to the Ascomycota phylum. These fungi include morel mushrooms, cup fungi, and other species. Ascocarps produce and release ascospores during the reproductive phase.

The fruiting body of a mushroom is called the basidiocarp. It is the reproductive structure of fungi belonging to the Basidiomycota phylum, which includes mushrooms. The basidiocarp is responsible for producing and releasing basidiospores, enabling the fungus to disperse and reproduce.

The mycelium is the vegetative and thread-like structure of a fungus, consisting of a network of hyphae. While essential for nutrient absorption and growth, it is not the specific structure associated with the reproductive phase like a fruiting body.

The term "thalamus" is not commonly used in the context of fungal biology. It is often associated with the receptacle or base of a flower in botanical terms and is not relevant to the structure of a mushroom.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Basidiocarp” Explanation: As mushroom (Agaricus) belongs to basidiomycota its fruiting body is called basidiocarp which bear basidia and basidiospores.

questions.text: The formation of a resistant structure in Rhizopus is a consequence of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmogamy is the fusion of the cytoplasm of two fungal cells, resulting in the formation of a heterokaryotic cell. In Rhizopus, plasmogamy occurs when two different mating strains come into contact, leading to the formation of a zygosporangium.
Karyogamy is the fusion of two nuclei, forming a diploid nucleus. In the life cycle of Rhizopus, karyogamy follows plasmogamy within the zygosporangium, resulting in the diploid zygote.

Heterokaryotization refers to the state where a fungal cell contains two or more genetically distinct nuclei. This state is common in the life cycle of many fungi, including Rhizopus, but it is not directly associated with the formation of the resistant structure.

Dikaryotization is the state in which a fungal cell contains two genetically distinct nuclei without undergoing fusion to form a diploid nucleus. In Rhizopus, dikaryotization occurs during the development of the zygosporangium after plasmogamy, and it precedes karyogamy in the life cycle.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Karygammy”Explanation: After karyogammy or diploidization, the diploid nucleus produces a resistant cyst around it and becomes resistant zygospore.

questions.text: Two individual organisms belong to the same species if they can:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Two individual organisms belong to the same species if they can interbreed. In biological terms, a species is often defined as individuals capable of mating and producing viable, fertile offspring.

While reproduction is a fundamental characteristic of a species, the ability to reproduce alone may not be sufficient to define a species. The key aspect is whether individuals can reproduce with each other to produce viable offspring.

Producing sterile offspring is generally not a criterion for defining a species. The ability to produce fertile offspring capable of further reproduction is a more common criterion for species designation.

Two individual organisms belong to the same species if they can produce fertile offspring. The ability of organisms to mate and produce viable, fertile offspring is a central concept in defining and identifying species in the biological classification system.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Species”Explanation: A species is a group of natural population which can interbreed freely among themselves and produce fertile offspring, but are reproductively isolated from all other such groups in nature. So mere interbreeding and reproduction is not enough to be a species as occurs in horse and donkey, both belong to differents species but they can interbreed and produce mule.

questions.text: The following names are used for onions in different regions of Pakistan, EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: "Bassal" is a term used for onions in some regions of Pakistan.
The name "Argavad" is not commonly used for onions in different regions of Pakistan.
"Ganda" is a term used for onions in some regions of Pakistan.
"Piyaz" is a term used for onions in some regions of Pakistan.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Argavad”Explanation:

questions.text: Pick up the odd one:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Golden shower refers to a flowering plant, Cassia fistula, known for its clusters of yellow flowers. It is not a vegetable like brinjal.
Brinjal is the odd one on this list. It is a vegetable, specifically a type of eggplant, commonly used in culinary contexts.
Purging cassia is another term for Cassia fistula, the same flowering plant mentioned in option (a). It is not a vegetable.
Gurmala is a term used for a variety of beans in some regions. Like brinjal, it is a vegetable used in cooking.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Brinjal”Explanation: See explanation of Q # 121.

questions.text: Choose the wrong statement regarding AIDS:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is correct. AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is characterized by a progressive weakening of the immune system, leading to increased susceptibility to opportunistic infections and certain cancers.

options.text: It is caused by the retrovirus HIV
options.explanation: This statement is correct. HIV is a retrovirus responsible for causing AIDS. It attacks and infects immune cells, particularly CD4+ T-lymphocytes, leading to immunodeficiency.

options.text: HIV selectively infects and kills B-lymphocytes
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) primarily targets and infects CD4+ T-lymphocytes, not B-lymphocytes. CD4+ T-lymphocytes play a crucial role in coordinating the immune response, and their depletion contributes to the immunodeficiency seen in AIDS.

options.text: Retroviruses have RNA genomes that replicate DNA intermediate
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Retroviruses, including HIV, have RNA genomes. During replication, they use the enzyme reverse transcriptase to convert their RNA genome into DNA, which then integrates into the host cell's genome.

questions.text: Which of the following is present in all viruses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While many viruses contain DNA as their genetic material, some viruses have RNA instead. Therefore, the presence of DNA is not universal among all viruses.

All viruses contain proteins. Proteins are a fundamental component of viral structure. Viruses consist of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat known as a capsid. Some viruses may also have an outer lipid envelope derived from the host cell membrane, but the presence of proteins in the form of capsid or envelope proteins is universal across all viruses.
Some viruses have RNA as their genetic material, and not all viruses contain RNA. The genetic material (either DNA or RNA) is encapsulated by a protein coat (capsid) in all viruses.
Not all viruses have an envelope. Some viruses, known as naked viruses, lack an outer envelope. The presence or absence of an envelope depends on the specific characteristics of each virus.

questions.text: The shape of TMV is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: TMV (Tobacco Mosaic Virus) does not have a spherical shape. It is a rod-shaped virus.
The shape of TMV is rod-shaped. TMV is a helical virus with a filamentous structure.
Tadpole is not a relevant description of the shape of TMV. TMV is best described as rod-shaped.
Pyramidal is not a relevant description for the shape of TMV. TMV is characterized by its rod-like or filamentous structure.

questions.text: Receptors for attachment of HIV are present on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term "cell wall" is more commonly associated with prokaryotic cells like bacteria and does not apply to eukaryotic cells, such as human cells. The receptors for HIV attachment are found on the cell membrane.

Receptors for the attachment of HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) are present on the cell membrane. HIV primarily targets and infects CD4+ T-lymphocytes, which are immune cells expressing the CD4 receptor on their cell membranes. The interaction between the viral envelope glycoprotein (gp120) and the CD4 receptor on the host cell membrane is a key step in the attachment and entry of HIV into the host cell.

Tail fibers are structures found in bacteriophages, which are viruses that infect bacteria. HIV is a virus that infects human cells, and tail fibers are not relevant to its attachment and entry into host cells.
This option is incorrect. Receptors for attachment of HIV are present on the cell membrane, specifically on the surface of certain immune cells like CD4+ T-lymphocytes.

questions.text: Which event of the life cycle of HIV occurs in the nucleus
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Uncoating is the process where the viral capsid is disassembled, releasing the viral genome into the host cell cytoplasm. It does not occur in the nucleus.

The event of the HIV life cycle that occurs in the nucleus is Reverse Transcription. During this process, the viral RNA genome is reverse-transcribed into DNA by the enzyme reverse transcriptase. The resulting viral DNA is then transported into the host cell nucleus, where it integrates into the host cell's DNA to form a provirus.

The provirus is the integrated viral DNA in the host cell genome, and its formation involves the reverse transcription process in the cytoplasm and integration into the nucleus. Provirus formation is not exclusive to the nucleus.

The assembly of new viral particles occurs in the cytoplasm, not in the nucleus. During assembly, newly synthesized viral components come together to form mature virions that are then released from the cell.

questions.text: Viroid is infectious_.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins alone cannot constitute a viroid. Viroids are unique in being composed solely of RNA.
Viroids do not contain DNA. Their genetic material is RNA.
Viroids are not composed of nucleoproteins. The term "nucleoprotein" typically refers to complexes of nucleic acids (such as DNA or RNA) and proteins, but viroids lack a protein coat.
Viroids are infectious agents composed of RNA. They are small, circular RNA molecules that lack a protein coat. Viroids cause diseases in certain plants by interfering with normal cellular processes.
